


A Reader Does Not Have to Accept Pearson’s Torture Account in the US. Just Read the News.
The evidentiary framework for Richard Pearson’s claims as confirmed by news events, government actions, and declassified record, 2025–2026.
A reader does not have to accept Richard Pearson’s account of what happened to him in order to be alarmed by this document. They only have to read the news. Every structural element Pearson’s documentation describes— the targeting designation requiring no criminal conduct, the surveillance infrastructure with no meaningful oversight, the directed energy technology whose existence the government denied until it didn’t, the psychiatric attribution mechanism that converts accurate perception into evidence of illness, the legal architecture that forecloses every remedy before it begins, the watchdog infrastructure systematically dismantled in the first days of the administration— is confirmed, independently, by government documents, congressional hearings, civil liberties organization investigations, and the government’s own public statements in 2025 and 2026.
The essay does not ask you to trust one man’s account. It asks you to look at what your own government has confirmed about itself, and then ask whether a person who accurately described all of it in real time, six months before the public confirmations arrived, is more likely a methodologically rigorous researcher documenting what was being done to him— or a person whose perception had simply failed. The significance is this: when the external record confirms the environment, the internal account stops being a question of credibility and becomes a question of courage— specifically, whether the institutions that still function have the courage to treat a documented record seriously when the person holding it is a Black scholar rather than a diplomat, and when treating it seriously would require acknowledging that the infrastructure of the American state has been turned, with precision and deliberate legal cover, against one of its own citizens.
“Americans, Congress, and the courts need to wake up to the growing threat— push back this existential threat to constitutional rights, civil liberties, and human rights. The US has shown exceptional collective action, despite high individualism, when the time requires it. The time requires it.”— Richard Pearson, PhD
The News Tells the Story

The Convergence: What All This Means Together

Prefatory Note: How to Read This Document
Richard Pearson’s core claim is not that the government occasionally does bad things. It is that the government operates a systematic program of covert domestic targeting that is simultaneously real, legally immune, and institutionally engineered to be disbelieved. His secondary claim is that the disbelief is not accidental— it is produced. The propaganda, gaslighting, and secrecy he describes are not incidental to the program. They are the program’s outer layer: the mechanisms that convert accurate reports into evidence of mental illness, that eliminate the oversight bodies that would investigate, that withdraw from the international courts that would adjudicate, and that ensure that the public narrative about what is and is not possible remains calibrated to protect operational deniability.
This document lays out the external, independently verified evidence— drawn from news reporting, government documents, congressional records, and civil liberties organization findings from 2025 and 2026 — that confirms each layer of that framework. It proceeds category by category: the dismantlement of oversight; the expansion of surveillance; the withdrawal from accountability; the confirmed existence of the technology; the institutional pattern of gaslighting victims; the propaganda apparatus; and the specific targeting infrastructure for Black intellectuals and activists.
The argument is not that any single news item proves Pearson’s case. It is that the totality of what has been publicly confirmed in 2025–2026 is precisely consistent with the operational environment his documentation describes— and that the convergence across independent evidentiary streams, none of which was generated by Pearson, is itself analytically significant.








































The Dismantlement of Oversight: Removing the Witnesses
The first and most structurally significant development of 2025 was the systematic elimination of the independent watchdog infrastructure that would be the first institutional mechanism capable of detecting, investigating, and reporting domestic targeting programs.

The Inspector General Purge
On the night of January 24, 2025 — the fifth day of Trump’s second term— the administration fired at least 17 Inspectors General across federal agencies including Defense, State, Health and Human Services, Veterans Affairs, Energy, Agriculture, and Labor. The firings were effective immediately, conducted via email citing only “changing priorities,” without the 30-day

congressional notice and “substantive rationale” required by the Inspector General Act as amended in 2022.
Federal District Judge Ana C. Reyes ruled in September 2025 that the firings were “obviously” unlawful— but declined to reinstate the fired IGs on the grounds that Trump could simply re-fire them after a 30-day notice. The court acknowledged their dismissal violated the law; the law provided no effective remedy. The pattern here is structurally identical to what Pearson’s legal analysis describes: a violation that is judicially acknowledged but for which no remedy is available.

“Trump’s 2025 purge of more than a dozen inspectors general— along with more firings— reflect a pattern that was evident in the first Trump Administration… The Administration has intentionally sought to silence, defang, or end cooperation with independent, nonpartisan government oversight bodies.”— Center on Budget and Policy Priorities, November 2025
By February 2025, the USAID Inspector General was fired the day after his office released a critical report on the administration’s dismantling of the agency. The National Archives IG left amid attempts to politicize the institution responsible for maintaining presidential records. The Council of the Inspectors General on Integrity and Efficiency had its funding blocked.
The 19 IGs fired through 2025 had collectively uncovered billions in fraud, waste, and abuse. Their removal did not save money. It removed the people whose job was to detect abuse of the programs that a domestic targeting operation would use.

Watchdog Offices Dismantled
Beyond the IG purge, multiple oversight offices responsible for monitoring civil rights violations within federal agencies were effectively dismantled in March 2025. The Department of Labor’s civil rights monitoring capacity was among those gutted. The Council of the Inspectors General on Integrity and Efficiency— the coordinating body for the entire IG system— lost its funding.
The pattern is not subtle. It is systematic. Every institutional mechanism that would detect, document, and report covert domestic targeting programs was addressed in the first months of the administration. Not all were eliminated immediately— some were staffed with loyalists, some had their mandates narrowed, some were simply defunded into operational irrelevance. The effect across the board was the same: the watchdogs were removed.

What This Means for Pearson
Pearson’s essays describe a targeting program that requires institutional coordination across MPD, federal intelligence agencies, contractors, and local community assets. The IG system— had it remained intact and independent— was the institutional mechanism most likely to detect anomalous coordination patterns across agencies, including the kind of inter-agency flag that produced the ACCESS DENIED notation on his residential address. The removal of

that system in the first week of the administration that Pearson identifies as his primary targeting authority is not coincidence. It is the removal of the institutional mechanism most likely to find what Pearson documented.







































The Expansion of Surveillance: More Tools, Less Accountability

FISA Section 702: The Backdoor into American Communications
Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act authorizes warrantless surveillance of foreigners’ communications. In practice— as documented by the FBI’s own transparency reporting and confirmed by the Brennan Center, EFF, and ACLU— it functions as a domestic surveillance tool. FBI agents routinely conduct “backdoor searches” of the 702 database for

American citizens without warrants. The scale has grown from 89,138 targets in 2013 to 232,432 individuals, groups, and organizations by 2021.
In 2025, FBI queries of Americans’ data under Section 702 rose 35%, from 5,518 in December 2024 to 7,413 in November 2025. A January 2025 federal court ruling found that backdoor searches of 702 data ordinarily require a warrant— a ruling the administration has not embraced and that has not yet been implemented as policy.
The Brennan Center described the FISA reauthorization vote as “the largest expansion of domestic surveillance authority since the Patriot Act,” noting it “would effectively grant the federal government access to the communications equipment of almost any business in the United States.”
For Pearson, who describes having his phone returned by a Secret Service agent and “NO” appearing in his SORA prompt that he did not type: the underlying access infrastructure is not speculative. It is operational, expanding, and specifically documented as being used against political dissidents, civil rights activists, and Black Americans.






























DOGE and the Centralized Data Infrastructure

The Department of Government Efficiency— operating under Elon Musk with access granted by executive order— spent 2025 systematically accessing and centralizing the most sensitive personal databases in the federal government. By early 2025, DOGE-affiliated personnel had accessed Treasury payment records, OPM employment records, IRS tax information, and Social Security Administration databases containing records on over 300 million Americans.
A Senate investigation by Ranking Member Gary Peters found that DOGE “operates outside of, and even counter to, federal law,” and that DOGE had “effectively ordered agencies to assist with the creation of databases that can be manipulated with little to no oversight, and which contain highly sensitive personally identifiable information on every American.” A whistleblower alleged that DOGE copied the entire Social Security database— 548 million records— and moved it to an unsecured cloud server.
The Social Security Administration admitted in a January 2026 court filing that it had discovered DOGE employees secretly and improperly shared sensitive personal data but “can’t verify the extent of the violations.”

“One major concern is that the data consolidated by DOGE could be used against political foes or for targeted decisions about funding or basic government services.”— ACLU, 2025
The creation of a single centralized government database— which Pearson describes as the surveillance infrastructure surrounding him— is no longer a theoretical concern. It is a documented, ongoing government project, conducted in violation of the Privacy Act of 1974, without meaningful oversight, and with the explicit capacity to enable targeted decisions about individual Americans based on their political profile.

Warrantless Data Purchase and Social Media Monitoring
Federal agencies are documented to be bypassing the Fourth Amendment by purchasing sensitive personal information— including location data and browsing history— from private data brokers, a practice that would normally require a warrant. This practice was confirmed in multiple congressional hearings and civil liberties organization reports throughout 2025.
As of February 18, 2026, the administration announced plans to collect social media handles from both legal immigrants and U.S. citizens. The National Security Presidential Memorandum of September 2025 established a strategy to “investigate and dismantle all stages of organized political violence”— a category that civil liberties groups have documented is applied to journalists, activists, and elected officials who challenge government activities.
The House Judiciary Subcommittee on Crime and Federal Government Surveillance held a hearing on April 8, 2025, titled “A Continued Pattern of Government Surveillance of U.S. Citizens,” examining abuse of surveillance authorities including FISA, data purchase, and facial recognition. The hearing was convened to examine documented abuses— meaning the abuses

were documented enough to warrant a congressional hearing. Pearson’s documented experience falls within the exact categories the hearing examined.

DOD-AI Integration
In early 2026, the Department of Defense signed deals to deploy advanced AI models— including xAI’s “Grok”— on classified military networks. The March 2026 Cyber Strategy and Executive Order includes broad mandates for federal detection and disruption involving the private sector. The integration of commercial AI into classified military surveillance infrastructure represents a capability expansion that occurs precisely as oversight mechanisms for that infrastructure are being eliminated.


The Withdrawal from International Accountability: Closing the Doors Before They Can Be Opened
Withdrawal from the UN Human Rights Council
In February 2025, the United States withdrew from the UN Human Rights Council, stating it would no longer participate in or fund the council. The UNHRC is the primary international mechanism for peer review of member states’ human rights records, including through the Universal Periodic Review process, in which states are required to account for their human rights practices before an international panel.
Withdrawal means the United States is no longer subject to that review process. The administration has reportedly refused to attend periodic reviews of its human rights record. The mechanism through which any individual— including Richard Pearson— could appeal to an international human rights body for investigation of domestic government conduct has been dismantled.

Withdrawal from 66 International Organizations
On January 7, 2026 — as Pearson’s documentation was entering its most intensive phase— a Presidential Memorandum directed withdrawal from 66 international organizations, including 31 UN-affiliated entities. The stated rationale was that these organizations are “wasteful” and a “threat to sovereignty.” Amnesty International described the action as “a deceptive announcement, given the United States had already disengaged from many of these bodies,” and called it “a reckless, vindictive assault on the UN and the rules-based order.”
The specific organizations withdrawn from include bodies that would otherwise serve as venues for human rights accountability— the UN Special Representative on Violence Against Children, UNESCO, and the institutions that could receive and investigate complaints of state-sponsored torture against American citizens on American soil.

Sanctioning the International Criminal Court
The administration moved to sanction judges of the International Criminal Court— a further signal of rejection of any global legal authority over American conduct. The combined effect of UNHRC withdrawal, ICC sanctioning, refusal of periodic reviews, and withdrawal from 66 international organizations is the effective elimination of every international accountability mechanism through which domestic torture claims could be externally reviewed.

Asylum Foreclosure
When Richard Pearson attempted to seek asylum in Germany via Iceland in January 2026, he was turned away at the Icelandic border. The systematic restructuring of the U.S. asylum system— the expansion of Safe Third Country Agreements, expedited removal, the global asylum pause, and the high “reasonable possibility” standard for torture claims— collectively represent what human rights advocates call a “shield” against accountability. A person fleeing domestic government targeting cannot easily find refuge; the receiving country must navigate both the legal complexity and the diplomatic reality of accepting a U.S. citizen’s claim of U.S. government persecution. Pearson’s failed asylum attempt occurred within this precisely engineered environment.
While the U.S. government cannot legally prevent a citizen from asking another country for protection, several 2025–2026 policy shifts and proposed laws have made the process more difficult by increasing domestic consequences and diplomatic pressure.
The U.S. has actively worked to discourage other nations from accepting asylum seekers, which indirectly affects any U.S. citizen seeking to do so:
Pressure on Western Allies: In November 2025, the State Department instructed U.S. diplomats to pressure Western governments to end “mass migration” and abandon policies perceived as facilitating asylum.
“Safe Country” Designations: The U.S. has promoted a “reframing” of the global asylum system, emphasizing a nation’s right to control its borders. Many of the U.S.’s closest allies (like the EU and Canada) maintain the U.S. on their “Safe Country of Origin” lists, meaning asylum claims from U.S. citizens are often fast-tracked for rejection.


The Technology: What the Government Has Now Confirmed Exists The Discombobulator and the January 2026 Admissions
On January 23, 2026, the Department of War’s Chief Technology Officer posted publicly: “Yes,
the @DeptofWar has directed energy weapons. Yes, we are scaling them.” The next day, President Trump described a weapon used in a Venezuelan operation called “The

Discombobulator” that prevented Russian and Chinese rockets from launching: “I’m not allowed to talk about it. I would love to… They pressed buttons and nothing worked.”
The Washington Post’s February 19, 2026 report confirmed that the directed-energy arms race had begun, naming specific technology categories: high-powered microwaves, acoustic weapons, pulsed electromagnetic systems. The Congressional Research Service documents that the DOD defines directed energy weapons as those using “concentrated electromagnetic energy to incapacitate, damage, disable, or destroy enemy equipment, facilities, and/or personnel,” and notes the DOD plans to scale these weapons from 150 kilowatts to 500 kilowatts and then to megawatt levels in the 2025–2030 timeframe. The GAO has confirmed the DOD spends approximately $1 billion annually on directed energy weapon development. The Active Denial System— a millimeter wave device that creates incapacitating pain through skin heating— has been demonstrated for civilian law enforcement use. The Silent Guardian, a smaller mobile version, is explicitly marketed to civilian law enforcement agencies and private security providers.
Richard Pearson named these technologies in July 2025, six months before the government publicly confirmed them. He described the precise phenomenology— microwave auditory transmission, pulsed electromagnetic systems, behavioral direction— that government officials boasted about in January 2026. The prior identification is not coincidental.

Havana Syndrome: The Institutional Gaslighting Template
The Havana Syndrome story is the single most instructive parallel to Pearson’s situation, because it documents— in exhaustive and now congressional-record detail— exactly the gaslighting and institutional suppression he describes, applied to the government’s own personnel.
Since 2016, hundreds of U.S. diplomats, intelligence officers, and military personnel reported symptoms consistent with directed energy weapon exposure: sudden pressure in the head, clear directional sound perception, severe vertigo, cognitive disruption, and lasting neurological effects. The CIA, under pressure from leadership, concluded that the symptoms were psychosomatic, attributing them to “environmental factors” or pre-existing conditions. Senior CIA leaders privately denigrated victims, accusing them of fabricating conditions for financial gain.
One former CIA officer stated that his superiors “wanted to push the conclusion that AHIs were psychosomatic” and that “they made fun of AHI victims.”

“The ODNI… made an official determination on Havana Syndrome that it was ‘very unlikely’ victims were attacked by a state actor or foreign adversary… Not only downplayed but dismissed, in some cases, buried.”— Former CIA officer, CBS News/60 Minutes, 2026
In 2024, a CIA Subcommittee report found “direct evidence” that the 2023 Intelligence Community Assessment “was developed in a manner inconsistent with analytic integrity.” In October 2025, the committee sent criminal referrals to the Department of Justice for potential

illegal actions by intelligence officials in suppressing the Havana Syndrome investigation. Victims were denied medical care. Their injuries were called psychosomatic. The CIA resisted compelling intelligence that challenged its preferred conclusion.


































In late 2024, a clandestine operation acquired a device— purchased for eight figures through an undercover DHS operation funded by the Defense Department— that investigators believe may be connected to Havana Syndrome cases. The Pentagon spent more than a year testing it. The device exists. It was worth tens of millions of dollars. Government officials paid that much because they took the weapon seriously even while the official public position was that no such weapon likely existed.
As of March 2026, a first-person account by a Havana Syndrome victim published in War on the Rocks stated: “Several senior officials told us directly: We believe you. One even admitted: We failed you.” The same official position—“very unlikely” a foreign actor— was maintained publicly even as senior officials acknowledged privately that the CIA’s approach was “terribly flawed.”

This is the template. Official denial. Private acknowledgment. Victim discrediting. Psychiatric attribution. And underneath it: a classified weapon acquisition program costing tens of millions of dollars that only exists if the weapon is real.
Pearson is not a diplomat. He is a Black academic. The same technology, applied to the same type of experience, produces official denial rather than even the partial acknowledgment afforded to government personnel. The institutional mechanism that produces this differential outcome— credentialed government employees get partial investigation, Black scholars get the psychiatric label— is documented in the Havana Syndrome record.


The Targeting Infrastructure: Who Gets Designated and How The Black Identity Extremist Designation and Its Successors
In August 2017, the FBI’s Counterterrorism Division issued an intelligence assessment
designating “Black Identity Extremists” as a domestic terrorism threat category. The assessment cited six unrelated incidents over three years in which Black individuals attacked police officers, and concluded these constituted evidence of a coordinated ideological movement. The FBI disseminated the designation to thousands of police departments nationwide.
Civil liberties organizations and former government officials immediately recognized the designation as a fabricated threat category. Studies show attacks against police officers are extremely rare and that white men carry out the overwhelming majority. The FBI’s own report contradicted itself by acknowledging the six incidents were “influenced by more than one ideological perspective.” A former homeland security official noted the FBI was “grouping together Black Panthers, black nationalists, and Washitaw Nation” in the same way one might lump together disparate white political groups and call it “white identity extremism.”
In 2019, FBI Director Wray testified that the BIE label was no longer in use. Documents leaked to The Young Turks demonstrated this was false: the FBI had simply renamed the label to “Racially Motivated Violent Extremism” (RMVE) while continuing the same targeting programs under the IRON FIST operational designation. The ACLU’s litigation established that “there is no indication that IRON FIST or any other programs used to target Black people for surveillance have been dismantled.”
The BIE designation required no criminal conduct for targeting. It required only the expression of political views the FBI characterized as consistent with the invented category. A Black public health scholar who publishes work on racial equity and systemic racism in government health policy— Pearson’s precise profile— fits the targeting criteria of BIE/RMVE without any criminal conduct whatsoever. The dissertation’s explicit criticism of government institutions and its “Critical Race” methodology are not merely consistent with the targeting logic. Under the FBI’s documented framework, they are the targeting trigger.

Executive Order 14173 and the DEI Targeting Logic
On January 20, 2025, Trump signed Executive Order 14173, which declared diversity, equity, and inclusion programs in federal agencies and federally funded institutions to be threats to national interest. The administration subsequently moved to terminate DEI offices across the federal government, defund DEI-related research, and investigate institutions receiving federal funding for DEI compliance.
Pearson co-authored a peer-reviewed paper introducing a framework for understanding racial inequity. His dissertation explicitly frames government health policy through a critical race lens and advocates for systematic challenges to those policies. His nonprofit focuses on equity scholarship. Under EO 14173’s logic— confirmed by subsequent agency actions— his intellectual work is not merely politically disfavored. It is within the category of activity the administration has declared contrary to national interest.
The convergence is precise: a Black PhD scholar publishing on racial equity, in a city where the administration has declared DEI a national security threat, in a year when the FBI’s RMVE designation continues to categorize Black political expression as a domestic terrorism risk, in a legal environment where every accountability mechanism has been systematically eliminated. The targeting logic does not require a conspiracy theory. It requires only that the documented programs operate as they are documented to operate.

Palantir and the Targeting Infrastructure
Palantir’s ImmigrationOS contract— documented in April 2025 — provides infrastructure for systematic targeting of individuals based on immigration status, political profile, and law enforcement history. ICE operations in 2025–2026 showed a 600% increase in “at-large” arrests, including many individuals with no criminal record. The infrastructure for identifying, locating, and operationally targeting individuals based on profile data exists and is being actively deployed.
The same data infrastructure— centralized federal databases, Palantir’s analytical platform, expanded surveillance authorities— that enables immigration enforcement enables the targeting of anyone whose profile is flagged in the federal system. The ACCESS DENIED flag on Pearson’s residential address in MPD’s database is the local expression of this federal classification infrastructure. It is not a different system. It is the same system with a different designation applied.


The Propaganda Apparatus: Narrative Control as Operational Tool The “Fake News” Defense

When the Social Security Administration’s inspector general investigation found evidence of DOGE data violations, the agency’s response was to call the reporting “fake news” designed to “scare seniors.” When fired inspectors general pursued reinstatement, the White House characterized them as “rogue, partisan bureaucrats who have weaponized the justice system against their political enemies.” When the CIA’s Havana Syndrome investigation was criticized, leadership characterized the skeptics as fabricators seeking financial gain.
This is the propaganda template as operational procedure: any accountability mechanism that produces inconvenient findings is characterized as politically motivated, mentally unstable, or deliberately fraudulent. The characterization serves the same function as the psychiatric label in Pearson’s account. It converts the accurate report into evidence of the reporter’s bad faith, without ever engaging the substance of what was reported.

Misdirection Through Institutional Language
The renaming of Havana Syndrome from a weapon attack to “Anomalous Health Incidents” is a textbook example of institutional misdirection through language. “AHI” is an incident category, not a diagnosis. It signals uncertainty while appearing to categorize. The label change simultaneously acknowledges that something happened while foreclosing the most threatening interpretation. It allows officials to say they are taking the matter seriously while the operational conclusion—“very unlikely” a foreign actor— makes treatment unavailable and accountability impossible.
The same linguistic operation functions in the RMVE designation. “Racially Motivated Violent Extremism” sounds like a category defined by violence. In operation, it targets people who have not committed violence and may never commit violence, based on their racial identity and political expression. The language of “extremism” and “violence” produces institutional credibility for a designation that requires neither.

The International Withdrawal Narrative
The administration’s stated rationale for withdrawing from 66 international organizations— that they are “wasteful,” a “threat to sovereignty,” or engaged in “anti-Semitism”— is a narrative designed to make accountability withdrawal seem principled rather than protective. The specific organizations withdrawn from are precisely those with mandates to investigate and report human rights violations, including against Americans. Framing the withdrawal as protecting sovereignty from “outside interference” reframes the elimination of accountability mechanisms as an expression of national pride.
This is the misdirection layer: the action (eliminating oversight) is reframed through a narrative (protecting sovereignty) that makes the action seem not only acceptable but patriotic. A person pointing out that the withdrawal eliminates the mechanism for investigating their own case is positioned as opposing American sovereignty rather than opposing their own persecution.

The Mental Health Attribution Machine

The Havana Syndrome record provides the most thoroughly documented example of the psychiatric attribution mechanism. CIA leadership characterized victims’ symptoms as psychosomatic. The ODNI’s official assessment concluded “very unlikely” a foreign actor, implying the symptoms were generated internally rather than externally. NIH studies found “no significant differences in imaging measures of brain structure or function”— a finding presented as evidence that nothing happened, rather than as evidence that the specific harm mechanism does not produce the imaging signatures being measured.
The mechanism is consistent with what Pearson describes: a genuine symptom cluster produced by external assault is diagnosed as internally generated. The diagnosis is not malicious in each individual instance— the clinician working with incomplete information produces the most parsimonious explanation available to her. The malice, if present, operates at the system level: by ensuring that the relevant history is classified, that the technology’s existence is denied, and that the treatment infrastructure for the actual injury does not exist, the system produces the psychiatric attribution through the ordinary functioning of clinical medicine rather than through deliberate falsification.

“We believe you. We failed you.”— Senior Biden White House official to Havana Syndrome victims, November 2024 Situation Room meeting, confirmed March 2026


The Gaslighting Architecture: Making Accurate Perception Incredible How the System Produces Disbelief
The most precise definition of institutional gaslighting is: a system that produces accurate
perception in its targets and then systematically destroys the credibility of that perception through mechanisms that appear legitimate to outside observers. The Havana Syndrome case is the cleanest documented example: hundreds of government personnel accurately perceived that they had been subjected to directed energy attack; the institutional response converted that accurate perception into psychiatric attribution through official denial, classified evidence suppression, and the language of “anomalous health incidents.”
The mechanism works because it does not require that any individual actor lie. The clinician who diagnoses psychosomatic symptoms is working from accurate clinical information without access to the classified operational context that would change the differential diagnosis. The judge who dismisses the lawsuit for lack of standing is correctly applying standing doctrine. The journalist who declines to report the story because they cannot verify classified evidence is following appropriate journalistic standards. Each individual institutional actor behaves reasonably. The gaslighting is in the system design, not in any individual’s conduct.

The Loop That Cannot Close

The essays describe what might be called the impossibility loop: to prove the targeting, you need discovery; to get discovery, you need a viable legal claim; to have a viable legal claim, you need specific facts; the specific facts are classified; therefore you cannot plead the claim; therefore there is no discovery; therefore you cannot prove the targeting. Add to this the psychiatric attribution mechanism— your persistence in trying to close the loop becomes evidence of obsession— and the loop is hermetically sealed.
This loop is not a metaphor. It is the documented operational reality of the American legal system as applied to classified domestic program claims. Every door Pearson documents having tried— the courts, the police, the mental health system, the asylum system, the media— closes through the normal functioning of its institutional logic, without any individual actor having to behave unlawfully. The system is designed to produce this outcome. The design is the gaslighting.

The Confirmation That Came Six Months Late
In July 2025, Pearson described directed energy technology producing auditory intrusions on the National Mall. In January 2026, government officials publicly confirmed the existence and domestic deployment of directed energy weapons. In March 2026, War on the Rocks confirmed that CIA victims were told privately “we believe you” while the official public position remained “very unlikely.”
The six-month gap between Pearson’s accurate description and the government’s public confirmation is the gaslighting in temporal form. During those six months, anyone who heard his account and lacked access to the classified evidence that the government was simultaneously acquiring and testing would rationally conclude his claims were implausible. The six-month gap was not an accident of timing. It is the operational structure: classified knowledge produces public implausibility, and public implausibility protects classified knowledge.


The Convergence: What All This Means Together
Richard Pearson’s claim is that the government is conducting a domestic targeting program against targeted individuals, using classified technology, legal architecture, and institutional design to make that program invisible, victims incredible, and accountability impossible. He claims the program operates against Black intellectuals and activists as a continuation of COINTELPRO. He claims the propaganda, gaslighting, and secrecy are not incidental features but operational necessities.
The independently verified evidentiary record from 2025–2026 confirms, at every structural level, the environment in which such a program would operate.
The oversight mechanisms that would detect it— 17 Inspectors General, civil rights monitoring offices, the CIGIE coordinating body— were removed in the first weeks of the administration.

The surveillance infrastructure that would enable it— FISA backdoor searches, warrantless data purchase, DOGE’s centralized database, social media monitoring, DOD-AI integration— was expanded and consolidated throughout 2025.
The international accountability mechanisms that would adjudicate it— the UNHRC, periodic review processes, 66 international organizations including human rights bodies, the ICC— were eliminated by withdrawal.
The technology it would require— directed energy weapons producing auditory intrusions, behavioral direction, neurological disruption— was publicly confirmed by the Department of War CTO and the President himself in January 2026.
The institutional gaslighting mechanism it relies on— official denial, psychiatric attribution, victim discrediting, classified suppression of contrary evidence— is documented in exhaustive detail in the Havana Syndrome congressional record, including criminal referrals for illegal conduct by CIA officials in suppressing the investigation.
The targeting designation that would authorize it— BIE/RMVE, Executive Order 14173, the National Security Presidential Memorandum of September 2025 on “organized political violence”— is documented and operational, requiring no criminal conduct from targets and applying specifically to the profile Pearson presents.
The propaganda apparatus that sustains it—“fake news” characterizations of critical reporting, linguistic misdirection through institutional language, “sovereignty” narratives that frame accountability elimination as patriotism— is documented and active.
None of these findings individually proves what happened to Richard Pearson. Together, they establish that every element of the environment he describes is confirmed to exist, to be expanding, and to be operating precisely as he describes it operating.
At the level of res ipsa loquitur — does the totality of the record, absent explanation, point in a single direction— the convergence of independently verified evidence across oversight, surveillance, accountability, technology, gaslighting, targeting, and propaganda points toward the environment Pearson describes as not merely plausible but structurally necessary. A government that has eliminated its watchdogs, expanded its surveillance, confirmed its weapons, withdrawn from international accountability, adopted targeting designations for Black scholars, and documented its own institutional gaslighting of weapon attack victims does not need to be theorized as a torturer. The record establishes the capability, the motive, the method, and the cover.
What the record does not establish— and what may never be established, precisely because the architecture was designed to prevent its establishment— is the specific operational order that aimed this infrastructure at one man in Washington, D.C. in March 2025.

But the question the convergence poses is the question the Havana Syndrome victims posed before the Situation Room meeting where officials said “we believe you”: not whether it is possible, but whether, if it happened, our institutions would be capable of knowing.
The answer, in both cases, is no. And in that structural incapacity— designed, maintained, and expanding— is both Pearson’s predicament and the document’s significance.


All findings drawn from independently published sources, 2025–2026. Sources include: Center on Budget and Policy Priorities; Brennan Center for Justice; ACLU; Human Rights Watch World Report 2026; Amnesty International; CBS News/60 Minutes Havana Syndrome investigation; War on the Rocks; Washington Post; Congressional Research Service; GAO reports
GAO-23–106717 and GAO-23–105868; Senate investigation (Ranking Member Gary Peters); House Judiciary Subcommittee hearing (April 8, 2025); White House Executive Orders 14173 and 14199; Presidential Memorandum (January 7, 2026); focus2030.org; CVT.org; and primary government documents cited therein. Document prepared March 2026.







The US Tortures More Than You Know. We Just Have Refused to Believe Victims.
At what point do we begin to see the bigger picture— countless victims, eerily similar stories, legal doctrines that make it easy? The Torture Dungeon is real.

What It Is and Is Not
The first precision Pearson demands is definitional. The Torture Dungeon is not a place. It is not a facility with walls, a registry of inmates, a director with a nameplate on a door. It is a condition — a persistent operational environment that exists in parallel with visible American constitutional democracy, drawing on the same institutional infrastructure, funded by the same budgets, staffed by the same agencies, but operating in a legal and social space specifically engineered to be invisible, unaccountable, and self-concealing.
Pearson’s Theresienstadt metaphor is exact. Nazi Germany did not conduct the Holocaust in its propaganda films. America does not conduct the Torture Dungeon in its constitutional law textbooks, its civil rights museums, or its State Department human rights reports.
The Upstairs — due process, judicial review, FOIA requests, the ACLU, the First Amendment— is real. It governs most Americans most of the time. Its reality for the majority is

precisely what makes the Dungeon’s operation against the minority structurally invisible. The Upstairs is not a lie. It is a selection mechanism: it applies to those the system has decided it applies to.




The Dungeon’s Legal Foundation Reynolds and the State Secrets Doctrine
The architectural cornerstone was laid in 1953. United States v. Reynolds established that the
government could assert “state secrets privilege” to terminate litigation before any court examined the underlying facts. The court defers to the assertion. No merits review. No discovery. Case dismissed.
When the Reynolds documents were finally declassified decades later, they contained not national security secrets but evidence of government negligence— the very misconduct the privilege had concealed. The doctrine built on fraud continues to govern. Every targeted individual who seeks legal remedy for classified program abuses runs into Reynolds first.

Sovereign Immunity
The Federal Tort Claims Act grants consent to be sued for a slipping hazard on a federal building floor. It does not grant consent to be sued for torturing a citizen with directed energy weapons. This was not an oversight. The consent structure was architected to exclude the most serious abuses while maintaining the appearance of government accountability.

The Systematic Elimination of Bivens

Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents (1971) created a cause of action against individual federal officers for constitutional violations. The Supreme Court has spent fifty years eliminating it. Any technology developed after 1971 constitutes a “new context.” Voice-to-skull technology: new context, no Bivens claim. Remote neural monitoring: new context, no claim. Directed energy behavioral control: new context, no claim. The agents who operate these systems against American citizens are personally beyond civil liability— not because their conduct is legal but because the remedy was never created.

Qualified Immunity
Even where a Bivens claim theoretically exists, qualified immunity requires that the specific conduct have been previously ruled unconstitutional in a near-identical case. Since no case involving directed energy neurological weapons has survived threshold dismissal to reach merits adjudication, there is no prior ruling. The first deployment is immune. The hundredth deployment is immune. The novelty of the method is built-in legal protection.

Glomar and Classification
Beyond these doctrines: Glomar responses (“we can neither confirm nor deny”), classification of operational details, redaction of responsive documents, and the ability to classify program existence itself create a hermetic seal. Pearson’s own research identified one hundred such legal mechanisms, layered cumulatively, each designed to terminate a case before the next layer is needed.
This architecture was not assembled accidentally. It represents deliberate institutional design spanning seven decades. The Dungeon’s legal infrastructure predates its most advanced operational capabilities.


Historical Continuity: How the Dungeon Was Built The Nazi Science Transfer
Operation Paperclip imported over 1,600 Nazi scientists, engineers, and physicians— including
SS officers, Dachau experimenters, Auschwitz researchers— into American military and intelligence programs between 1945 and 1959, with falsified war crimes records. The specific expertise transferred included:
Dr. Herman Schwan, who established American electromagnetic radiation safety standards in the 1950s and whose position— that there are no proven athermal biological effects of EMR— became official U.S. policy for forty years while classified research proceeded on exactly those effects.
Specialists in psychological manipulation, behavioral control, and human physiological tolerance limits.

Aerospace and weapons engineers whose work fed directly into classified programs.

The Dungeon did not develop its methods independently. It inherited them.

Project CHATTER to MKUltra: The Mind Control Lineage
The unbroken programmatic lineage begins with the Navy’s Project CHATTER in 1947 — explicitly inspired by Nazi mescaline experiments— runs through Projects BLUEBIRD and ARTICHOKE (hypnosis, barbiturates, physical coercion, amnesia induction), and consolidates in MKUltra from 1953 to 1973.
MKUltra’s scope was not limited to LSD experiments. Its 150+ subprojects across 80 institutions included:
Ewen Cameron’s psychic driving at McGill: patients chemically maintained in sleep for weeks or months, subjected to electroshock at 30–40 times standard dose, with looped audio messages played through helmets into unconscious minds— patients arrived with mild depression and emerged with destroyed identities and complete amnesia. The CIA funded it. Canada paid reparations. The U.S. did not.
Subproject 54, researching induction of concussion and amnesia through non-detectable means.
Research into bioelectric manipulation of human cognition.
Study of sensory deprivation effects that would later be reverse-engineered from SERE training into post-9/11 “enhanced interrogation.”
CIA Director Richard Helms destroyed most MKUltra records in 1973. What was found in a misfiled document cache and released in 1977 represents an incomplete record of a program whose full scope the destruction was specifically designed to prevent from becoming known.

The Frey Effect and Military Microwave Research
In 1961–1962, biophysicist Allan Frey published documented findings that pulsed microwave radiation could create auditory sensations perceived inside the skull, bypassing the ears entirely. The military funded immediate replication and refinement. By the early 1970s, researchers at Walter Reed Army Institute— Joseph Sharp and Mark Grove— had demonstrated intelligible speech transmission via pulsed microwaves. Don Justesen published a semi-public reference to this in American Psychologist in 1975. James Lin published a comprehensive textbook on microwave auditory effects and applications in 1978.
From 1975 to 1996, when Gloria Naylor experienced what she described, twenty-one years of classified refinement occurred at military and intelligence agency facilities with classified budgets. From 1996 to 2025, when Pearson experienced what he describes, an additional twenty-nine years of refinement occurred.

The Army maintained “Voice to Skull Device” in its online research thesaurus until removing the entry from public access. Multiple patents document the underlying principle. James Giordano, neuroethicist at Georgetown University and Pentagon/NATO advisor, stated publicly in 2025 that mind reading and mind control technologies have been “loosed into the world.” The U.S. Air Force Scientific Advisory Board’s 1995–1996 “New World Vistas” document— published concurrent with Naylor’s experiences— explicitly discussed non-lethal weapons providing “a condition of learned helplessness” through neurological targeting.


COINTELPRO and Its Successors
From 1956 to 1971, the FBI’s Counter Intelligence Program operationally targeted:

The Black Panther Party (Fred Hampton murdered at 21 in a pre-dawn FBI-coordinated raid while asleep; the FBI paid the informant who provided the floor plan)
The Southern Christian Leadership Conference and Martin Luther King Jr. (wiretapped for years; the FBI sent King an anonymous letter explicitly urging suicide days before he received the Nobel Peace Prize)
The Nation of Islam, SNCC, AIM, Puerto Rican independence organizations, antiwar groups
Individual writers and intellectuals identified as threats to “existing social order”

Tactics included: informant infiltration, forged letters to incite internal conflict, “snitch-jacketing” (falsely labeling members as informants to provoke retaliatory violence), blackmail, fabricated evidence, coordination with local police for raids.

The Church Committee exposed COINTELPRO in 1975. Congress passed new oversight laws. The apparatus of state secrets privilege was not dismantled. The FBI’s 2017 “Black Identity Extremist” designation— a fabricated domestic terrorism category with no statutory basis, requiring no criminal conduct for targeting, directed specifically at Black activists
post-Ferguson— demonstrates programmatic continuity. The successor programs continued under different names.
Operation MINARET simultaneously ran NSA watchlists targeting Black intellectuals, antiwar activists, and journalists. Every Black, Latino, and Asian writer who received press coverage was on a list that officially did not exist. This is not Naylor’s fictional Dick Simon’s invention. This is documented congressional testimony.


The Dungeon’s Operational Structure Target Selection
The targeting criterion varies generally. In Pearson’s case, it is precise: Black intellectuals of
achievement and strong racial identity, whistleblowers, activists, and anyone whose intellectual production or political presence represents a specific and manageable threat to existing arrangements of power.
“Specific” is important. The system does not target randomly. It targets people whose credibility, if maintained, would make the maintenance of specific power arrangements harder. A National Book Award-winning novelist who weeps at the Million Man March and writes about Black women’s lives with moral authority is a specific threat— not to national security as conventionally defined, but to the ideological infrastructure that normalizes racial hierarchy. A PhD equity scholar who publishes methodologically rigorous findings on racial inequality in the current political environment in which DEI itself is being declared a threat— is a specific threat by the same logic.
The targeting begins from a trigger, often petty. For Naylor: a neighbor’s cat and a sibling relationship that connected it to an NSA official. For Pearson: the precise trigger is not fully established, but the profile match is exact. The trigger is not the cause. It is the access point. The machinery was already there. The trigger provides an excuse to aim it.



The Personnel: Who Staffs the Dungeon
The Dungeon requires more bodies than any federal workforce can supply without creating accountability-exposing paper trails. The solution is the contractor structure:
Federal agencies — NSA, CIA, FBI, DIA, and through joint task force structures, local law enforcement— provide direction, technology, legal cover, and coordination.
Defense contractors — Booz Allen Hamilton (Snowden’s employer), SAIC, Leidos, CACI International (paid for Abu Ghraib interrogations), ManTech, and hundreds of smaller firms provide personnel, technical operations, and field implementation at arm’s length from direct federal accountability.
The ADL and similar organizations — The Anti-Defamation League was caught in 1993 running illegal domestic surveillance on over 12,000 individuals and 950 organizations, sharing intelligence with foreign governments including apartheid South Africa, infiltrating domestic political organizations, and working with police informants. It settled the resulting civil lawsuit for
$175,000. In Naylor’s documented account, ADL operatives were coordinated into her surveillance through a cousin at the Columbia office. Three years after the ADL’s documented illegal surveillance program was exposed.
Community volunteers and contractors — The Dungeon requires, for operations like vehicle formations, red-and-black color deployments, and street theater, a large number of individuals who are told only that they are participating in a security operation against a target who represents a threat. Some are paid. Some are ideologically motivated. Some, like the
72-year-old Dachau survivor Naylor describes— who joined the car pool because she was told there was a threat and knew from her own history what threats become if not addressed early— have no idea what they are actually participating in.

The pyramid is: classified technology and direction at the top, contractor implementation in the middle, and community-sourced labor for volume operations at the base. Each layer has plausible deniability about the layer above.

The Operational Rooms
Pearson describes the Dungeon as containing rooms. Each room is a distinct operational modality, deployed simultaneously:


Room One: Sleep Deprivation

The ceiling above the target’s bed becomes a delivery mechanism. Real-time localization technology— documented in both classified literature and published academic research on through-wall radar— allows tracking of a body within an apartment. The neighbor above receives direction, or is a placed operative, or the building itself has been instrumented. The sounds follow the target room to room. To the closet. At 3 AM.
Sleep deprivation is internationally recognized as torture under conventions the United States has signed, promoted, and cited as evidence of its commitment to human rights. It is being conducted through residential ceilings.
Its effects are not merely physical discomfort. Sustained sleep deprivation produces: impaired judgment, emotional dysregulation, paranoid ideation, hallucinations, impaired memory consolidation, and— critically— a presentation that looks clinically identical to the early stages of psychotic decompensation. The person describing their experience to a psychiatrist after weeks of targeted sleep deprivation will present exactly as someone developing a serious mental illness. This is the operational specification, not a side effect.


Room Two: The Noise Campaign and Environmental Manipulation

Adjacent to sleep deprivation but distinct in its mechanics. The noise campaign is not primarily about the noise. It is about the systematic conversion of the target’s environment from neutral to weaponized— while maintaining to every outside observer that nothing extraordinary is occurring.
Hammers. Chainsaws. Lawn mowers in calibrated patterns from a rented adjacent property. Car doors slamming in rotation outside a study window. A neighbor dropping objects precisely above wherever the target is located within their apartment. The frequency and precision that distinguishes deliberate operation from ordinary urban noise is perceivable to the target and invisible to anyone else.

The secondary effect, as David Shane explained in Naylor’s account and as Pearson documents, is environmental conditioning. The target becomes sensitized to specific stimuli. Once conditioned, the stimuli only need to be present sporadically to maintain the psychological effect. The target does the work of harassment themselves, perceiving threat in sounds that, by that point, may or may not be operationally deployed.


Room Three: Vehicle Formations and Street Presence

Coordinated vehicle operations serve multiple functions. In a rural area like St. Helena, a specific pattern— car arrives, turns, cruises past, turns, exits— on a dead-end road with no other destination communicates operational presence while remaining legally invisible. Each individual car is just a car taking a drive.
In an urban area like Washington DC, the formation adapts: vehicles pulling from parking spaces as the target walks, following at a fixed interval, always present when the target leaves home. The operational requirement is not that every vehicle be a deployed asset. It is that enough are that the pattern is perceivable to the sensitized target and explainable as coincidence to everyone else.
The color-coded clothing operation Pearson documents serves the same communicative function through different logistics. Forty-three people in coordinated red and black across a 90-minute walk in one neighborhood is not Washington DC’s fashion distribution. It is a deployment communicating: we are everywhere. We see you. We are organized. There is nothing you can do.








































Room Four: Home Entry and Object Displacement

The panini grill button. Naylor’s flashlight on the mantle. The disturbed attic insulation. The seed package with curled edges.
Entry without theft. Entry that leaves evidence of its own occurrence while leaving nothing that would constitute actionable evidence for law enforcement or legal proceedings. The message delivered is not: we were here and we took something. The message is: we were here. We can enter when we choose. We can touch anything in your home. We will leave only what you yourself can perceive, and nothing that anyone else would take seriously.
This is a psychological operation targeting the most fundamental security instinct: the belief that your home is a protected space. Once that belief is destroyed, the target has no refuge. Outside

is surveilled. Inside has been violated. The library is the only remaining sanctuary— and that only because the technology has a range limitation.


Room Five: Digital Compromise

The phone. The laptop. The email. The social media account.

What Pearson describes— phones disappearing and returning, a device returned by a Secret Service agent with his driver’s license still inside, text appearing in a SORA prompt that he did not type— is the experiential presentation of the documented NSA TEMPEST program’s
read-write capability. Van Eck phreaking (reading computer electromagnetic emissions) has a documented inverse: using directed RF emissions to interact with computer circuit boards. The NSA’s classified capabilities, running as documented by Snowden and others 15–20 years ahead of public technology, include exactly this.
PRISM, XKeyscore, MUSCULAR— all revealed by Snowden in 2013 — demonstrated that the NSA was already, before 2013, collecting essentially all electronic communications of essentially all Americans. The specific targeting of an individual’s device goes further: real-time monitoring of keystrokes, installation of hidden firmware that persists through factory resets, conversion of the microphone and camera into permanent surveillance instruments. The device the target carries everywhere becomes a room in the Dungeon that they carry with them.


Room Six: Informant Recruitment from the Social Network

C.J. Hudson. A friend of ten years. A Princeton historian. He dissolved sleeping pills in Naylor’s wine and handed her laptop to NSA operatives. He did it because they had leverage— a prior arrest on a morals charge involving a teenage male prostitute, made to disappear by one call, documented and held for exactly this moment.
The recruitment of people from within the target’s social network is both operationally efficient— trusted access, no surveillance signatures required— and specifically devastating because it destroys the target’s ability to trust anyone. After C.J., Naylor could not be certain that any friend might not be an asset. This is not paranoia. It is the rational response to confirmed operational fact.
Pearson’s account includes similar encounters with people whose behavior suggests prior briefing— excessive familiarity, code-like communication, knowledge of specific private details. “Lee” at the Potbelly’s. David Meyerowitz with his specific prepared narrative. The stranger in Rockville who quoted Bartleby before Pearson had time to tell anyone about “canceling the deal.”

The operational requirement is not that every social contact be an asset. It is that enough are that the target cannot maintain trust in any of them, and that the social network— the primary support structure against psychological disintegration— is converted into a source of threat.


Room Seven: Chemical Assault

Naylor’s garden was poisoned. The Boys played games with rat poison under her house, crawling through the crawl space with wrenches and bags of mud at 3 AM, calling it “poison the target.”
Pearson describes sitting at the Rockville Metro and discovering his pants soaked with an industrial chemical— sticky, highly odorous, adhering to his skin so severely that removing the pants later felt like tearing his epidermis. A dead bird at the location, presumably the delivery mechanism. The chemical had not soaked through from the environment. It had been applied.
The Dungeon’s documented appendix includes chemical assault as a standard operational tool: the NSA’s own Akwei lawsuit documentation references “chemicals and drugs into residential buildings with hidden NSA-installed and maintained plastic plumbing lines” for drug delivery.
Operation Sea-Spray deliberately aerosolized bacterial agents over San Francisco. The Army released Bacillus subtilis into New York City subway ventilation. Chemical delivery to a specific individual in a public space is, by comparison, trivially achievable.


Room Eight: The Voice

This is the Dungeon’s most technically advanced room and its most operationally brilliant— because the symptom it produces is identical to the most stigmatized and least credible psychiatric presentation available: auditory hallucinations characteristic of paranoid schizophrenia.
The Frey effect— pulsed microwaves depositing sound directly into the auditory cortex, bypassing the ears— was demonstrated in 1961. Speech transmission via this mechanism was demonstrated in the early 1970s. Sixty years of classified development have produced what Naylor described as a conversational voice operating nightly from an adjacent building, and what Pearson describes as a woman’s voice on a microphone directing him across Washington DC on a specific documented day.
The Army maintained “Voice to Skull Device” in its research thesaurus. Multiple patents document the underlying physics. The European Parliament’s 1999 resolution called for an international ban on weapons “which might enable any form of manipulation of human beings.” They were not calling for a ban on hypothetical future technology.

The operational specification of this room is precise and documented: the weapon was engineered to mimic paranoid schizophrenia because the misdiagnosis is the point. The psychiatric system— staffed by practitioners genuinely trying to help— becomes, without their knowledge or consent, an operational asset for the program it cannot see. The target who describes voices is given antipsychotics that do not address the actual cause, which creates apparent treatment resistance, which supports a more severe diagnosis, which deepens the psychiatric attribution, which closes the loop.
Both Naylor and Pearson describe the key diagnostic tell: the voices are localized. They come from a specific direction. They are absent in specific physical locations. They stop when the target moves beyond the device’s operational range. They have an acoustic signature— a woman on a microphone, background noise of an operational environment— that distinguishes them from internally generated experience. And they say things the target would never say to themselves: I hate Jews. I want to kill myself. We’re going to make you disappear.
The content is operationally chosen. It targets the specific psychological vulnerabilities of the specific individual. For Naylor, who had worked to resist antisemitism throughout the surveillance: you hate Jews. For Pearson, whose identity and mental health are both at stake: degrading names and suicide suggestions. The voice does not just harass. It attempts to produce the internal state that will manifest externally as evidence of the psychiatric presentation the operation has diagnosed in advance.


Room Nine: Pinning Technology

This room Pearson names and describes with more specificity than Naylor, who experienced it without vocabulary to identify it.
Pinning is the directional influence of behavior through targeted neurological stimulation— not auditory implantation but motor and cognitive direction. The experience is of having a strong, unusually compelling impulse to go to a specific location, make a specific choice, take a specific action— an impulse that the subject, in retrospect, recognizes as discontinuous with their normal decision-making pattern.
The Akwei lawsuit describes RNM capabilities including influence over motor control. The “New World Vistas” document describes inducing “learned helplessness.” Persinger’s 1995
peer-reviewed paper described theoretical pathways for behavioral influence at a distance via ELF waves. The neurological research underlying these capabilities— Delgado’s work on the stimoceiver, Adey’s work on ELF effects on neural calcium ion efflux, the classified military development on top of both— provides the scientific foundation.
The operational use Pearson describes is specific: he is directed toward Georgetown on July 13 as part of what he calls an “extraction attempt.” He is directed to return to the tub in the park near the Capitol, where contents appeared designed to compromise him in a specific way. The

pinning is not random behavioral disruption. It is targeted behavioral direction toward operationally useful outcomes.


The Psychiatric Trap: The Dungeon’s Master Seal
Every room in the Dungeon produces a symptom indistinguishable from a recognized psychiatric presentation:
Sleep deprivation: cognitive impairment, paranoid ideation, emotional dysregulation
Environmental harassment: hypervigilance, ideas of reference
Informant recruitment: paranoid attachment patterns, inability to trust
Voice-to-skull: auditory hallucinations characteristic of paranoid schizophrenia
Pinning: delusions of external control
Digital compromise: paranoid belief that devices are surveilling

The psychiatric trap works as follows: the target, experiencing real operations, begins to describe them. The description, absent knowledge of the operational history and classified capabilities, sounds exactly like a psychiatric decompensation. Friends and family apply the “crazy” label— not from malice but from a reasonable application of Occam’s razor in the absence of the relevant classified context. Employers lose confidence. The target is referred to mental health services. Mental health professionals, trained in diagnostic frameworks that do not include “directed energy neurological weapons as a differential diagnosis for auditory hallucinations,” apply the closest available diagnostic category. Treatment is initiated. The treatment does not address the actual cause. The apparent treatment failure reinforces the diagnosis. The target is further discredited.
The misdiagnosis then becomes evidence in the social network that the target cannot be believed. Whatever documentation they have produced is recontextualized as evidence of paranoid ideation rather than accurate observation. The entire record is sealed under the psychiatric attribution.
Both Naylor and Pearson obtained preemptive mental health evaluations specifically to defeat this mechanism. Both were found rational. Pearson explicitly describes understanding the trap was coming and moving to close it in advance.
The trap has a specific failure mode: it fails when the target has sufficient intellectual resources to understand what is being done to them, sufficient methodological training to document it systematically, and the specific courage to keep the documentation in the world before any classification order can reach it.


The Population: Who Is in the Dungeon

The Dungeon’s population cannot be precisely counted— this is a design feature. But Pearson reasons from available evidence:
Cheryl Welsh’s organization received over 1,800 documented claims by 2003 — a floor, not a ceiling, given the proportion who do not find reporting organizations. The gangstalking community numbers in the tens of thousands across documented online networks. Researchers who study this population typically apply psychiatric frameworks without engaging the documented history of COINTELPRO, the documented capabilities of intelligence agencies, or the documented legal architecture that makes verification impossible. The application of psychiatric frameworks without this context to a population that may in significant part be experiencing real operations is itself a form of institutional participation in the Dungeon’s psychiatric seal.
The whistleblower population, partially documented through congressional testimony and journalistic investigation, shows consistent patterns of post-disclosure harassment matching the operational profile.
The populations subjected to documented FBI Black Identity Extremist surveillance after 2014, the Muslim American communities subjected to NYPD/FBI infiltration programs documented by the Associated Press in 2011, the indigenous communities whose political organizing has been systematically disrupted— these are populations whose partial targeting is confirmed through official sources and represent the visible edge of a larger distribution.
Pearson’s estimate: tens of thousands at minimum. Potentially hundreds of thousands. Distributed across the country’s cities and towns and rural areas. Most without the educational resources, financial stability, or institutional connections to build the kind of record Pearson has built. Most already psychiatrized in ways that sealed their accounts before they were fully articulated.


The Dungeon’s Purpose
The Dungeon is not random cruelty. It has a specific structural function: the management of intellectual production and political presence that threatens existing arrangements of power.
This is precise. It does not target the powerless who cannot threaten anything. It targets the credible— the writer with the National Book Award, the PhD with peer-reviewed publications, the whistleblower with specific documented knowledge. The objective is not elimination but discrediting and neutralization: to convert a credible observer into an apparently mentally ill person whose observations can be dismissed, and to do so while maintaining the operational and social environment of the Upstairs — the appearance of constitutional democracy, due process, and the rule of law.

Social death rather than physical death, because dead targets become martyrs. Discredited, isolated, apparently mentally ill former intellectuals become evidence that the system works— that people who make extreme claims about government targeting are the kind of people whose claims should not be taken seriously.
The Dungeon is specifically calibrated to not produce martyrs. It produces cautionary tales.



The Two Americas: The Structural Relationship
Pearson’s most important analytical contribution may be his insistence that the Dungeon and the Upstairs are not in contradiction. They are structurally related.
The Upstairs is real. Due process, judicial review, the ACLU, the First Amendment— these function for most Americans most of the time. The Dungeon does not negate them. It depends on them. The Upstairs provides the legitimacy that makes the Dungeon invisible. The demonstrable reality of constitutional governance for most people makes claims about its absence for some people seem implausible on their face.
The state secrets privilege does not prevent lawsuits. It terminates them. This is only possible because a legal system exists for it to terminate— a visible, functional legal system whose operation provides the cover under which the privilege performs its concealment function.
The psychiatric system does not deliberately participate in the Dungeon’s operations. It genuinely tries to help. Its participation is structural, not intentional: it applies the diagnostic frameworks it has, without the classified context that would make a different diagnosis available. The genuine functioning of mental health services is what makes the psychiatric trap operable.
The Statue of Liberty does not lie. People do flee to America from governments that conduct surveillance, noise campaigns, neurological assault, and systematic discrediting of dissidents. They are not wrong to do so. Most of them will never encounter the Dungeon. The America they find is largely the America the Statue promises.
But the Dungeon operates in the same country. With the same money. Under the same flag. The Dungeon’s population— tens of thousands at minimum— is distributed through the same cities and towns as the people for whom the Upstairs functions as advertised.
The structural relationship is this: the Dungeon requires the Upstairs. The Upstairs enables the Dungeon. They are not two Americas. They are one America operating at two levels simultaneously, with the upper level providing the social and institutional conditions under which the lower level can operate with maximum invisibility and minimum accountability.


What the Dungeon Cannot Fully Seal
Pearson identifies one mechanism the Dungeon’s architecture does not adequately address: documentation that exists in the world before any classification order can reach it, addressed to the public before any institutional gatekeeper can route it to dismissal.
Naylor sat in the Brooklyn Public Library— outside the device’s operational range, in a public institution built on her mother’s dream of intellectual access— and printed in block letters: I DIDN’T WANT TO TELL THIS STORY.
Pearson built his record across fourteen months with the methodological discipline of a PhD researcher who understood from the first incident that the documentation had to precede the litigation, that the aggregate pattern was the only argument that could survive the psychiatric seal, and that the only protection against an operation designed to produce plausible deniability was overwhelming systematic evidence in the public record.
Both are Black American intellectuals whose professional formation gave them exactly the tools the Dungeon’s operations were designed to destroy— and who used those tools to document what was being done to them with a precision and comprehensiveness that the Dungeon, by Pearson’s account, did not fully anticipate.
The Dungeon was designed for the person who falls apart. Who stops trusting their own perception. Who cannot maintain the methodological standards of systematic documentation under sustained psychological attack. Who eventually presents to a psychiatrist and receives the diagnosis that seals the record.
The Dungeon was not designed for Gloria Naylor in the library with a trembling hand and a yellow legal pad.
It was not designed for Richard Pearson with a PhD, a timestamp on every photograph, a preemptive mental health evaluation in the record, and a hundred-mechanism legal analysis that demonstrates— before filing any claim— exactly how every available judicial pathway has been architecturally sealed against him.
The question the Dungeon did not design for— Pearson states it directly at the end:

You know this now. What you do with the knowing is the only question the dungeon did not design for.





“My Hand Began to Shake”: Pearson’s Torture Parallels Gloria Naylor’s 2006 Interview and Book

The convergence across 30 years is precise enough to demand serious attention.

Here is a careful comparative analysis of the parallels between Gloria Naylor’s 1996 and Richard Pearson’s torture diary, A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance in 2025.


Parallels Between Gloria Naylor’s 1996 and Richard Pearson’s 2025 Torture Diary

Both accounts describe African American intellectuals— Naylor a celebrated novelist, Pearson a notable PhD— who believe they have become targets of a coordinated, government-linked surveillance and harassment campaign. The structural and experiential parallels between the two accounts are striking.
The Initiating Grievance and the Neighbor

In Naylor’s account, the entire operation is traceable to a petty neighborhood dispute with Eunice Simon, a woman who misrepresents Naylor to her brother, an NSA official, framing her as an anti-Semite and drug dealer. The complaint triggers escalating institutional machinery far beyond what the original grievance could rationally justify. In Pearson’s account, a man named “David Meyerowitz” appears unsolicited in Old Town Alexandria, name-dropping Trump and Kushner, and is interpreted as a psychological assessment operative— part of a cast engaged in a campaign against him. Later Pearson encounters several other figures. Both accounts involve several figures as apparent linchpins who either initiate or participate in the surveillance, and both authors wrestle uncomfortably but explicitly with what that pattern means, while simultaneously documenting what they believe they observed.



The Noise Campaign

One of the most concrete and specific parallels is the deliberate use of noise as psychological torture. In 1996, the NSA operatives— specifically the adolescent recruits called “The Boys”— rent the plantation house next to Naylor’s property and run sustained campaigns of hammering, chainsaws, and lawn mowing to drive her from her home. The explicit strategic rationale, as stated by field commander David Shane, is environmental conditioning: deprive the target of peace at home so that leaving becomes preferable to staying.
In Pearson’s diary, the upstairs neighbors follow him through his apartment room by room, dropping objects on the floor precisely when he moves— even when he relocates his mattress to the closet. He interprets this as deliberate, location-aware psychological torment designed to deny him sleep and mental rest. Both accounts describe noise not as incidental annoyance but as a coordinated operational tool.
Drive-By Vehicle Surveillance

Naylor describes an escalating pattern of cars circling her rural St. Helena road— three or four per hour in a dead-end lane— in a formation designed to “contain the objective.” When she moves to Brooklyn, the operation continues with car doors slamming rhythmically outside her study window, and cars following her whenever she leaves her apartment. Pearson similarly documents cars taking off from parking spaces the moment he passes, tailing him through city streets. He sustains a red-and-black campaign, noting in excoriating detail. Both authors attempt to record license plate numbers, and both describe the formation as something beyond

coincidence— recognizable as military-style containment. As a researcher, Pearson conducts control studies.
Home Intrusion and Tampering
