The Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) of 1946 represents Congress’s partial consent to suit. But that consent is riddled with exceptions that operate as categorical immunities for precisely the conduct most likely to constitute government abuse. The intentional tort exception bars recovery for assault, battery, and intentional infliction of emotional distress committed by federal law enforcement— the very torts that constitute the core of a torture claim. The discretionary function exception bars claims arising from government conduct involving “judgment or choice”— and the decision to deploy directed energy against a domestic target is, as the legal analysis of the Pearson case notes, precisely the kind of discretionary policy decision the exception was designed to protect.
The result is that the government has consented to be sued for negligently maintaining a slippery floor in a federal post office, but has not consented to be sued for intentionally torturing a citizen. The consent structure is not neutral. It was constructed to leave the most serious abuses of executive power— the ones involving deliberate policy decisions by officials exercising judgment— outside the reach of civil accountability.
Pearson’s document addresses this directly: “Sovereign immunity is that generally people cannot sue the US government for damages due to constitutional or civil rights violations except where it has agreed to allow tort claims, which is only in limited circumstances.” He notes the Supreme Court’s cramped definition of false imprisonment— requiring that the person be prevented from leaving in all directions— with the observation that this “lacks any consideration of the emotional and financial determinants of imposed geographic and emotional prison.” A person can be imprisoned in a city, in a life, in a financial condition, and the law will not recognize it as imprisonment unless the walls are physical and total. The law protects against the least sophisticated forms of the harm. The sophisticated forms are unaddressed.
———

Bivens and Its Dismantlement: The Disappearing Cause of Action

In 1971, the Supreme Court recognized in Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents of Federal Bureau of Narcotics that a person whose Fourth Amendment rights had been violated by federal officers had an implied right to sue those officers for damages, even absent a specific statutory authorization. Bivens was the federal analog to Section 1983 — the mechanism by which citizens could hold individual federal officers personally accountable for constitutional violations.
The Supreme Court has spent the fifty years since Bivens systematically dismantling it. In Ziglar
v. Abbasi (2017), the Court held that extending Bivens to any “new context”— a case materially different from the three contexts in which the Court had previously applied it— requires asking whether “special factors counsel hesitation.” In cases involving national security, the Court held, those special factors virtually always counsel hesitation. The executive’s need for operational flexibility in matters of national security, the Court reasoned, means that courts should not create new causes of action that could constrain that flexibility.
The practical consequence: the use of technologies that did not exist in 1971 — directed energy weapons, remote neural modulation, voice-to-skull transmission— against American citizens produces no Bivens cause of action, because it is a “new context” to which no prior Bivens decision applies. The constitutional violation may be real. The harm may be documented. The perpetrators may be identified. But the legal mechanism for personal liability does not exist, because the Court has declined to create it for precisely the class of national security misconduct most likely to occur with the most advanced tools.
As the Pearson legal framework states: “Because lower courts are unable to expand new contexts of a Bivens cause of action (neuroweapons would be a new context), Pearson has no cause of action against the individual federal agents responsible— the legal mechanism for personal liability does not exist.” The agents who operated the program, if a program exists, are, as a structural matter, beyond the reach of civil liability. Not because they are immune. Because the remedy was never created.
———

Qualified Immunity: The Standard That Cannot Be Met

For the claims where Bivens still theoretically applies, qualified immunity provides a second layer of protection. Under Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982) and its progeny, individual government officials are immune from civil liability unless they violated a “clearly established” constitutional right— meaning a right so clearly established that “a reasonable person would have known” the conduct was unconstitutional.
The “clearly established” standard has been interpreted, in practice, to require that a prior court have addressed the specific conduct in question at a level of specificity sufficient to put a reasonable officer on notice. It is not enough that the general constitutional principle is established. There must be a prior case involving substantially the same conduct. This creates a self-reinforcing immunity: if a technology or technique is novel, there is no prior case. If there is

no prior case, the right is not clearly established. If the right is not clearly established, the officer is immune. If the officer is immune, no case is decided on the merits. And if no case is decided on the merits, no right is established. The technology must be used at least once— and the victim must survive, document, and litigate the case to a merits judgment— before the second victim has any recourse.
Applied to neuroweapons, directed energy behavioral control, and voice-to-skull technology: no prior case has addressed these methods. No right has been clearly established. No officer who deployed them is personally liable. The first deployment is effectively immune regardless of its constitutionality. The immunity does not depend on whether the conduct was lawful. It depends on whether the court, on its first encounter with the question, agrees that the law was clear enough to have given the officers notice. Given the technological novelty and the classified nature of the programs, courts presented with these claims for the first time will almost certainly find the law insufficiently clear.
———

The Glomar Response and the FOIA Dead End: The Right to Know Nothing

The Freedom of Information Act represents Congress’s partial commitment to governmental transparency— the citizen’s right to know what their government is doing. The CIA’s Glomar response, approved by the D.C. Circuit in Phillippi v. CIA (1977), represents the intelligence community’s response to that commitment: the agency will “neither confirm nor deny” the existence of the requested documents or program. This response was invented specifically to avoid the disclosure obligation FOIA imposes, while avoiding the false statement of saying the documents do not exist when they do.
The Glomar response is now routinely applied to intelligence programs. It is not necessary to prove that the documents are classified, or that their disclosure would harm national security. The agency asserts that even confirming or denying the existence of the documents would harm national security. The court defers. The FOIA request produces nothing. The documentary record supporting the plaintiff’s allegations cannot be assembled through the public-access mechanism that Congress created specifically to allow it.
The circularity is by design. The agency knows what it does. The plaintiff cannot discover it without documents. The documents are withheld. The plaintiff cannot prove the program exists. The case fails for insufficient evidence. The program continues. The documentation that would have made the case was always in the government’s possession and is never produced.
———

The FISA Authorization: Judicial Blessing for the Unexamined

The Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court— established by the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 in response to the COINTELPRO revelations— was designed as a check on domestic intelligence surveillance. Before surveilling an American citizen, the

intelligence community was required to obtain judicial authorization. The FISA Court would provide independent review. The safeguard would prevent abuse.
What the FISA Court became, as revealed in the Snowden disclosures and subsequent reporting, was a rubber stamp. The Court operates entirely in secret, hears only the government’s presentation, and approves the vast majority of the applications it receives. Its opinions are classified. Its orders are classified. The persons being surveilled do not know they are being surveilled and cannot contest the surveillance. There is no adversarial proceeding.
The judge who signs the order has access to no independent investigation and no opposing argument.
The legal consequence for a plaintiff: if the government can produce a FISA order authorizing surveillance of Pearson, that order carries a presumption of legality. The individuals operating under it are entitled to good-faith reliance on the judicial authorization. Individual liability is eliminated even if the order was later found to have been overbroad. The judiciary’s blessing— obtained in secret, ex parte, without the subject’s knowledge or participation— immunizes the conduct that the blessing authorized. The constitutional safeguard of judicial review has been converted into a mechanism for pre-immunizing government misconduct from civil accountability.
———

The Covert Action Finding and the Totten Bar: The Case That Cannot Be Named

The final doctrinal layer operates at the threshold itself. Under Tenet v. Doe (2005), invoking the earlier principle of Totten v. United States (1875), courts will not entertain suits that would require acknowledging the existence of a covert arrangement between the government and a private party— or, by extension, between the government and a covert program. Unlike the state secrets privilege, which might permit a case to proceed if the secret evidence can be excluded, the Totten-Tenet doctrine is a threshold dismissal: the case is dismissed because its subject matter is inherently secret, not because specific pieces of evidence are classified.
If the program targeting Pearson was authorized by a covert action finding signed by the President under 50 U.S.C. § 3093 — the statutory mechanism for presidential authorization of covert operations— then the court cannot hear a case that would require acknowledging the program’s existence. Not because the evidence is classified. Because the existence of the program is itself a state secret, and a court that acknowledges it in a published opinion has disclosed the secret.
The Totten bar is, constitutionally speaking, the most extraordinary of the doctrines surveyed here. It holds that certain presidential actions are simply beyond judicial review— not because the courts lack power, but because the subject matter of review is a category the courts have designated unreviewable. The President signs a finding. The program exists. The program operates. A citizen is harmed. The citizen sues. The court dismisses because the program

cannot be acknowledged. The harm is real. The remedy is unavailable. The President’s action is, as a practical matter, unreviewable by any branch.
———

The Procedural Gauntlet: One Hundred Ways to Lose Before the Merits

Assume, for the sake of argument, that a plaintiff survives the threshold doctrines. The state secrets privilege was not invoked. The Glomar response was challenged successfully. The Bivens context was extended by an unusually willing district court. Qualified immunity was denied at the pleading stage. The plaintiff has stated a claim and the case proceeds.
The procedural architecture of complex national security litigation then imposes its own attrition. Interlocutory appeals on qualified immunity, which are immediately appealable under Mitchell v. Forsyth (1985), stay the case for years while the immunity question is resolved at the circuit level. Government discovery responses produce thousands of pages of unresponsive documents while core materials are withheld under classification claims. Expert witnesses for the plaintiff are excluded under Daubert for methodologies that courts, on first encounter with the technology, cannot evaluate. Summary judgment is granted on the basis of a classified ex parte government declaration that the plaintiff never sees and cannot contest. Trial, if reached, occurs three to five years after filing, with a jury pool drawn from a jurisdiction heavily populated by federal employees and government contractors.
The asymmetry of resources is structural, not incidental. The government has unlimited resources to litigate. The plaintiff— who has, in Pearson’s case, been financially destroyed by the operation they are litigating— does not. Delay serves the government. Financial attrition serves the government. The complexity of the legal questions serves the government. Every motion practice advantage, every protective order, every scheduling delay is, in practical terms, a mechanism for achieving through procedural exhaustion what cannot always be achieved through substantive law alone.
The Pearson legal framework catalogs one hundred such mechanisms, ranked by finality and probability of case termination. They do not all operate simultaneously. But they operate cumulatively, sequentially, and in layers— each designed to end the case before the next is needed. A plaintiff who defeats the threshold doctrines encounters the pleading standards. A plaintiff who defeats the pleading standards encounters the discovery barriers. A plaintiff who defeats the discovery barriers encounters the expert exclusion rules. A plaintiff who defeats the expert exclusion rules encounters the summary judgment stage. And so on. The architecture is not a gauntlet with a finish line. It is a gauntlet designed to ensure that no one reaches one.
———

The Hermetic Seal: What the Architecture Achieves

Viewed in aggregate, the legal framework applicable to the Pearson case does not describe a system that tries to do justice and sometimes fails. It describes a system architecturally

designed to produce a specific outcome: the unreviewability of government conduct in the national security domain, and the immunity of individual officers who operate within it, regardless of the constitutional character of what they do.
The state secrets privilege insulates the program at step one. The frivolity and plausibility doctrines insulate it at step two. Sovereign immunity insulates the institution. Bivens’ dismantlement eliminates the individual remedy. Qualified immunity shields the individual officers. The FISA authorization pre-immunizes the surveillance. The Totten bar forecloses acknowledgment. The procedural architecture depletes the plaintiff.
As the Pearson legal analysis observes at Entry 22: “So the government could theoretically create a secret program that targets minority scholars like Richard Pearson and uses exotic technology like voice-to-skull and behavioral steering, THEN invoke state secrets privilege. A hermetic seal to lock in injustice.” The word “theoretically” is doing more work than the sentence acknowledges. The seal is not theoretical. It is operational. It is composed of real doctrines decided in real cases, applicable today, in federal courts across the country, to any plaintiff who alleges that the national security state harmed them.
The final entry in the legal framework— Entry 100 — describes the limit case of success: Pearson wins at every level, district court to Supreme Court, and is awarded damages and injunctive relief. The government complies with the injunction by terminating the specific program targeting Pearson. The broader directed energy infrastructure, behavioral direction capability, and AI orchestration platform remains intact, deployed against others, and now confirmed operational by the judgment itself. Even total legal victory, at the outer boundary of the conceivable, produces only personal remedy. The system continues. The architecture survives its own exposure.
———

The Reynolds Lesson: The Document That Was Always There

Return, at the close of this analysis, to Reynolds. The government asserted state secrets in 1953 to prevent examination of an accident report. The Court deferred. The families of the dead civilians who brought the case received nothing. Forty years later, the documents were declassified. They contained evidence of negligence, not secrets. The government had lied to the Supreme Court. The lie had worked. The Court did not revisit its ruling.
The Reynolds story is not a story about a system that was abused and then corrected. It is a story about a system that was designed with a vulnerability— the court’s inability to examine the very evidence it was deferring about— and that the government exploited that vulnerability the first time it had occasion to do so, and that the exploitation succeeded, and that the Court, when the exploitation was exposed, found no mechanism or inclination to respond. The doctrine stands. The fraud stands. The families received nothing.
Reynolds is not an outlier. It is the template. It illustrates the operational relationship between the legal architecture and the executive’s interest in concealing its conduct: the architecture

provides the mechanism, the executive exploits the mechanism, the courts defer to the exploitation, the truth is declassified decades later (if at all), and no one is accountable because the statute of limitations has long since run and the perpetrators are long since dead.
What changes between 1953 and 2025 is not the architecture. What changes is the technology available to the executive whose conduct the architecture conceals. The state secrets privilege that protected negligence in 1953 now protects, if the Pearson analysis is correct, directed energy behavioral control, hippocampal destination encoding, and remote neurological torture. The doctrine is the same. The conduct it shields has become, in the intervening decades, vastly more sophisticated and vastly more invasive.
———

What Rights Actually Are

The American Bill of Rights was not written as a self-executing guarantee. It was written as a set of instructions to the judiciary— instructions that the judiciary is required, in most circumstances, to follow. What this analysis of the Pearson legal framework demonstrates is that the judiciary has, over the course of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, built a parallel architecture of doctrines that systematically exempts the national security state from those instructions. The result is not that the rights do not exist. They exist in the text. They exist in the civic mythology. They exist in the law school classroom and the constitutional law treatise.
What they do not do, in the domain of national security, is constrain. A right that cannot be enforced against the actor most likely to violate it is a right in name only. The Fourth Amendment prohibition on unreasonable searches and seizures does not constrain a surveillance program authorized by a secret court on the basis of a classified standard that the surveilled person cannot contest. The Fifth Amendment protection against deprivation of liberty without due process does not constrain a behavioral direction operation that leaves no physical trace and can be attributed to voluntary choice. The First Amendment protection of speech does not constrain a targeting apparatus that identifies dissidents from their published scholarship and subjects them to operations designed to silence them not through censorship but through the obliteration of their capacity to speak.
The rights are real. The violations are real. The architecture that stands between the rights and the remedies is also real— and it is, as this analysis demonstrates, comprehensive, interlocking, judicially constructed, and designed to produce exactly the outcome it produces: a government that can do to Richard Pearson what the documented record indicates was done to him, in the capital of the country that calls itself the world’s leading democracy, and face no legal consequence, because the law it built around itself ensures that it will not.
This is not a counsel of despair. It is a description of a problem. Problems that are named can be addressed. The civil rights movement named the problem of state-sanctioned racial terror and built the political and legal pressure that produced, however imperfectly, a legal response. The COINTELPRO exposure— achieved not through litigation but through a physical break-in at

an FBI field office— produced congressional investigations, Senate Select Committee hearings, and a partial reform of intelligence oversight. The Snowden disclosures produced, ultimately, legislative changes to the bulk collection programs.
What each of these episodes required was not the success of a legal claim in federal court. It was the exposure of the conduct to a public that, when it learned what had been done in its name, found the conduct unacceptable. The law, in each case, followed public accountability rather than producing it. The architecture described in this essay is the government’s answer to that lesson: a system that prevents the exposure by preventing the litigation, classifying the evidence, dismissing the complaint, and shielding the actors before the public ever has occasion to form a view.
Richard Pearson has spent fourteen months building a record specifically designed to defeat that architecture through the mechanism the architecture cannot seal: documentation that exists before any litigation begins, published in public before any classification order can reach it, built by a researcher who understood from the beginning that the document had to exist in the world before anyone with the power to respond would respond. The legal architecture is designed for the case that files first and discovers later. He documented first. Whether that sequence changes anything remains, at this writing, unanswered. But the record exists. And the question the record poses— whether the country described in the Constitution and the country described in these one hundred legal doctrines are the same country— does not have a comfortable answer.
---



If Dr. Richard Pearson’s Torture in Washington, DC, is Scaled, Vast Segments of Population Harmed
Dr. Pearson’s nightmare likely suggests scaled technology of mass manipulation and simulated reality - moving people without their conscious choice, causing them to perform actions, and voice-to-skull.
This essay explores a hypothetical, but the blurred lines between reality and speculation are unknown without the government admitting that it is running a program arguably worse than slavery— at least enslaved people knew that they were enslaved. What happened to Dr.
Pearson can be scaled and is being scaled. Scaled for whom? To what extent? That is unknown. This essay takes an educated guess.

If what was done to Richard Pearson is being done to Black Washington


Richard Pearson is one man. He documented fourteen months of what a government campaign looks like from the inside— the surveillance, the directed energy, the behavioral direction, the street theater, the institutional closure, the physical assault, the framing operation, the methodical destruction of every condition that made his life in Washington viable.
He documented it because he is a trained researcher. He recognized what was happening because he had the analytical framework to recognize it. He survived it intact— intellectually, documentarily— because he applied to his own experience the same rigor he would apply to any research question.
Most people are not Dr. Richard Pearson.

Most people do not have a doctorate in research. They do not run probability calculations on the color of strangers’ clothing. They do not conduct controlled experiments in their hats. They do not recognize the microwave auditory effect from the inside of their own skull and correctly identify its mechanism six months before the government confirms the technology exists. Dr.
Pearson heard a voice in his head and found out that the same technology— causing a single person to hear a voice that others around the cannot hear— is used in museums.
Most people, experiencing what Pearson experienced, would have no framework for what was happening to them. They would have no methodology for documenting it. They would have no language for describing it that did not sound, to every person they told, like the description of a person losing their mind.
That is what scaled looks like.

Not one documented researcher holding the line of his own sanity through fourteen months of methodological rigor. A segment of the three hundred thousand people— disproportionately, Black residents of Washington, D.C.— experiencing the same apparatus applied at lower individual intensity, higher aggregate reach, with no Richard Pearson among them to name what is happening.
This essay is about what that looks like. In the streets. In the homes. In the bodies. In the institutions. In the slow, invisible, individually inexplicable accumulation of conditions that produces, from a distance of years, a demographic transformation no single person within it ever experienced as anything other than their own life.




In the Body
Pearson’s body was used against him in specific, documented ways. His skull became a receiver. His hippocampus became a navigation instrument. His face became a target. His neurological threshold was deliberately lowered to make him more susceptible to subsequent directed energy deployment.
At scale, none of this looks like what it looks like in Pearson’s account. There is no single incident of voice-to-skull transmission that a person can describe with clinical precision. There is no tub under a tree waiting with a framing payload. There is no moment of recognized attack.
There is instead a population-level pattern of neurological disruption that presents, in individual clinical encounters across the city, as the ordinary pathology of urban Black life in America.
A 47-year-old man in Ward 7 has been having headaches for eight months. They began, though he does not know this, at the same time the directed energy infrastructure in his neighborhood was recalibrated. His doctor attributes them to hypertension— already elevated, already undertreated, already the disease that kills Black men in Washington at rates that would constitute a public health emergency if anyone were tracking the cause rather than the outcome. He is given medication. He continues having headaches. He does not connect the

headaches to the specific streets where they intensify and the specific streets where they ease. He has no reason to make that connection.
A 34-year-old woman in Anacostia has been unable to sleep for six months. Not the insomnia of anxiety, which she knows from before. Something different— a jangling wakefulness that arrives at the same hour each night and does not respond to anything she tries. Her primary care physician, seeing her fourth time in a year, refers her to a psychiatrist. The psychiatrist diagnoses generalized anxiety disorder. The medication helps a little. The sleeplessness continues. She does not know that pulsed electromagnetic fields in the frequency ranges documented by Havana Syndrome researchers produce sleep disruption as a primary symptom. She has no reason to know this.
A 16-year-old boy in Congress Heights is failing three classes. His teachers describe him as distracted, unfocused, unable to sustain attention. His mother has taken him to two doctors. Both have suggested ADHD. The medication tried has not helped. What his teachers and doctors are observing is the cognitive disruption produced by chronic low-level directed energy exposure— measurable in the white matter microstructural changes that diffusion tensor imaging identifies in Havana Syndrome patients, invisible to every other clinical instrument available in a standard workup. His school has referred him for special education evaluation. His mother is exhausted and frightened and has been thinking, for several months now, that maybe it would be better to move somewhere else. She does not know why she keeps thinking this.
She attributes it to stress.

These are not three people. These are three representative instances of a pattern running across a population of 300,000 — producing, in aggregate, elevated rates of neurological complaint, sleep disorder, cognitive disruption, and treatment-resistant symptoms that every clinical framework available to Washington’s medical system will attribute to the
well-documented health burdens of structural racism, poverty, and stress.

The diagnoses are not wrong. The causes they miss are not visible to the instruments being used.
The population is sick. The sickness has a source. The source is classified.


In the Mind
Pearson documented being moved. Twice, on November 7, 2025, through impulses he experienced as his own— to a park, then to a specific spot under a tree. He recognized what had happened only in retrospect, because he had spent months building the analytical framework to recognize it.
At scale, no one has that framework.

What the population of Black Washington experiences is not recognized behavioral direction. It is the ordinary texture of urban life beginning to feel, in ways no one can articulate precisely, subtly wrong.
A couple in Ward 8 — both employed, both stable, both with deep roots in the neighborhood— have been having the same conversation for four months. It always starts differently and always arrives at the same place: maybe it’s time to think about moving. They cannot say exactly what has changed. The neighborhood seems fine. Their jobs are fine. Their children’s school is fine. And yet there is a pull— experienced by both of them independently, discussed between them as evidence that it must be real— toward elsewhere. Toward Atlanta, where her sister is. Toward Charlotte, where he grew up. Toward anywhere that is not here, though neither of them can explain why here has started to feel wrong.
They are not being moved to a specific location the way Pearson was moved to the park. The operational objective at population scale is not precision targeting. It is directional drift— the sustained low-intensity activation of the hippocampal circuits that produce spatial drives, calibrated not to a specific destination but away from a specific origin. Not: go there. But: do not stay here. The impulse is vaguer, lower intensity, more easily absorbed into ordinary life narrative. It feels less like a pull than like the slow fading of a reason to stay.
It is indistinguishable from what people in gentrifying neighborhoods across America describe as the feeling of being priced out— except that it is running on people who are not yet priced out, whose financial situation has not yet changed, whose neighborhood has not yet changed, whose lives are intact. The feeling precedes the conditions that would ordinarily produce it. It is the feeling itself that will produce the conditions— because feeling that it is time to go is the first step toward making it time to go.
Across the city, in hundreds of thousands of individual minds, the same low-intensity signal is running. Each person experiences it as their own thought. Each person’s thought is slightly different in its specific content— different reasons, different destinations, different timelines. The aggregate is a population drifting, individually and by its own free will, toward departure.
No one knows this is happening. There is no clinical instrument that detects it. There is no moment of recognized coercion. There is only a city full of people who have all, independently, started to think it might be time to leave— and who, when they discuss this with their neighbors, find that their neighbors have been thinking the same thing.


In the Street
Pearson documented the calling card pattern— bright solid red paired with solid black, deployed on his regular walking routes in densities his probability calculations showed were statistically impossible by chance. He documented control studies. He documented the pattern’s absence in neutral environments and its presence specifically on predictable routes at predictable times. He

documented the expressionless people: the white-haired woman at the Compass Real Estate doorway, the man at Eastern Market standing two feet from him with blank affect, the young man at the library exit with no bag, no phone, no microexpressions.
He documented scripted encounters: the man on the Virginia trail who said “oh, there you are” without breaking stride; the woman who quoted his private apartment speech back to him on a public sidewalk; the stranger in Safeway wearing his exact hat.
At scale, none of this is identifiable as a pattern by anyone experiencing it.

What it looks like, distributed across 300,000 people across the historically Black neighborhoods of Washington, is: an uncomfortable feeling, impossible to pin down, that public space has become subtly hostile. Not dangerous. Not threatening in any way you could report to police. Just— off. Watched. The specific feeling, familiar to Black Americans across generations, of being somewhere you are not entirely welcome, produced now not by individual human hostility but by a coordinated environmental manipulation that uses human beings— some conscious, some not, some directed by the same technology being used on the population— as instruments of ambient threat.
The woman in Ward 7 who starts taking a different route to the Metro because her usual route has started to feel wrong cannot explain what is wrong with it. Nothing has happened. Nobody has said anything. There is simply a feeling of wrongness that she avoids, the way you avoid a street that smells like something you cannot identify. She takes the longer route. She does not think about why. It adds eight minutes to her commute. She does not mention it to anyone.
The man in Congress Heights who stops going to the park where he has been taking his daughter on Saturdays for three years cannot say what changed. Nothing specific happened. The park is fine. But something in him does not want to be there anymore. They start going to the park in Maryland, across the border, where the feeling is not present. It is a longer drive. He does not think much about why he prefers it. He just does.
Multiplied across a population, these individual adjustments— each one rational, each one self-protective, each one producing no complaint because nothing specific
happened— constitute the systematic withdrawal of a community from its own public space. The parks empty. The streets become less familiar. The neighborhood’s texture changes not because its demographics have changed yet, but because its current residents have started to inhabit it differently— more cautiously, less fully, with a growing background sense that this place is not entirely theirs anymore.
This is the precondition for departure, manufactured before any economic or physical pressure that would ordinarily produce it. The community is being separated from its own geography before it is displaced from it. By the time the displacement happens, it will feel like the completion of a process that was already well underway— because it will be.


In the Home
Pearson’s home was penetrated. His private speech was monitored and fed to people positioned on his routes. The noise from the unit above tracked him from room to room. The power button from his panini grill was removed and placed in his laundry pile. A bottle he owned was taken and repositioned at a public event. The home was a site of constant, intimate violation— its privacy destroyed, its contents accessible, its resident’s most private moments available to the operation running against him.
At scale, the home surveillance does not look like what it looked like for Pearson— intimate, personalized, responsive to his specific movements and speech. It does not need to. At population scale, the objective of home surveillance is not only to know what each person is saying. It is to produce, in each person, the specific feeling Pearson documented: that nothing is private. That the home is not a refuge. That whatever you say and do within your own walls is available to something outside those walls.
This feeling does not require that anyone hear every conversation, but it does that anyway— from cellphones, strangers’ “smart” glasses that record, to home devices that pick up conversation, to everyday outdoor surveillance. It requires also the occasional, perfectly placed confirmation— the stranger who mentions something you discussed in private, the subtle evidence of access, the sense that specific things you did when you thought you were alone have been registered somewhere— to produce a generalized, permanent background anxiety about the home as a safe space.
For Pearson this confirmation came dramatically: a stranger quoting his exact words from a private apartment conversation, on a public sidewalk, without breaking stride. At scale it is quieter. A neighbor mentions something in passing that you are certain you only discussed inside. A conversation you had with your spouse last night seems to have somehow reached someone it shouldn’t have. You cannot say how. You cannot prove anything. You dismiss it. But the feeling that the home is not entirely private settles in, below the level of active thought, and stays.
The home that does not feel private is a home you want to leave. Not consciously. Not because you have decided it is unsafe. Because the specific psychological function of home— sanctuary, refuge, the place where you are not watched— has been compromised, and its compromise registers as a low-level, persistent discomfort that makes the fantasy of elsewhere feel like relief.
Three hundred thousand homes, each one compromised in this way, produce three hundred thousand people for whom leaving has begun to feel not like loss but like freedom.


In the Institutions

Pearson went to every door available to him. Congressional offices. The D.C. Council. The ACLU. Journalists. He attempted asylum in Iceland.
Because each institution, operating within its own framework, encountered the specific obstacle that the legal and classification architecture had placed in its path. Congress could not investigate a classified program without triggering the classification laws that would immediately suppress what it found. The courts could not grant standing to a plaintiff who could not prove harm from a program whose existence the government would neither confirm nor deny. Lawyers could not take a case with no discoverable evidence. Journalists could not publish a story whose central claims could not be confirmed by any official source. The ACLU cited limited resources— resources that are always limited, and always most limited precisely when the cases most requiring them arrive.
Each door closed for its own reason. The aggregate is a person with no recourse.

At scale, this is the daily experience of Black Washington in 2025 and 2026 — not in the dramatic form of a single person seeking asylum and being turned back, but in the accumulated small closures of ordinary civic life. The constituent who calls their council member’s office about the strange headaches that started when the new federal equipment appeared on their block and is told this is not something the council handles. The parent who reports to their child’s school that something seems wrong and cannot get a referral to anyone who can identify what. The resident who describes to their doctor symptoms that the doctor recognizes as consistent with stress and treats accordingly. The community organizer who tries to document the pattern and finds that each individual instance is explicable and the aggregate is invisible to every institution with the authority to act on it.
The institutions are not conspiring against these people. They are functioning exactly as they were designed to function— which is to say, they are functioning within a framework that was designed without reference to the possibility that the government would deploy directed energy and behavioral direction technology against its own civilian population. The framework has no provision for this because the framework was built before this existed. Its absence from the framework is not an oversight. It was engineered by the same administration that built the capability, which understood that the capability would only remain operational if the framework for stopping it remained absent.
The result is a population that is experiencing real, measurable harm— neurological, psychological, physical, social— and encountering, at every turn, institutions that are genuinely unable to help. Not unwilling. Unable. The framework does not contain the tools required. And the people who experience this inability as a kind of abandonment are correct to experience it that way, even though the individuals they encounter at each institution are operating in good faith within the constraints of what their institution can do.
A population that experiences the systematic failure of every institution it turns to— for health, for safety, for political representation, for legal protection, for media coverage— is a population that has learned, correctly, that it is on its own.

People who are on their own, in a place that is making them sick and surveilled and subtly dispossessed of their public space and their private sanctuaries, do not need to be directed to leave.
They make their own decision.


In the Aggregate
Zoom out far enough and what scaled looks like is this:

A city that has been, for a century, the specific destination of the Great Migration— the place Black Americans came to when they needed to be somewhere that was not the South— beginning, in the mid-2020s, to empty of the community that made it what it is.
The historians who eventually examine that data will reach for the explanatory frameworks available to them: gentrification, racial disparities, economic pressure, the Trump administration’s rollback of policies supporting Black communities. These explanations are real. They will be accurate as far as they go. The data they explain will confirm them.
What the data will not show— what the mechanism is specifically designed to ensure the data will not show— is the directed energy infrastructure running in Ward 7 and Ward 8. The behavioral direction technology operating below the threshold of conscious detection. The home surveillance producing the specific feeling of private speech being available to strangers. The street-level environmental manipulation manufacturing the withdrawal of a community from its own public space. The institutional architecture ensuring that every door a person goes to closes, not by conspiracy, but by design.
The data will show that Black Washington emptied. It will not show why.
That is what scaled looks like from the outside.


From the inside, it looks like ordinary life— slightly wrong in ways no one can name, slightly hostile in ways nothing has specifically done, slightly exhausting in ways that have no identifiable cause. The headache that follows you on certain streets. The sleeplessness that arrives at the same hour. The feeling that the home is not entirely yours. The pull toward elsewhere that never quite goes away. The institutions that are genuinely trying to help and genuinely cannot. The neighborhood that is fine, technically, on every measurable axis, and yet produces, in everyone who lives there, the growing background sense that it might be time to go.

Each person experiencing this is experiencing only their own life. Their own body. Their own thoughts. Their own decisions.
Pearson is the only one who documented what produces it. He is one man.
He documented one test.

This is the scale— if it not stopped.


Based on the documented account of Richard Pearson, PhD, Washington, D.C., 2025–2026. Full documentation available at criticalraceframework.com. Population figures: U.S. Census Bureau. Infrastructure documentation: Palantir ImmigrationOS contract, April 2025; DARPA N3 program documentation; Department of War CTO statement, January 2026; National Academies of Sciences, Havana Syndrome assessment, 2020; Anthropic statement, February 27, 2026.




Is a Mass Exodus of African Americans from Washington, DC the Next ‘Great Migration’?
When two white millennials referenced the “Great Migration” in conversation, Dr. Richard Pearson’s ears perked.
On August 10, 2025, Richard Pearson was walking along Pennsylvania Avenue near the US Capitol. A young white millennial couple passed him. Their conversation caught his attention.
White male (30s): “So what do you think is going to happen?

White female (30s): “There was the Great Migration.”

Four words, emphasis on was. She was not making a historical observation. She was answering her companion’s question about what is going to happen, and it resembled the Great Migration.
Her coded answer itself was revealing, even if only by inference. What could cause another “Great Migration” of African Americans? The original Great Migration was the mass movement of over six million African Americans from the rural South to cities in the North, Midwest, and West between 1910 and 1970. They fled white oppression, lynchings, state

terrorism, and second-class citizenship. Why would that be relevant in 2025? The couple knew but didn’t say.
This essay attempts to find out. It determines what can be inferred from her analogy. Whatever it is, it has to have a racial component and involve a form of mass oppression or injury.
This essay discusses the experiences of Richard Pearson— the man who was listening. He was no ordinary person. He was a PhD scholar whose life has been turned upside down. He was living in a nightmare of torture, manipulation, and two assaults.
Was his experience scaled? Was the US military technology that brought Pearson to locations or put a voice in his head scaled across the Black population in Washington, DC?
“Yes,” implied the white female in August 2025.

Racial — The US government targeted Pearson, an African American PhD. The Trump administration’s animosity toward African Americans played across executive orders, political rhetoric, agency policies, and criminal prosecutions. By employing the Great Migration, she was saying what it was racial in nature.
Structural oppression — Pearson encountered a multi-level set of threats beginning in 2025 — physical assault, chemical assault, psychological and tech-mediated torture, auditory “clicks” similar to the Havana Syndrome, stalking, and harassment. By employing the Great Migration, she was saying that it was at least as structurally and intimately oppressive as Jim Crow and white terrorism in the US South.
Displacement — Pearson attempted to flee to the Europe Union but was sent back. Millions fled the US South. By employing the Great Migration, she was saying that it could trigger a mass exodus.


The Criteria
The Great Migration had a specific structure. Invoking it as a frame is not casual. It implies seven precise conditions.
One: A racial component. The Great Migration was exclusively about Black Americans. Any invocation without Blackness at its center breaks the analogy entirely.
Two: State-backed structural oppression. The Migration was not produced by individual bad actors. It was produced by a system— law enforcement, courts, employers, landlords, government— operating in coordinated, mutually reinforcing ways backed by the full authority of the state.
Three: Displacement— flight from severe, state-inflicted violation. People left because staying meant bodily destruction. Not inconvenience. Not discomfort. The violation of their

physical safety, their bodily autonomy, their right to exist in their own bodies without state interference. The flight was rational, urgent, and desperate.
Four: Population-scale magnitude. The Migration affected millions simultaneously. The reference sets a numerical threshold that cannot be met by individual or small-group targeting.
Five: A catalyst— public exposure. The Migration accelerated around specific moments of disclosed atrocity that reached mass public awareness. Not private knowledge. Not rumor.
Public. Media. Undeniable.

Six: Permanent demographic transformation. The Migration did not reverse. It permanently restructured the racial geography of the United States.
Seven: Generational and institutional consequence. The Migration’s effects compounded across generations— reshaping cities, politics, culture, and institutions for a century.



Richard Pearson
Before measuring, you need to understand who Pearson is and what he documented.

He is an African American who holds a doctorate. His dissertation on racial equity frameworks is published. He has no criminal record. Two separate D.C. Department of Behavioral Health evaluations— by named clinicians, on record— found no psychiatric symptoms requiring intervention.
Several months after January 20, 2025 — the day the Trump administration signed executive orders designating DEI scholarship and racial equity frameworks as hostile ideology— he documented fourteen months of a government campaign against him with the discipline of a trained researcher. Timestamped photographs. Probability calculations. Control studies. Named witnesses. Preserved physical evidence. Controlled experiments.
What he documented:

On July 13, 2025, near the Capitol, he heard a woman’s voice originating inside his skull— not from a direction, not at his ear. The voice carried the ambient acoustic environment of the room transmitting it: other people present, microphone quality, the texture of a live feed. He heard the room the operators were sitting in. A hallucination cannot carry the background noise of a remote location. The U.S. Department of War confirmed directed energy weapons were operational and being scaled in January 2026 — six months after Pearson identified the mechanism from the inside of his own skull.
Related Reading: Projecting a Voice in Someone’s Head is Common Technology: Museums Use It
On November 7, 2025, he felt a pull— experienced as ordinary curiosity— toward a park near the Capitol. When he tried to leave, a second impulse redirected him to a specific spot under a tree he had not yet visited. A plastic storage tub was waiting: a red hat, a black shirt, a notebook referencing Adolf Hitler and a fictitious “European Removal Court,” two identical photographs of a blond man. A framing operation, waiting for his fingerprints. He photographed everything without touching anything. He returned six days later on video. The notebook and photographs were gone. The red hat and black shirt remained. Someone had monitored his visit, assessed that the framing attempt failed, returned to recover the incriminating materials, and left the calling card as a signature.
Beginning January 2026, geographic auditory clicks appeared at specific intersections in Southwest Washington— replicable by returning, absent elsewhere. On February 20, he placed a metal-cased phone and a battery in his knitted cap. No clicks. He had been getting them consistently for weeks. Metal attenuated the signal. He confirmed an external signal with a controlled experiment he ran himself, on a public street, in his hat.
Related reading: Yes, Technology Can Direct Humans to a Location Without Conscious Decision
His pants from a chemical assault at Rockville Metro remain preserved, untested. His bicycle was methodically destroyed over months— each component individually attributable to ordinary

wear, the cumulative effect forcing him onto his feet, slower, more predictable, more easily positioned around.
By February 2026 he was dispossessed except for a mattress and a suitcase. He attempted asylum in Iceland. He was returned under the safe country doctrine— the doctrine that treats the United States as safe for all its citizens, regardless of what specific citizens are experiencing.
His upstairs neighbor— not on the apartment lease, his address connecting to a Capitol Hill property linked to an ATF attorney— punched him in the face on December 27, 2025, targeting his head specifically when Pearson raised his arms to defend himself. The neighbor was not arrested. The week prior, Richard Pearson saw an ACCESS DENIED flag when the police searched his name in their database.
A woman passing him on Pennsylvania Avenue on August 3 said without stopping: “The mattress is against the wall, you know what’s next.” He had moved his mattress to the wall. Alone. In his apartment. With no digital transmission. That sentence required live audio surveillance of his private home.


The Technology
The behavioral direction Pearson documented rests on confirmed neuroscience.

The hippocampus contains place cells: neurons encoding specific locations that fire as the body approaches them, producing spatial drives felt as autonomous motivation. A 2020 study in Cell demonstrated that targeted activation of hippocampal place cells drives subjects to specific locations— experienced as their own movement. A 2024 study in Nature Neuroscience demonstrated non-invasive modulation of the human hippocampal navigation system using external electromagnetic stimulation, producing measurable changes in spatial behavior without implants or surgery.
DARPA’s Neural Engineering System Design program describes writing to neural tissue
non-invasively using electromagnetic waves. Dr. James Giordano of Georgetown University, the program’s consulting ethicist, testified before the House Armed Services Committee that tools for writing behavior to the human brain have been loosed into the real world.
Pearson was moved to a specific location through an impulse he experienced as his own. He did not know he was being moved. He found out because he is a trained researcher who had been documenting the campaign for months. Most people would never know.


Measuring Against the Criteria
Criterion One: Racial Component.

Pearson was targeted as a Black equity scholar on January 20, 2025 — the precise day executive orders designated his academic field as hostile ideology. The framing payload near the Capitol referenced Hitler and a “European Removal Court.” White supremacist participants are documented in his account. The FBI’s “Black Identity Extremism” designation provided the targeting framework. The racial basis is not inferred. It is stated in the executive orders that initiated the campaign.
At scale: Ward 7, Ward 8, Anacostia, Congress Heights are the neighborhoods the Great Migration built. They are majority Black by the design of six million people who chose Washington as their destination when they fled the South. Targeting them is targeting Blackness in its most concentrated and historically specific Washington form.
Criterion One: Met completely.


Criterion Two: State-Backed Structural Oppression.

The classification flag on Pearson’s police record cannot be placed by a private actor. The voice-to-skull technology confirmed by the Department of War in January 2026 is not commercially available. The behavioral direction technology is DARPA-derived and intelligence community-controlled. The FISA court provided judicial cover. The FBI provided the targeting framework. Local law enforcement provided surveillance and compliance. The D.C. government
was instructed not to resist. Every institutional door closed not by accident but by the design of a legal architecture— Glomar, state secrets privilege, standing doctrine, classification— built specifically to ensure it would.
This is not individual hostility. It is the full apparatus of the state operating in coordination against a person on the basis of his race and scholarship.
At scale: the same apparatus, the same agencies, the same legal architecture— except applied to African Americans systematically.
Criterion Two: Met completely.


Criterion Three: Displacement— Flight from Severe, State-Inflicted Bodily Violation.

This criterion requires precision. It is not about a quiet feeling of restlessness. It is not about desire manufactured below consciousness. It is about the violation of bodily autonomy so severe, so documented, and so backed by the full apparatus of the state, that flight becomes the only rational response to survival.
Consider what was done to Pearson’s body:

His skull was used as a receiver for voice-to-skull transmission without his consent. His brain’s navigation system was activated by external electromagnetic stimulation to move him against his autonomous will. His face and head were physically beaten in a targeted assault. His home was acoustically penetrated so thoroughly that his private speech was available to strangers on the street. A chemical of unknown composition was applied to his body at Rockville Metro. His neurological threshold was deliberately lowered through the assault to make subsequent directed energy deployment more effective.
His body was not his own. The government of the United States used his body— his skull, his brain, his hippocampus, his ears, his face— as instruments of its operation against him, without his knowledge, without his consent, and without any legal process.
He understood this. He sought asylum. He traveled to Iceland and applied for refugee status on the grounds that his government was torturing him. He was returned because the United States is classified as a safe country for all its citizens.
The asylum attempt is the most important single fact in this criterion. Pearson did not feel a vague pull toward leaving. He sought the formal international legal mechanism for escaping a government that is torturing you. He made the same rational calculation that asylum seekers from every country on earth make: I cannot be safe in my own country because my own government is the threat.
He was sent back.

At scale: Black Washingtonians who learn that the government has been activating the navigation systems of their brains without their consent— that their skulls have been used as receivers, that their desires have been manufactured, that their bodies have been moved through public space by external electromagnetic stimulation— will not feel a quiet pull toward leaving.
They will flee.

For the same reason Pearson fled to Iceland. Because the government has violated their bodily autonomy at the most intimate possible level— the level of their own cognition, their own desires, their own sense of autonomous will— and has done so on the basis of their race, with total impunity, backed by every institution that was supposed to protect them.
This is not managed displacement. This is people running from a government that has demonstrated it will use their own minds against them.
Criterion Three: Met completely. The asylum attempt is the proof.


Criterion Four: Population-Scale Magnitude.

This section requires expansion because the scale of what is being described is the element most easily dismissed as implausible.
Start with the infrastructure.

The Palantir platforms contracted to ICE and DHS integrate passport records, Social Security files, IRS tax data, license plate reader data, facial recognition feeds, commercial geolocation data, and social media monitoring into a single real-time targeting system. As of April 2025, ICE paid Palantir $30 million specifically to build ImmigrationOS— an AI enforcement platform designed to manage population-scale targeting and tracking. This platform was not built for Pearson. It was built for hundreds of thousands of people simultaneously. It is running now.
The directed energy infrastructure in Washington, D.C. is the densest in the United States. The Capitol complex, the White House, fourteen intelligence agencies, the NSA intercept infrastructure, the Pentagon across the river— the city is saturated with directed electromagnetic infrastructure whose operators are accountable to no municipal authority and whose operations are shielded by classification. The emitters required to produce geographic auditory phenomena at specific intersections— as Pearson documented— are not large, not visible, and not identifiable as weapons by anyone walking past them. Positioned at intervals across Ward 7, Ward 8, Anacostia, and Congress Heights, they would be operationally invisible.
The behavioral direction infrastructure— the temporal interference equipment producing beat frequencies at hippocampal depth— operates from range. Pearson was moved in a park near the Capitol from emitters estimated to be 50 to 300 meters away, potentially positioned in federal buildings with line-of-sight to his location. Ward 7 and Ward 8 are within operational range of federal infrastructure. The geometry required for population-scale hippocampal activation— two emitter sources per target zone, positioned to bracket the residential area— is achievable within existing federal real estate.
The AI orchestration layer is the capability the Pentagon demanded Anthropic remove its restrictions against in February 2026. Not restrictions against targeting individuals. Restrictions against mass domestic surveillance of Americans. The specific capability the administration declared a non-negotiable operational requirement is the management of population-scale
AI-orchestrated operations against American citizens. The dispute was public, reported across major outlets, and the administration’s position was not disputed by the Pentagon.
Now the numbers.

Ward 7: 75,000 residents. Ward 8: 83,000 residents. Anacostia and Congress Heights: additional tens of thousands. The remaining Black residential corridors of Capitol Hill, Petworth, Columbia Heights, and the broader historically Black geography of the city: hundreds of thousands more. Total Black Washington population: approximately 300,000 people.
For context: the entire Great Migration affected six million people across sixty years— an average of 100,000 people per year. Washington’s Black population of 300,000, displaced across five to ten years, would represent a demographic transformation comparable in its

concentrated geographic impact to the most intense phases of the original Migration— the Great Migration compressed into a single city, accomplished in a decade rather than sixty years, leaving no archive because the mechanism leaves no record.
The infrastructure to do this is not speculative. It is confirmed, contracted, operational, and being actively protected from restriction by the administration that controls it.
Criterion Four: Met. The infrastructure exists at the scale required.


Criterion Five: The Catalyst— Public Exposure.

Previous versions of this argument described the catalyst as a feeling— a manufactured desire to leave that, when disclosed, would recontextualize residents’ own experiences and produce flight.
That is wrong. It is too subtle. Too slow. It misses the actual mechanism.

The catalyst is media reporting. Public exposure. The moment a major news organization— or a congressional investigation, or a whistleblower with documents, or the cumulative weight of documented evidence reaching a tipping point of public credibility— reports that the U.S. government has been deploying behavioral direction and directed energy technology against Black residents of Washington, D.C.
Not Pearson’s individual case. The mass program.

The headline that reads: Federal Government Deployed Brain-Control Technology Against Black Washington Neighborhoods.
Or: Pentagon Directed Energy Weapons Confirmed in Operation Against Civilian Population in D.C.
Or simply: What Was Done to Richard Pearson Is Being Done to Hundreds of Thousands of People Right Now.
That headline— that public confirmation, reported by credible outlets, confirmed by technical experts, supported by the physical evidence that exists and has never been tested— does not produce a gradual demographic shift. It produces immediate, massive, rational flight.
Because what it tells every Black Washingtonian is not you might have been manipulated into wanting to leave. It tells them something far more severe and far more immediate:
The government has been operating on your brain without your consent. It has been using your skull as a receiver and your hippocampus as a navigation instrument and your desires as raw material for a demographic outcome you were never consulted about. It did this on the basis of

your race. Every institution that should have stopped it was complicit or captured. There is no protection. There is no recourse. And it is still running.
The response to that disclosure is not reconsideration of a vague feeling of restlessness. It is the same response Pearson had when he understood what was being done to him:
Get out.

He went to Iceland. He sought asylum from his own government on the grounds of torture and racial persecution. He was returned because the doctrine says the United States is safe for all its citizens.
Black Washington, upon public confirmation of the mass program, does not collectively attempt asylum in Iceland. It does what six million people did the last time it learned the government had decided its presence was intolerable and no institution would protect it:
It moves. Fast. To Atlanta, to Charlotte, to Houston, to wherever family is, to wherever the government is not operating a classified program against their brains.
The Great Migration was catalyzed by public events— Red Summer, Tulsa, Emmett Till— that confirmed, undeniably and simultaneously to an entire population, what the government would permit and what institutions would do. Each confirmation produced an acceleration. The accumulated confirmations over sixty years moved six million people.
This confirmation, delivered in a single news cycle, would move hundreds of thousands in months.
That is what the woman on Pennsylvania Avenue was describing. Not a slow demographic trend.
A specific future moment when the reporting comes out and Black Washington learns— all at once, publicly, undeniably— what has been done to their bodies and their minds by their government, and makes the only rational decision.
Criterion Five: Latent. The documentation exists. The physical evidence exists. The reporting has not come. When it does, this criterion is met immediately and completely.


Criterion Six: Permanent Demographic Transformation.

The people who leave will build lives elsewhere. Their children will not return. Washington D.C.— currently majority Black, currently the specific geography where Black political power is most concentrated relative to the seat of federal power— will be permanently reconfigured.

The transformation, once complete, becomes the new baseline. Future generations inherit it as simply the way things are. The question of what Washington used to be becomes a question for historians.
Criterion Six: Met completely— if displacement reaches scale.


Criterion Seven: Generational and Institutional Consequence.

Washington D.C. is not just a community. It is a political geography. The seat of federal power. Its majority-Black character is a specific form of Black political leverage that exists nowhere else in the United States.
Howard University. The HBCU ecosystem. The concentration of Black federal employees, Black lawyers, Black political operatives, Black scholars— the entire infrastructure of Black institutional power that accumulated in Washington precisely because the Great Migration sent people here. The political leverage that comes from being the majority population of the city that is the center of American governance.
Eliminate Black majority Washington and you eliminate that specific form of power. Not weaken it. Eliminate it. The children whose parents fled will not grow up with Washington as an identity, a community, a political base. The institutions that drew strength from Washington as a Black city will be hollowed or ended. The political leverage— gone.
Every subsequent generation inherits a Washington that was always this way. The history of what it was and what was done to change it will be, like the history of every previous removal, a story told in the past tense by people who were not there.
Criterion Seven: Met completely.




The Scorecard
Criterion Status Racial Component ✅ Met completely State-Backed Structural Oppression ✅ Met completely Flight from Severe Bodily Violation ✅ Met completely— asylum attempt is the proof Population-Scale Magnitude ✅ Infrastructure confirmed operational at scale Catalyst— Public Exposure and Media Reporting ⚡ Latent— documentation exists, reporting has not come Permanent Demographic Transformation ✅ Met completely if displacement reaches scale Generational and Institutional Consequence ✅ Met completely


Why She Wanted Him to Hear
The operation had his apartment audio in real time. It had his continuous location. It had been watching him build his documentation for months. It knew he was a researcher. It knew what he was writing down.
The encounter was not accidental. It was positioned on a route they knew he was walking, delivered without faces, timed to land in his notes.

She wanted it in his record because his record is the documentation that, when reported, is the disclosure. His fourteen months of timestamped evidence, clinical evaluations, controlled experiments, the SCI flag, the tub, the foreknowledge chat log, the metal cap experiment, and the asylum attempt— this is the foundation of the reporting that produces the catalyst.
She gave him the frame for what his documentation would cause. The record is the reporting waiting to happen.
The reporting is the catalyst.

The catalyst is the Great Migration.

She gave him all seven criteria in four words. He wrote them down.


The Window
There is a window between now and the reporting.

Inside it: the pants from the Rockville chemical assault can still be tested. The RF environment at Pearson’s documented click locations can still be measured by any researcher with a spectrum analyzer on a public sidewalk. The diffusion tensor imaging that identified directed energy damage in Havana Syndrome patients can still be applied to Pearson and to residents of affected neighborhoods. The Senate Intelligence Committee can still compel testimony.
These are not demands for conclusions. They are demands for investigation. For the application of existing instruments to existing evidence.
Pearson understood what was being done to him. He sought asylum. He was sent back. He built the record instead.
The record survives. The window is now.


Richard Pearson, PhD is a scholar whose full documentation is available. This essay draws on his account; D.C. DBH evaluations, September 2025; DARPA N3 program documentation; Carrillo-Reid et al. (2020), Cell; Boyle et al. (2024), Nature Neuroscience; Dr. James Giordano, Georgetown University, congressional testimony; National Academies of Sciences (2020), Havana Syndrome assessment; Department of War CTO statement, January 2026; Anthropic

statement, February 27, 2026; Palantir ImmigrationOS contract documentation, April 2025; Executive Order 14173, January 20, 2025




How the US Does Authoritarianism in Age of Trump: Secrecy, Denial, and Legal Defenses Against Transparency
One PhD scholar documented a torture campaign in Washington, D.C. that reveals a pathway to totalitarianism already in operation. It doesn’t look like what you expect.


We think we know what authoritarianism looks like.

We picture ICE raids, concentration camps, or death chambers. Cameras on every corner. Dissidents dragged from their homes. The visible machinery of a state that has stopped pretending. That image is not wrong— those things are happening. But there is a form of authoritarianism and torture that is unfolding in the United States right now.
What Richard Pearson, PhD experienced across fourteen months in Washington, D.C. cannot be any of those things. He was subjected to a campaign that included physical assault, chemical assault, warrantless surveillance of his private apartment, directed energy weapons producing sounds inside his skull, behavioral direction technology that moved him to specific locations through impulses he experienced as his own, a coordinated color-coded psychological operation run on his walking routes, and a framing operation in a park three blocks from the
U.S. Capitol.

He documented all of it. Methodically. With timestamps, photographs, probability calculations, named witnesses, preserved physical evidence, and the discipline of a researcher— because that is what he is.
This is not a story about one man. It is a story about a system. A system with six components, each individually defensible, that combine into something the United States Constitution was not designed to stop— because when the Constitution was written, the technology didn’t exist.
Following public disclosure of programs like COINTELPRO, MKULTRA, or, the human rights violations at Guantánamo Bay detention camp, US law has made it tougher, not easier, for victims of US-sponsored torture, even on US soil. The requirements for standing became stricter under the Rehnquist courts in the 1990s. FOIA courts, originally designed to be a check on development of such programs, now routinely place Americans under surveillance and targeting without their knowledge or due process. In fact, iterations of these programs maintain a simple template allowing for constitutional abuses. If history holds, then there are likely any similar programs that have existed for decades.

The Six-Step Pathway to Invisible Totalitarianism

Classify the action and Invoke a Legal Defense — Conduct operations against targeted individuals under classification, removing them from congressional oversight, FOIA, courts, and public record. The executive can also invoke any number of legal defenses to prevent victims and the courts from gaining knowledge of these programs. “National security” is a frequent defense that forces the courts to be highly deferential to the government.
Never arrest or charge — Deprive the target of liberty through deniable means, ensuring the constitutional moment at which due process could intervene never arrives. Victims do not even know that that they are in a program. If the program is as multi-layered, as Pearson’s account, then separating government action from private action is challenging, especially because the government won’t admit to anything.
Exclude all oversight institutions — Keep the program from Congress, the courts, and the press simultaneously, using classification, standing doctrine, and the absence of official confirmation to close every channel. If asked to confirm a program, dither, deny, or deflect.
Deny or manufacture an alternate explanation — Produce a counter-narrative, ideally pre-loaded into the design of the campaign itself, that attributes the target’s documented experiences to mental illness or coincidence. Falsify reports and encourage alternate theories until options are exhausted years later— an initial FBI report purportedly claimed that Havana Syndrome victims suffered from mass hysteria.
Use contractors and cutouts — Execute operations through private intermediaries who break the legal chain of custody between government authorization and government fingerprints
Use untraceable technology— especially neuroweapons — Deploy directed energy and behavioral direction tools that produce real harm while leaving no forensic trace that standard medical or legal instruments were designed to detect




Step One: Classify the Action
The first thing the system requires is that the government’s operation against a targeted individual be conducted under classification— invisible to Congress, immune to FOIA requests, unreachable by courts, and unpublishable by journalists.
The U.S. classification system was built for genuine national security. Over seventy years, it has grown into something else: a mechanism for permanent executive impunity. When Pearson filed a Freedom of Information Act request seeking records related to what was being done to him, the government invoked the Glomar doctrine— a legal principle that allows the government to refuse to confirm or deny that a relevant program even exists. His constitutional claims were dismissed before any court could examine them on the merits.
The week before Pearson was assaulted, he called the police to report the upstairs neighbors who were harassing him. The first officer ran his name, and encountered an ACCESS DENIED flag— Pearson saw it clearly. That also explains why the first officer called for back-up— a second officer who likely had higher clearance.
Someone inside the system had flagged Richard Pearson. No one has explained who, or why, or under what legal authority. Pearson was an academic with no criminal history.

Classification was designed to protect secrets from enemies. In Pearson’s case, it was protecting an operation from the person being subjected to it.


Step Two: Never Arrest or Charge
The Fifth Amendment says the government cannot deprive a person of liberty without due process of law. It is one of the most fundamental protections in the American constitutional framework.
It only works if the government does something visible enough to trigger it.

Pearson was never charged with anything. Never designated an enemy combatant, a terrorist, or a security threat on any public record. He was deprived of liberty— his body assaulted, his home surveilled, his neurological experience invaded, his behavior directed without his knowledge— through means that never produced the constitutional moment at which a lawyer could walk into a courtroom and say: this is what they did, and here is why it is illegal.
His upstairs neighbor, a man not on the apartment lease whose address traces to a Capitol Hill property connected to an ATF attorney, punched him repeatedly in the face on December 27, 2025. The neighbor was not arrested. He has never been told why the campaign against him was authorized, by whom, or under what law.
Due process does not protect you from a government that never formally acts against you. It only triggers when the government shows its face. This system was designed so the government’s face never shows.


Step Three: Keep Congress, the Courts, and the Press in the Dark
Three institutions were designed to constrain the executive branch: Congress, the judiciary, and the free press. The pathway to invisible totalitarianism requires that all three be neutralized simultaneously.
Congress can compel testimony and review classified programs through the intelligence committees— but only if members are briefed, and only if they have the political will to act on what they learn. Pearson visited dozens of congressional offices in December 2025. No one followed up with him. The Trump administration would be required to report such a program to the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence by law. Pearson does not think that this has happened.
The courts require standing— the ability to demonstrate that you were harmed by a specific government action. In Clapper v. Amnesty International (2013), the Supreme Court ruled that plaintiffs couldn’t challenge NSA surveillance because they couldn’t prove they had been
