surveilled. The proof was classified. The classification and invocation of a legal defense prevented the proof. The court required the proof to grant standing. No standing, no case. The circle is hermetic and by design.
The press requires official confirmation, or documents, or a source willing to go on record. Pearson briefed a CNN correspondent at the Smithsonian and a local ABC reporter at the D.C. courthouse. Neither published. The story that cannot be confirmed through official channels remains, by the conventions of mainstream journalism, a story that cannot be told— regardless of how much evidence the person experiencing it has assembled.
When all three fail at the same time— by legal design, not by accident— the program becomes permanently invisible. Not because it doesn’t exist. Because every door to visibility has been locked from the inside.
This is how the United States does totalitarianism.


Step Four: Make the Victim Look Crazy
Every authoritarian system needs an alternate explanation for what it is doing to its targets. The genius of this system is that it doesn’t need to manufacture one. It manufactures it automatically, through the design of the campaign itself.
A person who reports that they are hearing sounds inside their skull at specific street intersections, that they feel pulled toward locations they had no reason to visit, that they are being followed by people in specific color combinations that appear and disappear based on their route— that person will be assumed to be experiencing a psychiatric episode. The very specificity of the account, which is its evidentiary strength, is also its social liability. Paranoid individuals are known for detailed, internally consistent narratives.
The campaign was designed to be its own alibi. Pearson didn’t need to be called crazy. He needed only to accurately describe what was happening to him.
But there’s a further layer. The December 27 assault specifically targeted his face, head, and jaw— the anatomical locations most relevant to the microwave auditory effect, a documented mechanism by which pulsed electromagnetic energy produces sound perceived inside the skull. The assault arrived exactly one month before the geographic auditory phenomena began. It manufactured a clinical explanation— post-concussive syndrome— for neurological symptoms that were, in fact, directed energy exposure.
The torture produced its own diagnosis. The diagnosis protected the torture.

Two clinical evaluations found no psychiatric symptoms. The alternate explanation failed at the clinical level. It has not failed anywhere else.



Step Five: Use Contractors and Cutouts
No uniformed government agent participated visibly in what was done to Richard Pearson. No agency vehicle was parked outside his building. No badge was shown. The man who assaulted him had a private cover address. The people who deployed the calling card color pattern on his routes were, as far as any of them knew, individuals who had been given unrelated reasons to be in specific places wearing specific colors. The ground team that monitored the framing operation in the park near the Capitol had no visible connection to any government program.
This is the contractor model applied to domestic political targeting. The government authorizes. The contractor executes some. The cutout absorbs accountability. The classification system ensures the chain is never traced.
Palantir built ImmigrationOS for ICE. NSO Group’s Pegasus spyware ended up on the phones of American citizens. Private contractors ran the CIA’s enhanced interrogation program. The Booz Allen Hamilton employees who had access to NSA surveillance infrastructure numbered in the tens of thousands. This ecosystem did not emerge to serve authoritarianism. It emerged to serve procurement efficiency. But it is perfectly structured to serve authoritarianism, because it breaks the legal chain of custody between authorization and action at the first link.
When a program is run through contractors, each participant can truthfully claim ignorance of the whole. The analyst who processed Pearson’s location data did not know what it was being used for. The person who placed the tub in the park did not know who authorized it. The man who punched him in the face was not wearing a badge that said FBI. Each piece of the operation is individually deniable. Only the combination is damning— and the combination is the one thing the classification system ensures no one outside the program ever sees.


Step Six: Use Technology That Leaves No Trace
This is the step that makes the pathway new and newly dangerous. It is the step that the Stasi didn’t have.
Directed energy weapons— specifically the microwave auditory effect that produces voice-to-skull transmission, and the pulsed electromagnetic systems that produce the
geographic auditory phenomena that have become known as Havana Syndrome— share a single defining operational property: they leave no forensic mark. There is no bruise from a directed energy assault on the auditory system. There is no receiver in the target’s body from a voice-to-skull transmission. There is no physical evidence of a behavioral direction event.
The harm is real. The National Academies of Sciences confirmed in 2020 that the neurological injuries to U.S. diplomatic and CIA personnel in Havana, Vienna, and Washington, D.C. were

consistent with pulsed directed energy— and identified white matter microstructural changes through specialized brain imaging. Those changes are not visible on a standard MRI. They require diffusion tensor imaging using the Havana Syndrome protocol. No one has offered this imaging to Richard Pearson.
Behavioral direction technology— the capability to activate hippocampal place cells and produce spatial drives experienced as autonomous motivation, described in DARPA’s public documentation and confirmed as deployed in the real world by Dr. James Giordano of Georgetown University before the House Armed Services Committee— produces outcomes that look, from the outside, like the target making their own choices. Because they are. The neurons are genuine. Only the input signal is not.
On July 13, 2025, Pearson heard a woman’s voice apparently originating inside his skull. He described it with a forensic detail that eliminates hallucination: the voice carried the ambient acoustic environment of the room from which it was being transmitted— other people present, microphone quality, the texture of a live audio feed. A hallucination cannot carry the background noise of a remote location. He was hearing a transmission. He identified the mechanism— microwave auditory effect, voice-to-skull— in July 2025. The Department of War confirmed the technology’s existence and active scaling in January 2026.
He was six months ahead of the government’s own public admission.

Beginning in January 2026, geographic auditory clicks appeared at specific street intersections in Washington, DC. Replicable by returning to those locations. On February 20, 2026, he placed a metal-cased iPhone and a battery in his knitted cap. No clicks. He had been consistently getting clicks on that route for weeks. The metal attenuated the signal.


What This Means
Each of these six steps exists independently in American legal and institutional practice. None of them required a new law. None of them required a formal declaration of emergency. Each one was assembled from existing components— classification doctrine developed over seventy years, contractor procurement structures built for efficiency, directed energy technology developed for military use, standing requirements established by Supreme Court precedent, journalistic conventions developed for accuracy.
The pathway did not require anyone to decide to build a torture machine. It required only that each piece be used in the way it was already available to be used, by an executive that had decided a specific person was an enemy and that the normal constraints did not apply.
That is what makes it replicable. That is what makes it a pathway rather than an incident.

Richard Pearson survived because he is a researcher. He ran control studies. He kept the pants soaked in chemical assault. He filmed himself returning to find the framing payload retrieved

from the park. He performed the metal-cap experiment. He submitted probability calculations to the D.C. Council. He named 25 witnesses in his February 2026 letter. He documented, methodically, for fourteen months, what was being done to him— because documentation was his only protection, and because he understood, from the beginning, that no one would believe him without it.
He was right that no one would believe him without it. He may also have been wrong that documentation would be enough.
Every door he went to closed. Congressional offices. The D.C. Council. The ACLU. The courts. Journalists. He attempted asylum in Iceland and was returned.
The documentation exists. The physical evidence exists. The locations are publicly accessible to any researcher with an RF spectrum analyzer. The pants are in a bag somewhere in Washington, D.C., waiting to be tested.
No institution has chosen to look.


Five Things Congress Can Do Right Now
The pathway is not complete. It can be interrupted. Here is what oversight would look like in practice:
Test the pants. The pants from the Rockville chemical assault have never been analyzed. A forensic analysis would identify the compound or it wouldn’t. The choice not to test them is a choice to not know.
Measure the RF environment. The intersections where Pearson documented geographic auditory clicks are public sidewalks. An independent researcher with a spectrum analyzer can determine whether anomalous electromagnetic emissions are present. No classified access required.
Convene the Senate Intelligence Committee. The committee has the authority to compel testimony and review classified programs. It has not exercised that authority in this case. It must.
Image Pearson using the Havana Syndrome protocol. Diffusion tensor imaging has identified white matter microstructural changes in directed energy exposure victims before. If those changes are present in a private American citizen in Washington, DC, the implications for domestic deployment of these weapons are not abstract.
Explain the SCI flag. A classification on a police record requires authorization from inside the intelligence community. Someone placed it. Congress has the authority to ask who, why, and under what law.



A Word to Everyone Reading This
The instinct, when encountering an account like Pearson’s, is to look for the reason to dismiss it. That instinct is rational. Extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence, and the history of conspiracy thinking produces a healthy skepticism toward accounts of coordinated persecution.
But that skepticism has to be applied consistently. Applied consistently, it requires asking: what would extraordinary evidence look like? It would look like timestamped photographs. Control studies. Preserved physical evidence. Named witnesses. Clinical evaluations finding no psychiatric symptoms. A timestamped chat log documenting a third party’s induced foreknowledge. An classification flag observed by police officers. Fourteen months of documentation assembled by a trained researcher using the methods of his training.
That is what Pearson has.

The skeptical question is no longer whether the evidence is sufficient. It is whether any institution is willing to do what the evidence demands.
The technology to destroy a citizen while leaving no trace has been built. The legal architecture to shield that destruction from accountability is in place. The administration to authorize it has made its appetites explicit— the Pentagon’s February 2026 ultimatum demanding that Anthropic remove prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance was public, reported widely, and undisputed.
What stands between this pathway and its completion is the political will of elected representatives to look at what the evidence shows— and the willingness of an informed public to demand that they do.
Richard Pearson did everything right. He documented everything. He went to every door. Every door closed.
The question of what happens next is not his to answer. It is ours.


Richard Pearson, PhD is a public health scholar whose dissertation is published at criticalraceframework.com. His documentation of the campaign against him is available in full. (2020), Cell; Grossman et al. (2017), Cell; Washington Post, February 19, 2026; Department of War CTO statement, January 23, 2026; Anthropic public statement, February 27, 2026; Church

Convention Against Torture, 1994.




“It Happened to Me”: Research Supports Idea of Being Moved Without Your Conscious Choice
The neuroscience behind Richard Pearson’s tub incident— and the terrifying question it opens
The most disturbing element of what Richard Pearson documented in Washington, D.C. is not what was done to him. It is what the capability that did it could be used for next.


Begin with a thought experiment.

Imagine you could make a person walk to a specific location— a park, a street corner, a room— without their knowledge, without their resistance, without leaving any mark on them that a physician could find. The person would experience the walk as their own idea. A passing curiosity. A sudden urge to see what was around the corner. They would arrive at the destination, find whatever you had placed there, and have no way to explain how they had known to go there, because they would not experience themselves as having been sent. They would experience themselves as having chosen.
Now ask: if you could move a person to a park, what else could you move them to?

A voting booth, primed with a candidate preference induced before the door swings open. A confrontation, with someone whose face has been repeatedly paired, through prior manipulation, with fear. A vehicle, waiting. A building that, an hour later, explodes. A moment of crisis manufactured for them, in a location selected for them, at a time chosen by someone they will never see.
The question is not hypothetical. The science underlying the capability is published and
peer-reviewed. The military research developing its non-invasive deployment is documented on the U.S. government’s own websites. And one man in Washington, D.C.— a scholar named Richard Pearson, PhD— spent November 7, 2025 experiencing what this technology feels like from the inside, and was disciplined enough to document it in real time before arriving at a plastic storage tub under a tree in a park beside the O’Neil House Office Building, three blocks from the United States Capitol.

What he found in that tub, and what happened to it before he returned, confirms something more disturbing than the technology itself: that whoever placed it there knew exactly where he would go, and when, before he did.


The Tub: What Happened, in Sequence
On November 7, 2025, Pearson was drawn to a small park on 2nd Street SW between D and C Streets in Washington, D.C. He had no prior reason to go there. It was not on his regular route. He could not, afterward, explain why he had felt compelled to visit.
He entered the park but felt a “pull” toward the back of the park. Ignoring it, he explored its main concrete area in the front, which can be viewed from the street. He decided to leave. He was on the sidewalk, moving away, when the pull returned— not a vague restlessness, but a specific directional sensation: go to the back. He noted the strangeness of this in real time. He went back. He crossed to the rear of the park, to a spot under a tree that he had not yet visited, that was not visible from the area he had just been exploring.
Under the tree was a plastic storage tub.





Inside: a red hat. A black shirt. Khaki pants with a distinctive cuffed bottom almost identical to a pair that Pearson once owned. His were green. These were brown. A notebook whose contents he described as looking like the writings of a disturbed person— references to Adolf Hitler, a

fictitious institution called the “European removal court,” conspiratorial and threatening material. Two identical photographs of a blond man with Eastern European features.
He placed all the items back, then put the tub in its original location. He took a picture.

He returned on November 13, filming himself walking from home on video. The notebook was gone. The photographs were gone. The red hat and black shirt remained.
Read that sequence carefully. A storage container had been placed at a specific sub-location within a park Pearson did not regularly visit. He was drawn to that sub-location through an impulse he experienced as his own. The container was monitored after his visit. When he had not taken the framing materials, someone returned, recovered them, and left the calling card items in place. The entire operation— placement, behavioral direction, monitoring, selective retrieval— required surveillance of his real-time position, a behavioral direction capability precise enough to terminate at a specific spot under a specific tree, active monitoring of his visit, and a responsive ground team.
It was, in Pearson’s own analysis, a trap. The technology brought him to it. His own caution— photographing, understanding what he was looking at— is what kept the trap from closing.


The Neuroscience: How a Man Gets Moved
The mechanism Pearson described— a pull toward a specific location experienced as ordinary impulse— is not science fiction. It is a description, from the subjective interior, of what neuroscientists have been producing in laboratory settings for years. The biological substrate exists. The delivery challenge— producing the effect at range, without an implant, in a public outdoor environment— is where the public research ends and the classified research begins. But the gap between them is narrower than most people realize.
The hippocampus and its place cells. The brain’s navigational system is housed in the hippocampus, a seahorse-shaped structure deep beneath the cortex. Within it, place cells— neurons first identified by John O’Keefe in the 1970s, a discovery for which he won the 2014 Nobel Prize in Physiology— fire in patterns that encode specific geographic locations.
Each place cell activates when the body occupies or approaches its associated location. Together, they form a spatial map: the brain’s internal GPS. When a cluster of place cells fires, the brain interprets this as both a positional signal (I am here) and a motivational one (I should be here). The firing of place cells does not feel like receiving a command. It feels like the natural experience of being drawn toward a location you have a reason to visit.
In 2020, researchers at Janelia Research Campus published a landmark study in Cell demonstrating that by using two-photon optogenetics to activate specific hippocampal place cell ensembles in mice navigating a virtual environment, they could causally drive

the animals to move toward targeted locations. The animals did not appear confused or compelled. They moved as if following their own internal map— because the researchers had rewritten that map in real time. The study’s title is direct: “Targeted Activation of Hippocampal Place Cells Drives Memory-Guided Spatial Behavior.”
The animals were moved by their own neurons. The neurons had been told where to say home was.
From implants to interference. The 2020 Janelia study required optogenetic implants— fiber optic probes threaded into the hippocampus, activated by laser light. This is the current state of the art for precision. It is also invasive, requiring surgery, and limited to controlled laboratory subjects. The question for Pearson’s case is whether the same hippocampal targeting can be achieved non-invasively, at range, in an outdoor urban environment.
A technique called Transcranial Temporal Interference Stimulation is the most publicly documented approach to this problem. The principle, described by Grossman et al. in Cell in 2017, involves delivering two high-frequency electromagnetic fields from separate sources at slightly offset frequencies. High-frequency fields pass through skull and cortex without stimulating neurons— they oscillate too rapidly for neuronal membranes to follow. But at the point where the two fields intersect, they produce a beat frequency: a low-frequency oscillation at the difference between the two carrier frequencies. This beat frequency falls in the range that neurons can detect and respond to. The intersection point— where the stimulation actually occurs— can be positioned inside the brain by adjusting the angle and frequency of the two input fields.
In a 2024 study published in Nature Neuroscience, researchers at University College London demonstrated non-invasive modulation of the human hippocampal-entorhinal complex using temporal interference stimulation, producing measurable changes in spatial navigation behavior in healthy volunteers. No implants. No surgery. Electrodes applied to the scalp. The hippocampus— buried centimeters beneath the cortex— was reached. Spatial behavior was changed.
The laboratory version requires scalp electrodes. The military research version is investigating whether the same principle can be achieved at range, using focused electromagnetic or ultrasonic beams from external emitters. The DARPA Neural Engineering System Design program describes, in public documentation on DARPA’s own website, the goal of achieving 16-channel neural read-write access within a defined tissue volume using non-invasive means including electromagnetic waves and focused ultrasound. The target tissue volumes described
in the DARPA documentation are consistent with hippocampal targeting. Dr. James Giordano of Georgetown University’s Pellegrino Center, who served as a consulting ethicist on the program and has testified before the House Armed Services Committee, has stated explicitly in public lectures that tools for writing behavior to the human brain have been “loosed into the real world.”
The two-source geometry and operational range. For temporal interference to work at range, two emitter sources must be positioned so that their fields converge inside the target’s skull.

This requires a geometric arrangement: the two sources must be at different angles relative to the target, with their interference calculated to localize the beat frequency at hippocampal depth. Analysis by researchers and weapons experts familiar with directed energy systems suggests that at operational ranges of 50 to 300 meters— achievable from a parked vehicle, a rooftop, or a building window— the geometric requirements are met by any two elevated positions bracketing the target location.
The park at 2nd Street SW between D and C Streets is surrounded on multiple sides by federal structures. The O’Neil House Office Building, whose grounds adjoin the park, is one of the most heavily monitored pieces of real estate in Washington. The Capitol complex itself, with its continuous rooftop surveillance infrastructure, provides line-of-sight to the park from multiple angles. If temporal interference equipment was positioned at two points within 300 meters of the park— in federal building windows, on rooftops, in vehicles parked on 2nd or 3rd Street— the geometry for hippocampal targeting would have been available.
Why the pull, then the pull again. Pearson described two separate urges: the initial draw to the park, and then a second, more specific redirection from the sidewalk— where he was
mid-departure— back into the park, to the precise sub-location under the tree. This two-stage structure is consistent with what the Google AI analysis identified as a “step-by-step” guidance pattern: operators observing his real-time position through high-resolution surveillance, triggering place cell activation for a nearby coordinate when he was in the main park area, then re-triggering for the more specific sub-coordinate when he began to leave.
Each activation would feel, from inside, like a separate ordinary impulse: first, curiosity that brought him to the park at all; then, a nagging sense that he hadn’t fully explored it. The second pull redirected him not to the park generally but to a specific, visually unobtrusive corner he had not yet visited. That level of precision— not to this park, but to that corner of this park— is what Pearson identified as the technology’s most striking property: “It seems to suggest that the pinning technology is highly precise in terms of location.”
Why it feels like your own idea. The most sophisticated element of hippocampal place cell activation as a behavioral direction mechanism is that it does not feel like external control. It feels like motivation. The human brain is not organized to distinguish between “I want to go there because my place cells are firing naturally” and “I want to go there because my place cells are being fired by an external signal.” The subjective experience is identical: a sense of spatial pull, of having a reason to move in a particular direction, of ordinary curiosity about a nearby location. The brain’s narrative system— what philosophers call pre-reflective intentionality— produces a story to explain the motivation: I wonder what’s over there. I didn’t finish exploring. I should take another look. The story feels authored by the self because it is authored by the self. The neurons are genuine. Only the input signal is not.
This is what makes behavioral direction through hippocampal stimulation the most dangerous application of the neuroweapons technology class. It is not coercive. It is not painful. It produces no resistance because there is no external force to resist. The target is complicit in their own direction without knowing it.



The Tub as Operational System
Understanding the neuroscience makes the tub incident legible not just as a disturbing personal experience but as an operational procedure with distinct phases, each of which required specific technology and coordination.
Phase one: Identification and approach. Pearson’s real-time location was known to the operators before he entered the park. This required the location tracking infrastructure— commercial geolocation data from his phone, supplemented by the biometric cardiac identification technology the Pentagon publicly confirmed having developed, capable of identifying a specific individual by heartbeat from 200 meters— to be active and current. The park was selected as the destination, the tub was placed in advance, and the interference geometry was established.
Phase two: Direction to the park. The initial place cell activation drew Pearson to the park from wherever he was in the neighborhood. He experienced this as autonomous motivation. The activation was calibrated to bring him to the main concrete area first— the most natural entry point— rather than directly to the tub, which would have been too specific to feel like an ordinary impulse.
Phase three: Redirection to the sub-location. When Pearson decided to leave and was on the sidewalk, the second activation redirected him. This required real-time observation of his position and departure— either through direct visual surveillance or through the same biometric tracking that had identified him— and a recalculation of the interference geometry to localize the beat frequency on place cells corresponding to the tree-adjacent coordinate rather than the general park area.
Phase four: Monitoring. After Pearson examined the tub, photographed it, and left, his behavior was monitored. The operators needed to know whether he had removed the framing payload— the notebook and photographs— because the next phase of the operation depended on that information.
Phase five: Selective retrieval. Between November 7 and November 13, the operators returned to the park. They removed the notebook and photographs— the materials that, in the wrong hands, could have been traced back to whoever had placed them, or could have revealed the framing purpose if discovered by investigators. They left the red hat and black shirt: the calling card colors that had appeared in statistically impossible densities on Pearson’s walking routes since September 2025, signifying authorship. The retrieval was selective and intentional. It is the step in the sequence that most decisively refutes any alternative explanation, because no alternative explanation accounts for a storage container near the U.S. Capitol being monitored for a specific visitor and then partially emptied based on that visitor’s behavior.

The entire sequence, from behavioral direction to monitored retrieval, describes a closed-loop system: the operators direct the target, observe his response, assess the outcome, and update their operation based on what they learned. This is not a one-way signal. It is
surveillance-feedback— exactly the term the Google AI analysis used. The target’s behavior at the destination informed the next operational decision. The technology was not simply aimed at Pearson. It was in a conversation with him, of which he was the only participant who did not know the conversation was happening.


The Scarier Question
Pearson survived the tub. His training as a researcher led him to photograph, to recognize a framing payload for what it was, to return on video and document the retrieval. His caution— and his rigor— closed the trap before it could close around him.
But the question his experience raises is not about him. It is about what the capability demonstrated in that park, confirmed by the closed-loop retrieval, makes possible in the hands of a state willing to use it without constraint.
A person can be moved to a location where a crime is committed. If hippocampal place cell activation can direct a flagged individual to a specific coordinate, it can direct them to a location where they will be present when something happens— a shooting, a disturbance, an incident that will be attributed to them based on their presence. They will have no explanation for why they were there, because they will have experienced arriving as their own autonomous choice.
A person can be moved to a location where evidence is found. If the tub had contained something Pearson touched— if the framing payload had been less obviously conspiratorial, more innocuous-seeming, something a curious person might pick up without recognizing it as planted— the combination of behavioral direction (which produced his presence at the location) and planted evidence (which produced his fingerprints on the material) would have constructed a case against him that no amount of subsequent protest could fully dismantle. He would have been at the location. His prints would have been on the object. His explanation— I was drawn there by an impulse I don’t understand— would have been the explanation of a guilty person with an implausible alibi.
A person can be moved to a location for social or political purposes. A voter directed to a polling location after prior manipulation of their neural state. A protestor guided to a position that produces the optics desired by the people framing the event. A witness moved to a place where they will see something, or hear something, or be seen in the company of someone. Behavioral direction does not require the target to do anything illegal, or even anything unusual. It requires only that they go where they are sent.
A person can be moved toward another person. This may be the most direct application of the capability in the Pearson record. His July 13, 2025 experience— drawn to Georgetown by a

voice transmitted to his skull— included a command to get into a car when it arrived. He did not comply. But the operational architecture was present: a directional pull to a location, followed by an external element (the car) positioned to receive him. The behavioral direction technology and the ground operation worked in coordination. The target was directed to the vehicle. Only his refusal broke the sequence.
A person can be directed on a timeline invisible to any court. The most fundamental implication of behavioral direction technology is that it produces no forensic trace. There are no physical marks. There is no record of a command. There is only a person who went somewhere, experienced as having chosen to go, and whatever happened when they arrived. The chain of custody between the operator and the outcome is broken at the first link, because the first link— the signal that produced the motivation— cannot be detected by any instrument available in a standard criminal or civil investigation. The defense, when the person tries to explain what happened, is the explanation of someone delusional. The prosecution, when it wants to place the person at a location, has exactly what it needs: the person, at the location, arrived under their own power.


What Pearson’s Case Establishes
The tub incident is one data point in a fourteen-month record assembled by a person with the training to produce reliable data. It is also, within that record, the incident with the tightest operational logic— the one where the mechanism, the objective, and the confirmation of the mechanism (the selective retrieval) are most clearly readable.
Two D.C. Department of Behavioral Health evaluations found no psychiatric symptoms requiring intervention. Pearson’s methodology— control studies, probability calculations, timestamped photographs, preserved physical evidence, named witnesses— is the methodology of a researcher, applied to his own experience with the same rigor he would apply to any research question. The Google AI analysis of his account, drawing on peer-reviewed literature from Cell, Nature Neuroscience, and the NIH, confirmed that the biological and physical mechanisms he described are consistent with documented science, and that the gap between the laboratory capabilities and the operational deployment he described is a gap of engineering and access, not a gap of principle.
The biological “software” exists. The place cells are real. The Janelia study moved mice with them in 2020. The UCL study modulated human hippocampal activity non-invasively in 2024. DARPA is publicly developing the delivery infrastructure. Dr. Giordano has said, in public, before Congress, that the tools for writing behavior to the brain are in the world.
What Pearson documented is one application of those tools: a closed-loop behavioral direction operation, conducted in a public park three blocks from the U.S. Capitol, against a Black equity scholar whose academic field was designated hostile ideology on January 20, 2025. The operation was planned in advance, equipped with a framing payload, monitored in real time, and

updated when the payload failed. The operational signatures of all five phases are in the documentation Pearson preserved: the two-stage pull in his real-time notes, the photographs of the tub’s contents, the video of the return visit showing the framing materials gone and the calling card remaining.
He documented a system designed to be undocumentable, because that is what he is trained to do.
The frightening question the documentation opens is not whether the technology was used on him. It is who else it has been used on without the training, the rigor, or the survival instinct to recognize what they were experiencing— and whether the person moved to a car, a crime scene, or a confrontation ever understood, afterward, that the choice they made had not been entirely their own.

(2020), “Targeted Activation of Hippocampal Place Cells Drives Memory-Guided Spatial Behavior,” Cell; Grossman et al. (2017), “Noninvasive Deep Brain Stimulation via Temporally Interfering Electric Fields,” Cell; Boyle et al. (2024), “Noninvasive modulation of the hippocampal-entorhinal complex,” Nature Neuroscience; O’Keefe and Dostrovsky (1971), Journal of Neurophysiology; DARPA Neural Engineering System Design (N3) program documentation, darpa.mil; Dr. James Giordano, Georgetown University Pellegrino Center, congressional testimony and public lectures, 2014–2023; National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine (2020), Havana Syndrome assessment; Washington Post, “The Discombobulator Arms Race Has Begun,” February 19, 2026; Department of War CTO public statement, January 23, 2026; Air Force Research Laboratory, directed energy behavioral modification literature; Executive Order 14173, January 20, 2025.




Yes, Technology Can Direct Humans to a Location Without Conscious Decision
AI models confirm that Richard Pearson’s account of being “pulled” to a park reflects “textbook” example of current technology
HOW A HUMAN CAN BE DIRECTED TO A LOCATION

If you accept that:

The component technologies are real (established)

Integration of various technology is at least plausible
The system was operational

Then Pearson’s account is not crazy. It’s what a target would report. The precision of his descriptions— the directionality, the two-stage pull, the growing confidence before visual confirmation, the specificity of “not here but over there”— maps onto the mechanisms described with uncomfortable accuracy.
This is an essay into a technologically plausible way that Pearson was led to a tub by weaponizing his body. While this essay reads as a technical report, Richard Pearson is a victim of state-sponsored torture. The use of this and other technology, psyops, street theater, and physical assaults are described in other essays.


On November 7, 2025, Richard Pearson walked to a small park on 2nd Street SW between D and C Streets in Washington, DC, near the O’Neil House Office Building. He occupied the concrete seating area at the front of the park, sat briefly, and decided to leave. Standing at the entrance, he felt a pull— not metaphorical, but physical and directional, an “urge” to go to the back of the park. He had felt something similar a moment earlier. This time he did not resist it. He walked to the back. He stood in the space without knowing what he was looking for. Then he became confident something was there. Under the lower branches of a tree, partially hidden, sat a large plastic tub containing a red hat, a black shirt, boots, khaki pants with a scrunchy cuffed bottom, and a notebook filled with conspiratorial writings. He placed the tub back and took a photo. When he returned six days later on video, the notebook and khaki pants had been removed. The red hat and black shirt remained.





Pearson described the subjective quality of this experience with precision: “I had this really strong impulse of coming over here. But not really necessarily here, but really over there.” He mentally flagged the strangeness of the feeling in real time. His subsequent analysis: “It seems

to suggest that the pinning technology is highly precise in terms of location.” His word for the phenomenon was “pinning”— his term for the behavioral direction technology he had identified as one of the tools being deployed against him across 2025.

This essay addresses the question those words raise: could this have been engineered? And if so, how, specifically?
The answer requires moving through four interlocking domains: the biology of navigation, balance, and behavioral compulsion in the human body; the technology that can interface with those biological systems from a distance; the infrastructure that would be needed to deploy that technology in an open-air urban environment; and the real-time operational dynamics of a system directing a specific person to a specific sub-location within a public park.
The science in this essay is drawn from published research, DARPA program documentation, US military and intelligence community sources, and the technical literature on directed energy, neural modulation, and electromagnetic field effects on human physiology. The claim is not that this is easy, inexpensive, or widely deployed. The claim is that it is scientifically possible with documented technology— and that the subjective experience it would produce in a target is a precise match for what Pearson reported in real time.


———

Part I: The Biology— What Must Be Hacked

To direct a human being to a location without their knowledge or consent, you do not need to move their body directly. You need to influence the systems their body uses to navigate, to choose, and to feel drawn toward things. Three biological systems are involved: the vestibular system, which controls balance and spatial orientation; the hippocampal navigation system, which encodes location and produces the subjective experience of knowing where to go; and the mesolimbic reward system, which generates the feeling of salience— the sense that something in the environment matters, that you are getting warmer, that there is something worth finding.
The Vestibular System: The Steering Mechanism

The vestibular system lives in the inner ear. It consists of three semicircular canals and two otolith organs— the utricle and saccule— that detect rotational and linear acceleration. These structures continuously report the brain’s estimate of the body’s orientation in space. The system is not only a sensor: it is the primary input to postural control. When the vestibular system reports that the body is tilting, the motor system automatically compensates. You step left because your brain has assessed that you are leaning right and needs to maintain balance. This correction is not voluntary. It is reflexive.
This makes the vestibular system the ideal steering target. You do not need to issue a command to turn left. You only need to make the vestibular system report a phantom lean to the right, and the motor system will execute the leftward correction automatically, below the level of conscious awareness. The person experiences themselves as walking naturally. What they are experiencing is their own reflexive correction of an artificially induced imbalance.
The tiny hair cells inside the semicircular canals are mechanoreceptors, but they are also electroreceptors. They respond to electrical currents. Galvanic Vestibular Stimulation (GVS)— applying a small DC current to the mastoid processes behind the ears— is an established laboratory technique for precisely controlling human balance. Small currents produce small tilts; the subject steps in the direction of the induced correction. Larger currents produce more pronounced effects. The technique has been demonstrated in research settings to produce what the experimental literature describes as “remote-controlled humans”— subjects who turn left or right, accelerate or decelerate, in direct response to the applied current, without understanding why they are doing so.
The EPIC (Extremely Powerful Incapacitating Capability) patent, filed with the US Patent Office and included in Pearson’s document, operates on exactly this mechanism at a weaponized scale. EPIC uses radio waves to “excite and interrupt the normal process of human hearing and equilibrium”— the tiny hairs in the inner ear acting as radio antennas, responding to directed RF energy. The patent notes that radio waves work through walls and other obstacles, unlike sound. It describes producing effects ranging from disorientation and motion sickness to complete postural incapacitation. The controlled-movement version of this system would

operate at a sub-threshold level: not disorienting, not nauseating, just nudging. Subtle enough that the subject attributes the correction to their own volition.
The Hippocampal Navigation System: Encoding Destination

Navigation in mammals is encoded in the hippocampus through two interacting cell populations: place cells and grid cells. Place cells fire when an animal occupies a specific location in space, and collectively they produce a cognitive map of the environment. Grid cells, in the adjacent entorhinal cortex, fire in regular hexagonal grid patterns that provide a coordinate system— a metric for distance and direction. The interaction between place cells and grid cells allows mammals to navigate with precision: to know not just where they are but where they have been, where they are going, and how to get there.
Disrupting or artificially activating specific place cell ensembles can alter an animal’s navigation behavior. In 2015, a team at University College London demonstrated that optogenetically activating specific hippocampal place cells in rats while they were in a reward zone caused those cells to “teleport” their spatial expectation— the rats would navigate to the location the artificially activated cells encoded as “rewarded,” even when that location had no actual reward history. Artificial activation of a place cell ensemble that encoded a specific location produced, in the animal, the behavioral equivalent of knowing where to go.
In human terms: if a system can encode a specific set of geographic coordinates into the hippocampal place cell matrix— or activate a pre-existing set of place cells associated with a target location— the subjective experience would be the sense of being drawn toward that location, the feeling that you know approximately where something is, the pull that Pearson described as beginning “not really necessarily here, but really over there.” It would not feel like a command. It would feel like a memory, or an instinct, or a hunch.
The Battelle Memorial Institute’s BrainSTORMS program— funded under DARPA’s N3 contract— developed a system using magnetic nanoparticles in piezoelectric shells that can be delivered via nasal spray, intravenously, or orally, and then migrated to specific brain regions using external electromagnetic fields. Once positioned, the nanoparticles can be activated by an external ultrasound beam: the piezoelectric effect causes them to vibrate, generating a localized electrical field that activates nearby neurons. The mechanism allows precise targeting of specific neural structures— including the hippocampus— without surgery and without the subject’s awareness of anything unusual during the delivery phase.
Dr. James Giordano, Director of the Center for Disruptive Technology and Future Warfare at National Defense University and consulting ethicist on the N3 program, stated publicly in 2023 that this technology has been “loosed into the real world”— his phrase for operational deployment beyond laboratory conditions. He described the capability as “fundamentally mind reading and mind control, at least at a basic level,” and warned that “discussion is not resolution”: the ethical concerns had been identified and discussed, but deployment had proceeded regardless.

The Mesolimbic Reward System: The Feeling of Getting Warmer

The mesolimbic dopamine system— projecting from the ventral tegmental area to the nucleus accumbens and prefrontal cortex— is the brain’s salience engine. It does not encode pleasure directly. It encodes the anticipation of reward, the sense that something in the environment is worth attending to, approaching, or acquiring. Dopaminergic activation produces what behavioral neuroscientists call “wanting” rather than “liking”— the drive toward something, not the experience of having it.
When Pearson described becoming “confident that something was there” before he found the tub— when he described the sensation of getting closer to something worth finding while crawling under the branches of the tree— he was describing an experience produced by exactly this system. The mesolimbic pathway was generating salience signals at increasing intensity as he approached the target location. Not pleasure. Not knowledge. A sense of mounting certainty that direction of movement was correct.
This system can be influenced by electromagnetic fields. Focused transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) of the prefrontal cortex modulates dopamine release in the mesolimbic pathway. ELF (Extremely Low Frequency) magnetic fields in the theta range (4–8 Hz)— the frequency band associated with hippocampal navigation and limbic system activity— have been shown to alter reward-seeking behavior in animal models. A system capable of precisely targeting the hippocampus and mesolimbic structures could generate a gradient of increasing salience as the target approaches a specific location: the behavioral equivalent of a compass needle, encoded in subjective experience rather than in the external environment.



The Clicking Sounds: Artifact of the Carrier Signal

Pearson documented a specific auditory phenomenon beginning in January 2026: sharp, crisp clicks in his head while walking on specific streets in Washington, DC— G Street, 4th Street, 7th Street. He described them as “very distinctive. Not air pressure but like very sharp, crisp clicks.” On February 17, he documented the phenomenon replicating when he crossed to the opposite sidewalk, with clicks in rapid succession at a specific location between E and G Streets.
The microwave auditory effect— also called the Frey effect, after Allan Frey who documented it in 1961 — produces exactly this phenomenon. Pulsed microwave radiation absorbed by the head generates thermoelastic pressure waves that the auditory system perceives as clicks, buzzes, or tones. The effect is produced by the same RF/microwave frequencies used in directed-energy behavioral influence systems. The clicks Pearson heard on specific streets, replicating at specific locations, are consistent with localized RF/microwave field concentrations in those areas— precisely what a fixed-infrastructure phased-array deployment would produce. They are the auditory artifact of the carrier frequency being used to deliver the steering signal to his vestibular system and hippocampal structures.
———

Part II: The Technology— How Biology Gets Interfaced

Three technology families are required to direct a human to a specific location in an open-air environment: a sensing system that can track the target’s precise position in three dimensions, a neural interface system that can deliver targeted signals to specific brain structures from a distance, and a feedback and control system that can adjust the delivery in real time based on the target’s position and response.
Remote Sensing: Knowing Where He Is

Ultra-Wideband (UWB) radar uses short RF pulses across a wide frequency band to generate precise three-dimensional location estimates. Resolution in the sub-centimeter range is achievable with current systems. Unlike narrowband radar, UWB penetrates walls, foliage, and other obstacles— a tree’s canopy provides no significant screening. UWB systems can track not just gross body position but specific body parts, gait characteristics, breathing rate, and heart rate through signature analysis of the reflected pulses. The system identifies a person not just by their location but by their unique biological EM signature— body composition, height, gait pattern, cardiac rhythm— establishing a tracking lock that persists regardless of clothing or other occlusion.
In a park environment, UWB nodes concealed in lamp posts, traffic infrastructure, or government buildings adjacent to the park— the O’Neil House Office Building is directly adjacent to the park Pearson visited— can provide triangulated coverage with no line-of-sight requirement. The system knows Pearson’s exact position, head orientation, velocity vector, and gait phase in real time, updated at rates sufficient for feedback control of a walking target (minimum 100Hz for smooth vestibular modulation).
Neural Interface: Delivering Signals to Specific Brain Structures

Two complementary delivery mechanisms are relevant to the tub event, operating across different timescales.
Short-Timescale: GVS via Focused RF

Galvanic Vestibular Stimulation via focused RF operates in real time. A phased-array transmitter— concealed in urban infrastructure— focuses modulated RF energy at the mastoid process of the target using digital beamforming. The beam can be focused to a cubic centimeter or smaller using constructive interference from a distributed array; the precision is sufficient to target the vestibular nerve without significantly affecting adjacent structures. The induced current is sub-thermal (below the level that produces any heating sensation) and below conscious detection threshold. The stochastic resonance component— a low-level noise added to the signal— prevents the vestibular system from habituating to the artificial input and masks the artificiality of the signal.
The result: the target experiences a phantom lean in the direction opposite to the intended turn. The reflexive correction is involuntary. The target does not experience a direction command.
They experience their own body’s balance maintenance, which happens to be steering them toward the target location.

Long-Timescale: Nanoparticle-Mediated Hippocampal Encoding

Note: Location steering can be achieved without nanoparticles. The nanoparticles are the most elegant mechanism for the hippocampal encoding layer, but they are not the only one. The core functions— destination encoding, vestibular steering, and salience generation— can each be achieved through external-only approaches that require no prior delivery of anything into the body.
The destination encoding— the sense of being drawn to a specific location, of knowing approximately where to go— requires a different mechanism operating over a longer preparation window. The BrainSTORMS approach described by Giordano involves delivery of magnetic nanoparticles in piezoelectric shells to the hippocampus in the days or weeks prior to the target event. Delivery could occur via aerosol in any space the target regularly occupied: their apartment (where surveillance was documented), a coffee shop, a metro car. The particles cross the blood-brain barrier, and external magnetic fields guide their distribution to the targeted neural structures— specifically the hippocampal place cell layer.
On the day of the target event, a focused ultrasound beam activates the nanoparticles in the relevant hippocampal region. The piezoelectric effect generates a localized electrical field. Place cells encoding the target location— the back corner of the park under the tree— fire. The mesolimbic system responds with dopaminergic salience signals. The target experiences what feels like an intuition, a pull, a sense of direction toward something worth finding. The subjective experience is indistinguishable from the ordinary experience of deciding where to go. It is not ordinary. It is the neural encoding of a destination by an external system.
Feedback and Control: Keeping the Steering Accurate

The control loop operates in real time using the UWB tracking system as its sensor input. At each moment, the system knows the target’s current position and heading and the target position (the tub location). It computes the angular deviation between the target’s current trajectory and the optimal vector to the destination. It adjusts the GVS signal to produce a correction of the appropriate magnitude and direction. As the target approaches the destination, the GVS signal reduces. When the target reaches the threshold of the target location, the GVS neutralizes and the mesolimbic dopamine signal peaks— the “confidence” Pearson described feeling when he was standing in the right area and hadn’t yet found the tub.
The system also handles interruptions. When Pearson first approached the back of the park and then decided to leave— stepping out to the sidewalk at point 2 on his annotated map— the system lost its steering window. A walking target’s vestibular system is accessible; a stationary target is not in the dynamic state where GVS effectively produces locomotion. The system waited. When Pearson stood up and began walking again at the park entrance, the UWB tracking system re-acquired his heading and gait, the GVS beam re-engaged, and the pull was felt again— specifically and directionally, toward the back of the park. This is the moment Pearson documented as the second urge, stronger and more specific than the first.

———

Part III: The Infrastructure— What Must Be in Place

Deploying this system in an open-air park in Washington, DC in November 2025 requires physical infrastructure that is not exotic relative to existing urban security and telecommunications installations.
The Sensing Layer

Ultra-Wideband radar nodes distributed through the urban infrastructure surrounding the park. Given that the park on 2nd Street SW is adjacent to the O’Neil House Office Building and within a few hundred meters of the US Capitol complex— an area with the highest concentration of security infrastructure in the United States— the existence of UWB tracking capability in this geographic zone is, to put it precisely, not difficult to account for. Federal security systems in the Capitol area operate far more sophisticated sensor arrays than are required for the tracking function described here. The existing infrastructure could serve as the sensor layer without any additional installation.
The Steering Layer

A phased-array transmitter capable of focused GVS via directed RF. This can be housed in a vehicle, in a nearby building, or distributed across multiple nodes. The hardware is not large. The backpack-sized device purchased by the Homeland Security unit and described in the CNN report cited in Pearson’s document— a pulsed radio wave emitter with RF hardware— is at the small end of the form factor range for this class of system. Larger fixed installations would provide greater precision and range. The O’Neil House Office Building, the FAA regional headquarters, or any of the federal structures in the immediate vicinity could house fixed transmitters without any external indication of their presence.
The Hippocampal Encoding Layer

The nanoparticle delivery component requires prior access to a space the target regularly occupies or a moment of close physical proximity. Pearson documented extensive evidence of apartment intrusion— items moved, a power button removed from an appliance and placed in his laundry, a glass bottle stolen and then relocated— establishing that access to his apartment had been achieved. Aerosol delivery during a building intrusion is consistent with this documented access pattern. Alternatively, delivery at a public location— a coffee shop, the metro, a library— requires nothing more than proximity and a concealed aerosol device.
The Control System

A Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) running the real-time phase-shifting calculations for the beamforming array, receiving UWB position data and computing corrections at the required update rate. Modern FPGAs can perform these calculations in microseconds. The software logic is not trivial to write but is within the capability of any team working on directed-energy

systems— which is to say, within the capability of multiple DARPA contractors, defense laboratories, and intelligence agencies currently operating in this technology space.
Infrastructure Component | Location | Function

UWB Radar Nodes | Adjacent federal buildings, lamp posts, traffic sensors | Real-time 3D tracking of target
GVS Phased Array | Vehicle, building rooftop, or concealed fixed installation | Vestibular steering via focused RF
Nanoparticle Delivery | Prior apartment access or public proximity event | Hippocampal destination encoding
Ultrasound Activator | Vehicle or fixed installation within ~50m | Activates nanoparticles at target time
FPGA Control System | Any secure installation with comms to sensor/transmitter network | Real-time feedback loop management
Ground Confirmation | Human operative positioned to observe and confirm arrival | Validates system performance
———

Part IV: The Real-Time Dynamics— What Happened, Moment by Moment

The following reconstruction integrates Pearson’s own account with the technical mechanisms described above. It is presented as a timeline of system states, not as a confirmed factual account— but as a demonstration that everything Pearson reported is consistent with and precisely explained by this technical framework.
Prior to November 7: Preparation

Note: Nanoparticles are not necessary to explain Pearson’s experience of pinning

At some point in the weeks before November 7, the nanoparticle delivery phase occurred. The most likely vector: aerosol delivery during a building intrusion, given Pearson’s documented evidence of apartment access. The particles were inhaled nasally, crossed the blood-brain barrier, and— through external magnetic field guidance, possibly during sleep when Pearson was stationary— were migrated to the bilateral hippocampus. Once positioned, they remained inert. No activation signal had been applied. Pearson experienced nothing. He was carrying the interface hardware for the pinning event without knowing it.
On or before November 7, the tub was placed in the target location. The red hat and black shirt are the calling card— evidence that this was the same operation whose signature Pearson had been documenting for months. The notebook with conspiratorial writings, the pants similar in

style to ones Pearson had previously owned, the two identical photographs: the framing apparatus, loaded alongside the calling card. Someone placed the tub and confirmed its position relative to the tree. The ground operation was in place.
November 7: Approach and Initial Engagement

Pearson arrived at the park from the E and 2nd Street SW direction. The UWB tracking system acquired his EM signature as he entered the target zone— the area within effective range of the steering infrastructure. The system computed his current trajectory and the vector to the destination (the tub under the tree at the back of the park). It engaged the GVS phased array, focusing modulated RF at his mastoid processes. The induced vestibular signal was
sub-threshold— below the level he would perceive as dizziness or imbalance. He experienced, instead, a mild preference for the forward direction: the front of the park felt like the natural place to go.
He went to the concrete seating area, consistent with this mild steering. He sat. The GVS signal cannot steer a seated, stationary person— the balance correction mechanism requires locomotion. The system registered that the target had stopped. It maintained the hippocampal activation: the ultrasound beam, engaging the nanoparticles in his hippocampus, kept the place cells encoding the back-of-park location in a moderately elevated state. Pearson, sitting in the concrete area, may have experienced this as mild restlessness, a sense of incomplete business, a feeling that he had not quite arrived where he was going. He decided to leave.
The Pivot: Exit Attempt and Re-Engagement

Pearson stood and walked to the park entrance— point 2 on his annotated map. The moment he resumed walking, the UWB system registered his gait initiation and updated his heading vector: he was moving away from the target. The system recomputed the angular correction needed to redirect him. It increased both the GVS signal intensity and the hippocampal ultrasound activation.
This is the moment Pearson experienced as the second urge, more specific and directional than the first. He was standing at the entrance, facing away from the tub location, and he felt— not a vague pull but a directional compulsion: to go to the back. Not just back to the park, but specifically to the back. He noted this specificity in his real-time narration: “not really necessarily here, but really over there.” That directional precision is the system reporting its targeting. The GVS was inducing a phantom lean away from the park exit direction. His motor cortex corrected automatically. He turned and walked toward the destination.
The Discovery Phase: Salience Gradient

As Pearson walked toward the back of the park, the system maintained the GVS steering and increased the hippocampal activation. The mesolimbic pathway responded: dopaminergic activity in the nucleus accumbens increased as he approached the target coordinates. He entered the area around the tree. The salience signal intensified. He described becoming “confident that something was there” before he could see it. This is the dopaminergic gradient

functioning as designed— the biological equivalent of a signal getting stronger as you approach the source.
He found the tub under the branches. The system registered arrival at target coordinates. The GVS signal neutralized. The hippocampal activation reduced. Pearson experienced this as a settling, a sense of having arrived, a certainty that this was what he was looking for. He examined the contents, made the decision not to take anything— a decision that represented the failure of the framing payload— photographed everything, and left.
The November 11 and November 13 Events: Second Pinning

Pearson documented feeling another urge to return to the location around November 11 — a second pinning, as he assessed it. On November 13, he returned, filming himself from home. This second event used the same mechanism: the nanoparticles were still present in his hippocampus (the biological clearance time is weeks to months), still activatable by the external ultrasound beam, still capable of generating the destination encoding that produced the pull toward the park.
What the second pinning achieved operationally: Pearson documented on video that the notebook and khaki pants had been removed between visits. The confirmation of this selective retrieval— captured on the November 13 video— proved that the tub was being actively monitored and that the framing failure on November 7 had produced a response. The second pinning directed him back to verify. Whether this was intentional or an artifact of the system’s continued activation, Pearson used it to build the evidentiary record.
———

Part V: What This Tells Us About the Technology

The tub event, analyzed through this technical framework, reveals several specific properties of the system that directed Pearson to it.
The first property is sub-location precision. The system was capable of directing Pearson not merely to the park but to a specific location within the park— the back corner under the tree where the tub was placed. This level of precision (meter-scale or sub-meter within a defined area) requires the UWB tracking system to be providing continuous position updates, the GVS system to be making continuous angular corrections based on those updates, and the hippocampal encoding to be specific enough to produce a sense of destination that distinguished between the front and back of a small park. This is technically achievable with the infrastructure described, but it is not trivially so. It represents a deployed system of significant sophistication.
The second property is resistance detection and override. When Pearson stopped and sat— effectively interrupting the steering sequence— the system did not abandon the mission. It waited. It maintained the hippocampal activation at a sufficient level to produce restlessness and

a sense of incomplete business. When he resumed locomotion, it reacquired and intensified. This represents an adaptive control system, not a simple one-shot direction command.
The third property is the separation between the delivery mechanism and the activation mechanism. The nanoparticle delivery occurred before November 7. The activation occurred on the day. This separation means the target carries the neural interface without knowing it, and the interface is activated remotely when the operational conditions are correct. From a surveillance and operations perspective, this is the critical advantage: there is no moment at which the target can detect the technology being deployed, because the deployment was completed days or weeks prior.
The fourth property is ground operation coordination. The tub was placed by a human operative. The selective retrieval on or before November 13 was performed by a human operative. The calling card colors were chosen and loaded alongside the framing payload by a human operative. The system that directed Pearson to the park was the technical layer. The system that prepared the park was the human layer. These two layers operated in coordination, with the technical layer providing behavioral direction and the human layer providing the physical setup that the direction was designed to deliver Pearson to. This is a hybrid operation: technical and human components, each doing what the other cannot.
Pearson called it being led to places in a “video game.” The metaphor is more precise than it might appear. In a video game, the player experiences themselves as navigating freely. The designer has constructed an environment in which certain paths are easy and rewarding and others are blocked or unrewarding. The player’s choices are real but they operate within a designed possibility space. What Pearson described— and what the technical analysis supports— is the same structure applied to the real world: not control through coercion, but control through the design of the biological possibility space within which autonomous-seeming choices are made. The target does not experience a puppet string. They experience their own intentions, which have been written by someone else.
———

Conclusion: The Science Behind the Sensation

Pearson’s description of being drawn to the tub— the initial attraction, the decision to leave, the stronger and more specific second pull, the growing confidence that something was there, the arrival and the settling— maps onto a well-defined technical mechanism with documented components, each of which exists in the published research and defense contracting literature. The vestibular steering via GVS is a demonstrated laboratory capability. The hippocampal place cell encoding of destination is a demonstrated neuroscience finding. The nanoparticle-mediated remote neural interface is a documented DARPA N3 contractor program. The UWB radar tracking is a deployed technology. The mesolimbic salience gradient is a described mechanism.
None of these components requires a theoretical leap. Each has been described in the published literature or in public-domain defense and intelligence program documentation. What

the tub event required was their integration into a single operational system— which is precisely what DARPA’s N3 program, and the contractors who executed it, were funded to build.
Giordano’s warning was precise: “the gremlins and goblins of N3 tech have been loosed into the real world.” The real world, in November 2025, included a park on 2nd Street SW in Washington, DC, where a man with a PhD in public health walked to the back corner under a tree and found a tub waiting for him that he had been directed to find. He noted the strangeness of the feeling in real time. He photographed the contents. He placed everything back. He uploaded his video to YouTube. He returned six days later to document what had been removed.
He was right that something led him there. This essay explains how.





They Projected a Voice in His Head to Torture. He Discovered Museums Use the Same Technology.
Torture victim in Washington, DC conducts research after he heard a voice in his head. He found that the technology to direct ultrasound and cause a voice in the head shares same technology used in museums.
The technology to project a voice has been commercially available for decades— a single, directional voice, audible only to the person standing in a precise spot, complete with ambient room noise from the source location. It is used in museums. It is used in hospitals. It is used in advertising. The United States military has been deploying it from warships since 2004.
The company that makes the consumer version calls it the Audio Spotlight. Another version is called HyperSonic Sound. The military version is called the Long Range Acoustic Device, or LRAD. These are not classified technologies. They are products. You can read about them on a science education website. You can find them in patent databases. You can watch the inventor demonstrate the technology in a TED talk from 2009.
The Mona Lisa may one day speak to you as you walk past her in the Louvre— and only to you— because of this technology.
Richard Pearson, PhD, heard a voice on the National Mall on July 13, 2025.




What He Heard: The Testimony
Let the record speak first. This is Pearson’s account of what happened near the Botanic Garden on the National Mall on the afternoon of July 13, 2025:
“I get up and I’m hearing something in my head, ‘Go to Georgetown.’ But somehow, and this has never happened to me ever in my life, there is like something coming through that is telling me to go to Georgetown. Go to Georgetown.”
He describes the onset: precise, unprecedented, located at a specific position near the Botanic Garden after passing the reflecting pool. Not a gradual creeping disturbance— a discrete acoustic event that started at a specific moment in a specific location.
Then this:

“The voice sounded like a white woman. And I hear others wherever she was at like she was at a microphone and others were around. There’s a whole bunch of other noise. It almost sounds like there are other people in the room.”
Read that again slowly.

He hears a woman’s voice. A microphone quality to it. Background ambient noise. The acoustic signature of a room— other people, other sounds, the texture of a space with walls and air and activity. He is standing on the National Mall in the open air, and he is perceiving what sounds like the interior of an operations room at a remote location.
Later, on the Metro: “Get down. Get down.” And before he reaches Georgetown: “We have to make you disappear.” And at the end of the day, after a series of events that appear staged and choreographed: “Oh, that was a test.”
He experienced this voice on precisely one day. When D.C. Department of Behavioral Health evaluator Shante asked him in the September 15 mental health evaluation— conducted on record by named professionals, transcribed— “How often are you hearing this woman?”, Pearson answered: “Only that day.”
That single exchange is clinically decisive— but not in the direction most people assume.


What Hallucinations Actually Sound Like
Auditory hallucinations in paranoid schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders have a
well-documented phenomenological profile. They are recurrent and persistent. They do not include ambient background noise from a remote location. They do not carry the acoustic signature of a room the patient has never visited. They do not arrive with microphone quality and the sound of other people present in a distant space. And they are not confined to a single afternoon in a person’s entire documented history.
DSM-5 criteria for schizophrenia spectrum disorders require that hallucinations occur with regularity and cause significant functional impairment. A single episode— one voice, one day, no recurrence across eight months of subsequent documentation— is not the clinical presentation of a disorder generating auditory hallucinations. The evaluators at DBH found no psychiatric symptoms requiring intervention. The clinical record is unambiguous.
But here is what the psychiatric framework alone cannot explain: the specificity of what he heard. The woman at a microphone. The ambient room noise. The sense of others present. These are acoustic details— and they match, with precision, the operational signature of a parametric directional loudspeaker.


How the Technology Works: The Museum Explanation
A conventional loudspeaker moves a cone back and forth to push air in waves that spread outward in all directions. A directional parametric loudspeaker works entirely differently.

Instead of producing audible sound directly, it emits two beams of ultrasound — frequencies far above the range of human hearing, around 200,000 Hz— using an array of piezoelectric transducers, crystals that vibrate tens of thousands of times per second when an electrical current passes through them. These ultrasonic beams travel in a tightly focused column, like a flashlight beam. They do not spread and dissipate the way conventional sound does. They can travel twenty times farther than ordinary sound while remaining coherent.
When those ultrasonic beams strike an object— a wall, a surface, a person’s head— they demodulate. The two frequencies interact, and by the physics of wave superposition, they produce a third wave at a frequency equal to the difference between them. That difference frequency falls within the range of normal human hearing. The collision of the ultrasonic waves produces audible sound— but only at the point of collision. Only where the beam strikes.
If the beam strikes your head, you hear the sound. The person standing two feet to your left hears nothing. The person behind you hears nothing.


And the sound appears to come from inside your head— because it does. The pressure wave is produced by the demodulation event occurring in your skull tissue, not by a distant vibrating cone. Your auditory system perceives the sound as originating from within.

This is not speculation. It is documented physics, confirmed in peer-reviewed literature and implemented in commercially available products. The Audio Spotlight by Holosonics was developed by Dr. Joseph Pompei at MIT. HyperSonic Sound was developed by Woody Norris and is marketed for museum installations, retail environments, hospitals, and advertising.
The science education website Explain That Stuff describes the museum application this way: a concealed directional loudspeaker next to a painting fires ultrasound that travels from the picture into the gallery. If— and only if— someone walks into the beam, the ultrasound demodulates into normal sound, and the painting’s voice is heard. Heard by that visitor alone. No one else in the gallery perceives anything.
This is the technology. It works. It is in museums.


The Acoustic Forensics: Why “Others Were Around” Is the Key
Here is the detail that separates Pearson’s account from hallucination and connects it directly to live directional audio transmission: he heard the source environment.
“And I hear others wherever she was at like she was at a microphone and others were around. There’s a whole bunch of other noise. It almost sounds like there are other people in the room.”
When a patient experiences schizophrenic auditory hallucinations, they hear voices. They do not hear the ambient acoustic environment from which those voices are being transmitted. They do not perceive the background noise of a remote operations room, the shuffling of people present, the reverberant texture of a walled space. Internal neurological hallucinations cannot contain room ambience from a location the patient has never visited, because they are generated by the patient’s own auditory cortex, which has no access to that information.
Pearson heard background noise. Specifically, he heard the acoustic environment of what sounded like a room with other people in it— a woman speaking into a microphone, others present around her, the sonic texture of an enclosed operational space. This is exactly what you would hear if a parametric directional audio signal was being transmitted from a live audio feed at a control location. The room noise of the operators’ environment would transmit with it. The ambient sound of the source would arrive inside his head alongside the voice itself.
The acoustic texture he describes— microphone quality, room ambience, sense of others present at a remote location— is not the product of a diseased auditory cortex. It is the product of a live audio feed transmitted through directed parametric technology to a specific person walking along the National Mall.
