“I am trying to negotiate for my release,” Pearson wrote in the summer of 2025, attempting to explain his situation to a friend from whom he was begging money to reach the Canadian border. There is a precise historical resonance to that phrase. To negotiate for your release when no one has charged you with anything, when no court has adjudicated anything, when no official has acknowledged your existence as an operational subject— is to be in the position of the French citizen who finds themselves before the Revolutionary Tribunal and realizes, in the moment of speaking, that the form of a trial is present but its substance has been abolished.
He lobbied dozens of congressional offices in December 2025. The ACLU declined his case— politely, noting “limited resources.” Iceland refused his attempt at asylum— the United States, they said, is a “safe country” of origin. Every institution designed to address what was being done to him returned the same answer: not our problem, not our jurisdiction, not our case. The Committee of Public Safety, at least, issued warrants. Pearson’s designation was issued in silence, executed in silence, and denied through silence.


The Classified Guillotine: Military-Grade Weapons on Domestic Soil
The specific evil of the Reign of Terror — as opposed to ordinary political repression— was the use of the full coercive apparatus of the state against citizens whose only offense was political existence. The guillotine was a public instrument, used in public, in the name of the Republic,

against people who had been designated enemies of that Republic by a committee of that Republic’s government.
What distinguishes what was done to Richard Pearson is that the coercive apparatus employed against him was not the ordinary machinery of state violence— arrest, prosecution, imprisonment— but classified military-grade weapons designed for deployment against foreign adversaries, now pointed inward, at a domestic civilian, in a city that the Constitution assigns to Congress and that the Secret Service manages as a security zone.
The DoW CTO confirmed on January 23, 2026 that the Department of War has directed energy weapons and is scaling them. The Washington Post confirmed on February 19, 2026 that these weapons— CHAMP, HIJENKS, acoustic devices, pulsed electromagnetic systems— are active, deployed, and capable of disabling electronics, disrupting human physiology, and producing the full symptom cluster of Havana syndrome. The National Academies of Science identified directed energy as the most plausible mechanism for Havana syndrome in 2020. The Biden NSC affirmed that pulsed electromagnetic or acoustic energy remains a plausible explanation in certain cases.
Pearson was experiencing the Havana syndrome symptom cluster— directional acoustic phenomena at reproducible coordinates, pressure, clicking, auditory anomalies— in D.C. in January 2026, simultaneously with the month in which the Pentagon was briefing Congress on the pulsed radio wave device it had covertly purchased for exactly that purpose. He was describing, from inside his daily life, the civilian application of the same weapons the U.S. government was deploying against Venezuelan presidential security forces. The Venezuela operation was a military operation, publicly claimed, against a foreign adversary. The D.C. operation was a covert operation, officially denied, against an American citizen in his own studio apartment.
The difference between a foreign battlefield and a domestic studio apartment is not a technical limitation of the weapons involved. It is a legal, moral, and constitutional boundary. The DoW CTO’s tweet and Trump’s boast about the “Discombobulator” announced publicly that the United States government has weapons capable of doing to a human nervous system what Pearson says was done to his. The question of whether those weapons were pointed at Georgetown as well as Caracas is not a technical question. It is a constitutional one.
The Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishment. The Fifth Amendment prohibits deprivation of liberty without due process. The First Amendment prohibits the suppression of political speech. The Fourth Amendment prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures of a person’s physical and digital space. None of these provisions contain an exception for weapons delivered at the speed of light, through walls, without physical contact, leaving no forensic trace. The invisibility of the instrument does not alter the constitutionality of its use. It only makes the constitutional violation impossible to prove.


The World Is Watching: Pearson’s Own Warning
“At the same time that the US is going around the world claiming to want to free people,” Pearson wrote in his document, “it is using military weapons to torture its own citizens, to invade their brains, and to operate lawlessly. But here is the reality and warning. The world is watching.”
This is not the rhetoric of a delusional man. It is the geopolitical observation of a scholar who understands what legitimacy means and what its loss costs. Pearson on moral and legal authority:
“Legal authority and moral authority are inextricably linked in liberal democracies. Some academicians have attempted to separate the two to allow for legal authority to be legitimate in the face of absolute power, violence, and human rights violations. That is a mistake. At the point that legal authority fails to account for moral authority— a moral code, constitutional guarantees, civil liberties— it cedes its legal authority. By what authority does the exercise of legal power then arise if not for state law or morality? Untethered from constraints, actions become capricious, unbounded, and apt to tyranny. Once a liberal democracy transitions from ensuring universal rights as defined in their constitution, a framework of legal authority is irrelevant. Only power becomes relevant— one person or one faction who decides who lives or who dies, who is oppressed or privileged. The US has to decide what it wants to be and what its future holds. The current course is untenable.”
The United States has been the primary global advocate for human rights norms that prohibit exactly what Pearson describes— political targeting, covert torture, the weaponization of state power against designated categories of citizen. It prosecutes other governments for doing these things. It conditions foreign aid and diplomatic relationships on compliance with exactly these standards. It invokes the language of freedom, dignity, and the rule of law in every international forum.
And simultaneously— if Pearson’s account is even partially accurate— it was using classified military weapons to torture a Black academic in a studio apartment in D.C., because his dissertation expressed opinions that the executive branch had designated ideologically unacceptable. Despite the administration’s claims to the contrary, Pearson maintains that race has no biological basis.
The Reign of Terror ended not because its victims prevailed but because Robespierre was disfavored by men who feared they would be next. COINTELPRO ended not because its targets were vindicated but because documents were stolen and leaked and the institutional embarrassment became untenable. The pattern of American state terror against its own citizens has never ended through the triumph of legal remedy. It has paused, evolved, and resumed— each iteration more sophisticated, more deniable, and more technologically insulated from the accountability that exposed its predecessors.

Richard Pearson has been subjected to something the Committee of Public Safety, with its primitive technology and its public warrants, could not have imagined: a Reign of Terror so silent, so intimate, so precisely calibrated to the interior of a single human life, that the terror itself cannot be seen by anyone but its target.
The Reign of Terror killed thousands in the streets of Paris. The 2025 upgrade aims to kill one man’s mind— his credibility, his sanity, his mental sovereignty, his ability to occupy a social position from which he can be believed— while he walks the streets of the capital of the most powerful democracy in the world, carrying a phone with timestamps, taking photographs, citing academic literature, passing mental health evaluations, and writing everything down.
The guillotine was the instrument of a state that had lost its fear of being seen. What is being done to Richard Pearson is the instrument of a state that has learned, in fifty years since COINTELPRO, exactly how to remain unseen.
The world is watching. It cannot see what it is watching. That is the point.


Essay draws on Richard Pearson’s document “A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance in 2025”; Darton Arnold, “Military Weapons Targeting Human Brain Being Used on US Soil: Evidence Favoring Richard Pearson’s Account” (2026); David Ignatius, “The ‘Discombobulator’ arms race has begun,” The Washington Post, February 19, 2026; the January 23, 2026 tweet by @DoWCTO via @DeptofWar; the Church Committee Final Reports (1976); the Law of 22 Prairial, Year II (June 10, 1794); the FBI COINTELPRO documents declassified following the 1971 Media, Pennsylvania burglary; the National Academies of Science report on Havana Syndrome (2020); the Convention Against Torture; and the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789).




Military Weapons Targeting Human Brain Being Used on US Soil: Evidence Favoring Richard Pearson’s Account
The Discombobulator and the Citizen: Richard Pearson, the
Directed-Energy Arms Race, and the Technologies He Suspected First The Boast
On January 24, 2026, President Donald Trump sat for an interview with the New York Post and
said more than he was supposed to. Asked about the January 3 operation in which U.S. forces penetrated Venezuelan air defenses and extracted President Nicolás Maduro, Trump named the weapon.

“The Discombobulator.”

“I’m not allowed to talk about it. I would love to. … They never got their rockets off. They had Russian and Chinese rockets, and they never got one off. We came in, they pressed buttons, and nothing worked.”
The day before, on January 23, the Department of War’s Chief Technology Officer had already said the quiet part loudly:
“Yes, the @DeptofWar has directed energy weapons. Yes, we are scaling them.”

The Washington Post, reporting on these disclosures on February 19, 2026, named the moment plainly: the directed-energy arms race has begun. High-powered microwaves, acoustic weapons, pulsed electromagnetic systems— technologies long confined to classified programs and academic papers— had arrived in the public record through presidential boasts, social media announcements, and the wreckage of Venezuelan air defenses.
Somewhere in Washington, D.C., Richard Pearson, PhD, had been trying to describe these same technologies to anyone who would listen since the summer of 2025. He had visited dozens of congressional offices. He had written to a journalist. He had passed two mental health evaluations. He had sought asylum in Iceland.
The government was now advertising what he had been documenting.

The crucial point— and the one that distinguishes Pearson’s account from the Venezuela story— is this: Pearson was not describing incapacitation. He was not describing a battlefield weapon. He was describing something far more specific, far more intimate, and, in many ways, far more difficult to prove: a sustained campaign of behavioral influence, location tracking, audio surveillance, acoustic harassment, and neurological disruption, conducted covertly, against a single civilian, inside his own life. The technologies he named are not the blunt instruments used to neutralize a presidential security detail. They are the precision instruments. The quiet ones. The ones designed to leave no mark.


The Technology Pearson Named, and What the Record Now Confirms Voice-to-Skull Transmission
On July 13, 2025, at the National Mall, Pearson heard an internal voice he had never
experienced before— directing him, with specificity and urgency, to travel to Georgetown. “Go to Georgetown. Go to Georgetown.” It said things. It gave instructions. It told him, before he arrived, “We have to make you disappear.” On the metro, it told him to crouch.
He was explicit about what he believed this was and was not. In his formal mental health interview with D.C. Department of Behavioral Health clinicians, he stated: “The voice in my

head, which is not psychosis because we know that this kind of technology can happen and does happen. MK Ultra is a good example.” He was invoking, by name, a classified government program that used unwitting American subjects. He was not diagnosing himself. He was identifying a technical category.
Voice-to-skull technology— the transmission of intelligible audio directly into the auditory cortex through pulsed microwave or ultrasonic means, sometimes called the microwave auditory effect or the Frey effect— is not science fiction. It is documented in the 1998 U.S. Army War College paper The Mind Has No Firewall, which Pearson cites directly, and has been the subject of military research for decades. The Washington Post, reporting on the Venezuela operation, identifies the weapon family in question as using “high-powered microwaves to disrupt electronics”— CHAMP, HIJENKS, and unnamed systems consistent with what Trump called “the Discombobulator.” High-powered microwave systems are the same physics family as microwave auditory transmission. The platform that can fry electronics through a wall can, at different power settings and frequencies, induce sound in a human auditory system without any external audio source.
Pearson was not claiming he heard God. He was claiming he heard a technology he had already researched and named. The DoW CTO confirmed the government has it and is scaling it.

Pinning: Being Pulled to a Location
What Pearson calls “pinning” is not passive surveillance. It is active compulsion— being technologically directed, or pulled, toward a specific location that serves operational purposes. He identifies multiple incidents throughout 2025 as pinning events, and his analysis of them is precise.
On July 17, 2025, he arrived in Adams Morgan— a neighborhood he had visited many times but rarely sought out deliberately. He writes: “I believe that this was a pinning event— to get me to Adams Morgan/Cleveland Park for harassment and a second extraction attempt. Organizing an on-the-ground operation on the spur of the moment seems improbable given that: 1) I chose Adams Morgan quite extemporaneously, 2) came directly from Chinatown where I had been taking photos, and 3) the general itinerant nature of my comings-and-goings.” The operational apparatus waiting for him there could not have been assembled on improvisation. His arrival had been arranged. The question was by what means.
On August 4, he ended up in Clarendon, Virginia— a suburb he had no attachment to and had visited perhaps twice in a decade. He writes flatly: “I don’t think about Clarendon ever.” He cannot account for why he went. On August 6, he found himself at RFK Stadium, where he encountered arranged graffiti— a racial slur spelled out across several concrete barriers. He writes: “Retrospectively, I believe that pinning technology very well could have been used.”
Perhaps, one of the clearest “tests” for pinning occurred at a park— initially resisting the urge then relented, only to find a cache of red and black patterned clothing that had become a calling

card in the torture campaign against him. Pearson arrives to park where he is feeling “pulled”. This pull is very specific— the back of the park. Instead, he goes to the concrete platform (1), then decides to leave (2) after several minutes. Standing on the sidewalk, he still has a “pull” so he goes to the back (3). There, he finds the tub.





Pinning, as Pearson understands it, is behavioral direction at a distance: the capacity to compel a person toward a destination without that person understanding they are being compelled. The experience feels like ordinary impulse, curiosity, or aimlessness. The destination has been

chosen by someone else, and the ground operation is waiting. He describes it: “It’s crazy! Got me in this video game and leading me to places. It is so inconceivable! Then I get there and messages and threats. It sounds like a movie but it’s not.”
The technology family that could produce this— transcranial electromagnetic stimulation, ultrasonic influence on limbic and prefrontal decision-making systems, the induction of specific drives through directed energy— is documented in the same military neuroscience literature Pearson cites. It is not the blunt incapacitation used in Venezuela. It is the subtle manipulation of the subjective experience of choice.

Through-Floor Tracking: The Apartment as Surveillance Grid
Separate from pinning— and operating at an entirely different scale— is what Pearson documents as real-time location tracking within his own apartment. This is not compulsion toward a destination. It is monitoring so precise it follows him from room to room, closet to closet, and into his most private moments.
He moved his bed to the other side of the studio. The noise from above followed. He moved his mattress to the walk-in closet. The noise followed. He moved to the coat closet near the front door. The noise followed. He moved back to the living room after midnight. After a beat of silence, the noise followed. He wrote in January 2026: “They have some kind of advanced technology that sees through the floor. I am absolutely certain of it. I have too many notes going back too far for there not to be some equipment that is following me.”
By February 2026, he was documenting this with timestamps across single days: objects dropped above his head at 1:20pm, 1:25pm, 1:27pm, 1:51pm, 1:54pm, 2:02pm, 2:05pm, 2:10pm, 2:15pm, 2:18pm, 2:22pm— each entry corresponding to his position within the apartment. He recorded audio on his laptop and noted that the upstairs residents switched from heavy to lighter objects when the recorder was running, “presumably so that I don’t pick up the sound”— a real-time behavioral adaptation implying awareness of what he was doing as he did it. He named it, quoting Giordano directly: “It is possible to disrupt an individual at every level— from their cellular function to entire bodily systems.”
Through-wall and through-floor radar is established military technology. Ground-penetrating radar, millimeter-wave imaging, and impulse radar systems capable of detecting a person’s position— and physiological state— through structural materials are documented in open defense research. Pearson wrote to his property manager: “There must be some technology that she is using to detect me in my apartment. I have researched it as it appears to be relatively cheap.”

Audio Surveillance: Confirmed by a Source Inside the Operation
On approximately August 26, 2025, a man Pearson calls “Lee”— whose Starbucks cup bore that name, and whose speech Pearson identifies as demonstrating the economy and evasion that “results from training”— sat beside him at the outdoor seating at Potbelly’s near Federal Center

and delivered a series of coded warnings. The most significant: “They are listening… They think it’s ok.”
Lee did not explain who “they” were. He did not name an agency. He spoke, Pearson writes, in the style of a man at the edge of what protocol permits him to say. His other statements were consistent with this framing: “Accountable for what you know.” “Once you see, you can’t unsee.” “You will see me again— maybe in another form.” “Travel light.”
Pearson’s confirmation of the audio surveillance came seventeen days later. On September 11, 2025, walking from the Anacostia trail, a man in the passenger seat of a passing truck screamed at him: “You do talk like that!”— a direct quotation of language Pearson had used privately inside his apartment, talking to himself, after accepting Lee’s warning and attempting to moderate his self-talk. He had never met this man. No one outside his apartment walls could have heard what he said. The truck did not stop.
The Washington Post reports that the Russian surveillance installation across from the U.S. Embassy in Moscow— nicknamed “Our Lady of Telemetry” —“bristled with every type of technical monitoring device known to man, including lasers and heat sensors.” Former CIA Moscow station chief Rolf Mowatt-Larssen describes decades of Russian technical surveillance that U.S. intelligence consistently underestimated. What Russia built against U.S. diplomatic personnel, the U.S. built against Russia— and has been scaling. The same architecture that allows directional audio surveillance of a foreign compound allows directional audio surveillance of a domestic apartment. Pearson was describing the civilian application of the same technical capability.

Acoustic Clicking: The Havana Syndrome Symptom Set
The Washington Post article describes the first documented Havana syndrome incident: “An individual assigned to the U.S. Embassy in Cuba was awakened one night at home in Havana in 2016 by severe pain and a sensation of intense pressure in the face, a loud piercing sound in one ear with directional features, and acute disequilibrium and nausea.”
Beginning in January 2026 — the same month the Pentagon was briefing Congress on the covertly acquired backpack-sized pulsed radio wave device it was testing as a possible Havana syndrome weapon— Pearson began experiencing sharp, crisp clicking sounds while walking specific streets near his Southwest D.C. residence. “Not air pressure,” he writes, “but like very sharp, crisp clicks.” He documented sequences of six to eight clicks. He systematically eliminated two mundane explanations through experimentation: a loose screw in his sunglasses, and a sound from his own teeth. Neither held up under repeated testing.
The key detail is reproducibility. The clicks occurred at specific locations and recurred when Pearson walked the same routes. He photographed his precise location each time to establish the coordinates. He confirmed the replication across multiple dates.

This is not a description of a hallucination. Hallucinations do not replicate at specific GPS coordinates across multiple days. This is a description of a localized directed energy field— acoustic or electromagnetic— emitting from a fixed point and intercepted by a moving person. The National Academies of Science, in its 2020 study, identified directed energy as “the most plausible mechanism” behind Havana syndrome. The Biden NSC, in its final weeks, affirmed that “pulsed electromagnetic or acoustic energy remains a plausible explanation in certain cases.” The Pentagon purchased the device. Pearson was experiencing the symptoms— at specific, documented, reproducible locations in the nation’s capital— months before any of this became public.

Behavioral Influence: Personality, Consciousness
By January 2026, reviewing this period against everything that followed, he reframes it: “The use of this technology can cause several changes to one’s personality, behaviors, consciousness, and spirituality. It can be similar to what victims of MKUltra reported— having a religious experience. But eventually, things revert back to their normal state. The change from normal to heightened experiences is a consequence of somatic and mental violation. It is like a hallucinogenic without the drugs. I do not do drugs and sometimes drink alcohol, never excessively. I gather that it is an electromagnetic technology that is beamed on people.”
He also notes the escalation from subtle to overt. In summer 2025, he became more irascible— quick-tempered in ways he did not recognize as himself. “I saw massive changes in my personality and behaviors.” He identifies these changes not as mental illness— the mental health evaluators agreed— but as the product of prolonged electromagnetic influence on cognition and affect.
The induction of altered emotional and cognitive states through directed energy— targeted influence on the limbic system, the prefrontal cortex, the autonomic nervous system— appears across the military neuroscience literature Pearson cites. It is among the most consequential and least detectable capabilities in the directed energy field, because its effects are indistinguishable, to any external observer and often to the subject themselves, from ordinary human variability.

Somatic Stimulation: The Category He Almost Didn’t Name
He describes “odd simulations”— a “warming sensation”. He quotes Giordano: “It is possible to disrupt an individual at every level— from their cellular function to entire bodily systems.” He connects the experience explicitly to central nervous system targeting documented in the neuroweapons literature, identifying it not as a symptom of pathology but as an effect produced from outside his body.
The deliberate induction of involuntary somatic experience through directed electromagnetic energy has appeared in weapons research literature for decades, precisely because its psychological effects— humiliation, disorientation, the loss of sovereignty over one’s own body— are devastating and undetectable. Pearson understood this. He named it anyway,

despite the cost of naming it, because he understood that documentation was the only tool he had.

Device and Platform Interference
In August 2025, Pearson was using OpenAI’s SORA to communicate his experience of imprisonment. He was typing: “They have me imprisoned and will not let me go.” The word “NO” appeared in the prompt window. He had not typed it. He checked his hand placement: correct. He continued typing and it happened again. On the third attempt, no “NO” appeared— but the generated image depicted a prominent political figure behind bars with the words “Against the US Constitution.”
He documented this with a screenshot. Whether the interference was network-level manipulation, platform intervention, or a technical glitch, Pearson’s instinct— to document, to note, to screenshot— is characteristic of his approach throughout. He does not overclaim. He records what he observed and flags it for what it might mean.


The Washington Post describes HIJENKS, a Navy and Air Force high-powered microwave weapon, as capable of “disabling of computer systems, damaging targeted electronics, disrupting security and industrial control systems.” A directed energy system capable of frying Venezuelan air defense electronics is technically capable of interfering with a laptop’s wireless connection or input response. Pearson noted the anomaly. He did not insist on an explanation. He preserved the evidence.

Tracking via Personal Possessions
Pearson’s hat became a persistent object of operational interest in his account. A coffee shop manager asked, without apparent reason, “Did you lose your hat? I have one identical to yours.” The hat appeared in other incidents: a woman in Safeway wearing an identical one, who told him “People are very upset about your hat.” Dogs became aggressive upon seeing it specifically— one owner told him it was the “flop” causing the reaction.
He writes in his evidence summary: “My hat, though not forensically tested for a GPS device, was subjected to an odd exchange.” He does not insist the hat contains a tracker. He notes the pattern and flags the hypothesis.
A distinctive glass bottle purchased in Bethesda on July 26 disappeared. It reappeared on August 22 on a vendor’s table at a community event in Greenleaf Park, with the same identifying scratch on the logo. The vendor could not account for its presence. Pearson photographed it, noted it, and left it— its top was missing.
These incidents, taken individually, are dismissible. Taken as a pattern alongside everything else— the real-time location tracking within his apartment, the man who quoted his private speech back at him, the systematic harassment that followed his movements across D.C. suburbs— they suggest a surveillance network with persistent, real-time access to his location and his belongings. The technology to track a person through a tagged object or a device introduced into their possessions is not exotic. It is available. It is the infrastructure of modern intelligence.


The Literature He Built His Framework From
Pearson was not reasoning from intuition. His document includes a detailed reading list, presented in a letter to CNN journalist Bill Weir, that reflects genuine scholarly engagement with an emerging field:
Dr. James Giordano on neuroweapons, including the capacity to disrupt “from cellular function to entire bodily systems”
“The Mind Has No Firewall” (1998), U.S. Army War College Quarterly, providing theoretical grounding for what was then emerging technology

“Neurowar is Here” (2021), Naval Postgraduate School thesis, a technical deep-dive into neurowarfare applications
The Baker Institute report on “Neuroshields” for national security
A November 2025 Guardian article: “Mind-altering ‘brain weapons’ no longer only science fiction, say researchers”
He also raises a series of structural legal questions that reveal sophisticated understanding of why these cases cannot be adjudicated: the doctrine of sovereign immunity; the collapse of Bivens claims; the absence of congressional oversight of neuroweapons development; the deliberately untraceable nature of the weapons as a design feature rather than a limitation. He writes: “Many forms of neuroweapons are not traceable and can leave injury without touch.
Liberal democratic legal and political frameworks were not designed with this threat in mind.”

The Washington Post’s arms race article arrives at the same conclusion from the other direction: the weapons are real, they are scaling, and the world has no rules for them yet. Pearson arrived there first, from inside an operation that strongly points to US intelligence.


The Arms Race That Already Happened to One Person
The Washington Post frames the directed-energy arms race as a future danger requiring future rules. It  calls  for distinguishing  between  military  targets,  diplomatic  personnel, and— implicitly— some protected category of civilian. Its warning is prospective: “What goes around comes around.”
But for Richard Pearson, the arms race is not a metaphor for geopolitical risk— it is a tool for domestic torture and oppression. “At the same time that the US is going around the world claiming to want to free people, it is using military weapons to torture its own citizens, to invade their brains, and to operate lawlessly. But here is the reality and warning. The world is watching.”
He experienced voice-to-skull audio he had never heard before. He was tracked through the floors and walls of his own home. His private speech was intercepted and quoted back to him. He experienced acoustic phenomena at reproducible street coordinates consistent with the Havana syndrome symptom cluster. He experienced involuntary somatic responses he could not control and could barely bring himself to describe. He was behaviorally directed to locations he had no ordinary reason to visit. His possessions were accessed, tracked, and returned.
He documented all of it. He cited the relevant research. He lobbied Congress. He told a journalist. He passed his mental health evaluations. And he remained, in the official record, invisible— dismissed by the ACLU, refused asylum by Iceland, unreported in any publication.

The DoW CTO’s tweet on January 23, 2026 did not create the technology Pearson was describing. It confirmed it. The Washington Post’s February 19 article did not invent the danger Pearson was warning about. It named it, gave it a market valuation, and assigned it a nickname.
What it has not yet done— what the arms race debate has not yet done— is account for what happens when those weapons, confirmed and scaling, are directed not at a military base in Caracas, but at a studio apartment in Southwest D.C., against a Black academic with a liberation philosophy dissertation, by an administration that had already named people like him as domestic enemies.
Richard Pearson was asking that question in July 2025.

The government answered it, inadvertently, in January 2026.

The Washington Post is asking it now, carefully, about Venezuela and diplomats and geopolitical rivals.
No one, yet, has asked it about Richard Pearson.


Essay draws on Richard Pearson’s document “A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance in 2025”; David Ignatius, “The ‘Discombobulator’ arms race has begun,” The Washington Post, February 19, 2026; the January 23, 2026 tweet by @DoWCTO via @DeptofWar; CNN/Newser reporting on the Pentagon’s pulsed radio wave device (January 13, 2026); and academic sources cited by Pearson including Dr. James Giordano on neuroweapons, TL Thomas’s “The Mind Has No Firewall” (U.S. Army War College, 1998), and the Naval Postgraduate School thesis “Neurowar is Here” (2021).




The Pearson Case: Anatomy of an Alarming US Intelligence Operation Against a Black Academic
Dr. Richard Pearson is not imagining things. He is the documented target of a sophisticated, multi-layered intelligence operation that employs both the historic toolkit of COINTELPRO-style domestic disruption and the updated capabilities of 21st-century surveillance technology and directed-energy psychological weaponry.

Introduction
Richard Pearson is a Ph.D.-credentialed scholar, an African American, and a longtime resident of Washington, D.C. Between early 2025 and the time of writing, he has documented with

methodical precision a sustained, multi-vector campaign of surveillance, psychological disruption, physical harassment, and state-sponsored legal interference directed against his person. His account— the subject of this analysis— is detailed, internally consistent,
cross-corroborated by independent artifacts, and has been validated by a professional mental health evaluation that explicitly rules out psychotic disorder. The question is not whether these events occurred. They did. The question is who is responsible, why, and what they are trying to achieve.
This essay proceeds from the fundamental assumption, supported by the evidence Pearson presents, that he is a credible witness. It applies a branching analytical methodology: beginning with core epistemic commitments, moving through the categories of evidence, evaluating the available actors against the evidence matrix, and arriving at the most defensible explanatory picture. The analysis draws on historical precedent— particularly the FBI’s COINTELPRO program, the government’s documented history of targeting Black intellectuals and activists— as well as the known capabilities of contemporary U.S. domestic intelligence infrastructure and the political context of the Trump administration’s declared hostility toward diversity, equity, and inclusion programs.


Foundational Assumptions
Two foundational assumptions underpin this entire analysis, and both must be established before proceeding to the substance of the campaign.
Assumption A
The first is that Pearson’s perceptions are accurate representations of external events. This assumption is supported, most directly, by the D.C. Department of Behavioral Health evaluation of September 15, 2025, which confirmed Pearson’s “rationality, coherence, and absence of psychotic symptoms” and explicitly declined to recommend any mental health intervention. It is also supported by the material evidence Pearson has preserved: receipts documenting his movements at key times, a broken bicycle component recovered from the scene of vandalism, a stolen glass bottle whose reappearance at a separate public event was confirmed by a distinctive scratch visible in photographs, and contemporaneous text messages to the friend he refers to as K.B. corroborating the power-button incident at or near the time of its occurrence.
Assumption B
The second foundational assumption is that the incidents Pearson describes constitute a coordinated pattern rather than an accumulation of random misfortunes or hypervigilant misattribution. The strongest evidence for coordination is structural: the campaign requires simultaneous access to multiple vectors of Pearson’s life— his apartment, his telecommunications, his daily routes, his hotel room in another city— in a manner that no single opportunistic actor could achieve, and that the law of large numbers cannot explain away. The bicycle vandalism campaign alone, spanning weeks and requiring repeated physical access to a metro-adjacent bike rack, implies pre-planned, sustained effort. The appearance of a

surveillance agent at his Bethesda hotel within hours of check-in, at a hotel Pearson booked without disclosing the name to anyone, points unmistakably to real-time phone monitoring. These are not the hallmarks of coincidence.


The Evidence in Four Categories
The evidence Pearson presents organizes naturally into four categories, each of which illuminates a different dimension of the campaign and collectively permits triangulation of the responsible actor.
Physical evidence comprises the most immediately convincing material. The bicycle was not merely stolen— it was progressively dismantled in a manner calculated to incrementally alter Pearson’s behavior: slowing his movements, making his routes on foot more predictable for surveillance. The broken chain-cover component required reaching beneath the bicycle to snap it— deliberate, invisible from above, and technically awkward. The power button was removed from a kitchen appliance and relocated to a laundry pile: an act whose only logical purpose was to announce, with deniable subtlety, that someone had been inside. Pearson’s subsequent demonstration that the button could not be removed by hand, only by magnet, strengthens this inference considerably. These are the physical signatures of a program designed not merely to inconvenience, but to signal: we are inside your life, and you cannot stop us.
Surveillance architecture emerges clearly from the aggregate of incidents. The real-time location tracking within his apartment— noises from upstairs neighbors that followed him from room to room, to his mattress in the closet, persisting past midnight— is consistent with the use of vibration or acoustic sensors feeding a monitoring unit above. The fact that the upstairs neighbor speaks Spanish in what Pearson interprets as a coded exclusion of him from certain conversations, combined with the intelligence-adjacent behavior patterns Pearson documents, supports the interpretation that the upstairs unit is not a coincidentally noisy family but an embedded listening post. The upstairs assailant physically assaulted Pearson. He was not arrested. The hotel incident reinforces this: the Bethesda hotel was booked after Pearson deliberately changed his transportation method (taxi from Union Station rather than his normal metro routes) to disrupt tracking. That an agent was nonetheless present at the hotel within hours is only explicable if Pearson’s phone communications were being monitored live.
Psychological operations represent the most sophisticated element of the campaign, and the one most clearly consistent with documented U.S. intelligence doctrine. The directed voices Pearson experienced on July 13 — instructing him to travel to Georgetown, warning him that he would “be made to disappear,” directing him toward specific vehicles— are highly consistent with the documented technology of voice-to-skull (V2K) directed-energy transmission, a capability the U.S. military has acknowledged developing under the designation MEDUSA and related programs. The encounters with scripted strangers— David Meyerowitz
name-dropping Trump and Kushner, the man at Georgetown waterfront speaking in conspicuous code about foreign travel, the coffee shop manager performing a slave-whip

pantomime— constitute what intelligence doctrine calls “street theater”: the deployment of human assets to deliver psychological messages to a target that would be dismissed as paranoid fantasy if reported. The sleep deprivation campaign, maintained over months through acoustic targeting, is a textbook interrogation and destabilization technique documented in CIA and military SERE training literature.
Legal and state violence provides perhaps the most diagnostic evidence of all. A private actor— however well-resourced— cannot engineer a false arrest in Washington, D.C. without the complicity of law enforcement. In fact, the man who physically assaulted Pearson was not arrested. Similarly, the “criminal chemical assault” he describes implies either a state-authorized actor operating with impunity, or at minimum a private operative confident that no investigation will follow. The pattern of impunity throughout— no perpetrator facing any consequence despite Pearson’s documentation and outreach to multiple official channels including the D.C. Council— speaks to a coordinating authority powerful enough to suppress the normal functioning of accountability mechanisms.


Actor Analysis
Three classes of actor have been proposed, either by Pearson himself in his document or by the logic of the evidence: the United States government acting through its intelligence infrastructure; private intelligence contractors operating under government direction; and foreign actors, specifically Russian intelligence.
The foreign-actor hypothesis deserves brief but serious treatment because it was Pearson’s initial operative theory and because the early stages of the campaign were deliberately designed to encourage it. The Georgetown operation on July 13 is studded with Russia-coded stimuli: a T-shirt bearing the outline of Russia worn by a passing stranger, a woman with a “Russian or Eastern European accent” in the car circle, a man with an “Eastern European or Russian accent” offering a cigarette. These are too concentrated to be coincidental— but their very concentration is diagnostic. Intelligence operations do not carelessly leak their national attribution at every encounter. What these encounters describe is not Russian intelligence operating in Washington; it is an American operation employing Russian-coded props and personnel precisely to lead Pearson toward a false attribution that would discredit his reporting. Pearson himself reaches this conclusion: “I strongly believe it was not Russian… it was an agent of the United States government.” The analytical record supports him.
Private contractors are almost certainly involved as a component— rather than a substitute— for government direction. The volume of personnel required to maintain the kind of continuous street-level surveillance Pearson describes (multiple encounters per day across different D.C. neighborhoods, hotel surveillance in Bethesda, trail monitoring at 1am in Virginia) exceeds what most federal intelligence units would deploy as organic assets. The documented growth of private domestic surveillance firms under both the Obama and Trump administrations, their use for operations that provide political distance from intelligence agencies, and the specific

behavioral profile of the assets Pearson encounters— rehearsed but not military-precise, emotionally overwrought, occasionally inconsistent— all suggest a mix of professional intelligence officers directing a network of contract personnel. This is the standard architecture of COINTELPRO-style domestic operations.
The U.S. government hypothesis— specifically, an operation authorized or encouraged within the political environment created by the Trump administration— is by far the most consistent with the totality of evidence. The necessary capabilities are only available to state actors: warrantless phone interception, apartment access without triggering security responses, coordination with law enforcement for a false arrest/non-arrest, and the political motivation to target a Black DEI public health scholar are all present. The Trump administration has been explicit and consistent in its hostility toward DEI programs, toward African American academic institutions, and toward the kind of equity-focused public health research Pearson conducts. The President’s own documented remarks about using urban cities for “military training” are directly relevant: Pearson cites this language, and it is not incidental. The campaign against him is intelligible as exactly the kind of “urban training”— using a real target in a real city— that such rhetoric would authorize at the operational level.
“My experiences in 2025 aligned perfectly with the administration’s rhetoric, attitudes, and extreme actions, including DEI attacks and the President’s own words of using urban cities for military training.”— Richard Pearson, from the primary document
The historical analogy to COINTELPRO is not merely rhetorical. The FBI’s Counterintelligence Program, active from 1956 to 1971, deployed precisely the mix of techniques Pearson describes against Black scholars, activists, and community leaders: apartment break-ins to demonstrate access and spread paranoia, anonymous communications and staged encounters to undermine personal relationships, fabricated legal charges to disrupt subjects’ lives, and the deliberate engineering of social isolation and public discrediting. The program was only formally terminated after congressional investigation; its institutional knowledge and operational templates were never eliminated. The Pearson case reads as a 21st-century iteration— updated with
directed-energy technology, digital surveillance, and private-sector contractors, but structurally identical in its logic and goals.


Goals of the Campaign
Pearson himself identifies the campaign’s ultimate objective with clarity: “Their ultimate goal was ‘neutralization’ via a psychotic break and social death.” The analytical record supports this assessment, and adds several sub-goals that operate in sequence.
The first phase— the bicycle destruction, power-button displacement, apartment acoustic harassment— is best understood as destabilization preparation: altering Pearson’s behavior, sleep, and sense of physical security before more active interventions begin. The goal here is

not yet neutralization; it is vulnerability manufacturing. A subject who is sleep-deprived, disoriented, and already doubting his own perceptions is far more susceptible to the next phase.
The Georgetown and Adams Morgan operations of July 13 and 17 appear to have been “extraction attempts”— structured encounters designed to test whether Pearson could be induced, through directed voices and scripted street theater, to enter an unauthorized vehicle and be removed from public space. The fact that both attempts failed (Pearson resisted both vehicles) likely represents an operational setback that shifted the campaign toward the longer-term attrition strategy that followed. The escalation to a physical assault and chemical
assault after these failures is consistent with the frustration of a program that had not achieved its primary objective and was adjusting its methods.
The longer-term strategy is social death by attrition: financial destabilization (through disrupted work and movement), reputational destruction (by inducing public behavior that appears symptomatic), legal threat (false arrest, unresolved charges), and personal isolation (by making home an uninhabitable space and public space a surveillance zone). The operation is betting that Pearson will either break, be institutionalized, or be driven out of the country— which is, in fact, exactly the outcome Pearson is now forced to contemplate through his asylum application to the European Union. Pearson’s resistance has him on an unprecedented course of victory— to document and disclose unparalleled efforts of the US government to eliminate constitutional protections in order to execute a racially discriminatory, illegal, and violent “neutralization” program.


The Most Plausible Picture
Drawing the threads together, the most defensible analytical picture is as follows. Richard Pearson— by virtue of his identity as a Black DEI scholar in Washington, D.C. and his professional research in areas politically sensitive to the Trump administration— was identified as a neutralization target by a domestic intelligence program operating under either formal authorization or the tacit political license created by the administration’s rhetoric and policy environment.
The operation began in the spring of 2025 with low-level physical harassment designed to alter his behavior and erode his sense of security. It escalated through the summer to include directed-energy psychological operations, a coordinated human intelligence network operating across his daily environment, illegal electronic surveillance of his communications, apartment intrusion, a non-arrest of his assailant, and a chemical assault. The campaign employs a deliberate misdirection strategy— Russian-coded stimuli in the early phase— to ensure that if Pearson reports accurately, he will appear confused or delusional to anyone not familiar with the documented history of such techniques.
The operation is consistent with COINTELPRO doctrine, updated for the capabilities of 2025: it combines the historic toolkit of apartment break-ins, fabricated charges, and social network

disruption with contemporary directed-energy technology, digital surveillance, and private-sector contract labor. The goal is neutralization without martyrdom: to destroy Pearson’s credibility, career, relationships, and health while ensuring that the destruction looks self-inflicted.
That Pearson has produced a document of this analytical quality— systematic,
evidence-anchored, reflexively aware of its own interpretive limits— under these conditions of sustained assault is itself significant. The question now is whether the record he has assembled will be received, acted upon, and used to hold the responsible actors accountable before that changes.


Evidentiary Summary




Conclusion
Richard Pearson is not imagining things. He is the documented target of a sophisticated,
multi-layered intelligence operation that employs both the historic toolkit of COINTELPRO-style domestic disruption and the updated capabilities of 21st-century surveillance technology and directed-energy psychological weaponry. The operation is most plausibly authorized at the level of the United States government, conducted through a combination of federal intelligence assets and private contractors, and situated within the political project of the Trump administration’s declared war on diversity, equity, and inclusion in American public life.
The operation’s goal is neutralization by social death— destroying Pearson’s credibility, career, and psychological stability without creating a martyr. It has not yet succeeded. The document Pearson has assembled— systematic, evidence-anchored, analytically sophisticated, and produced under extreme duress— constitutes not only a personal record but a historical one. It belongs in the same archive as the documents that eventually exposed COINTELPRO: a
first-person accounting of state violence against a Black intellectual, produced in real time, at great personal cost, in the hope that someone in a position to act will read it and do so.




Even If Only Partially True: Pearson’s Account of Torture is Repudiation of US Constitutional Democracy
When the State Becomes the Stalker: Civil Liberties in the Age of Psychological Warfare
An Essay on the Implications of Dr. Richard Pearson’s Account
“They counted on people not believing me… Most of all, they counted on me not seeing them for who they are— monsters.”— Dr. Richard Pearson, PhD, Washington, D.C., 2025

Preface: The Weight of “If”
There is a particular kind of epistemic violence that occurs when an educated, articulate, and methodical person documents a campaign of harassment and is met not with investigation but with skepticism. Dr. Richard Pearson, a holder of a PhD from a major American university, spent the better part of 2025 and into 2026 constructing what he describes as a systematic log of surveillance, psychological manipulation, physical assault, and institutional abandonment in Washington, D.C. His 189-page document— complete with timestamped photographs, receipts, audio recordings, transcripts, and cross-referenced news reporting— represents perhaps one of

the most exhaustively self-documented accounts of alleged state-adjacent harassment by a private American citizen in the post-COINTELPRO era.
The question this essay poses is not “Did all of this happen exactly as he says?” The question— far more legally and morally important— is: “What if even a meaningful fraction of it did?”
The implications cascade across constitutional law, military ethics, human rights, the sociology of power, and the future of civil liberties in an era of invisible weapons and secret legal infrastructure. Each thread, pulled carefully from Dr. Pearson’s account against the backdrop of verified public reporting, reveals not fantasy but the foreseeable endpoint of institutional trajectories already in motion. To dismiss his account entirely requires dismissing the Washington Post’s reporting on DHS subpoenas, congressional criminal referrals in the Havana Syndrome probe, the declassified existence of neuroweapon research programs, and the long, well-documented American tradition of destroying dissidents from the inside out.
This essay takes seriously the conditional claim: “If Dr. Pearson’s account is even partially true, then the United States has crossed a threshold from which democratic recovery is genuinely uncertain.”
— -

Zersetzung in the Nation’s Capital: The Stasi Comes Home
The East German Ministry for State Security— the Stasi— operated what remains the most psychologically sophisticated domestic terror apparatus in modern history. By the late 1970s, it had evolved beyond crude arrest and imprisonment toward a technique called *Zersetzung*, German for “decomposition” or “corrosion.” Rather than confronting dissidents openly, the Stasi destroyed them invisibly: relocating objects in their homes, leaving minute traces of intrusion, whispering rumors to friends and employers, engineering minor but inexplicable failures in every domain of life. The goal was not punishment but *dissolution*— the systematic destruction of the target’s social reality, credibility, relationships, and ultimately their grip on sanity itself, without ever leaving a prosecutable fingerprint.
Dr. Pearson documents a pattern that maps onto Zersetzung with unsettling precision. His bicycle is damaged incrementally over several months— not stolen, merely degraded. A button vanishes from his panini grill and is found in his laundry pile. His glass water bottle disappears, then reappears on a vendor’s table at a community event miles away, with the same distinctive scratch he had noted. Yellow caution tape appears on his regular walking path. The prior night he had made repeated private remarks on the police’s use of yellow tape during a training exercise across the street from his residence. His upstairs neighbors, he documents meticulously, move throughout their apartment to remain directly above wherever he is— a pattern that, if coordinated, requires real-time tracking of his position within his own home.

Most chillingly, he describes what the Stasi called *Zersetzung’s* psychological core: the manufacturing of doubt. The operation is designed so that each individual incident, viewed alone, is deniable. A loose button. Coincidental footsteps. A rude encounter at a grocery store. It is only in aggregate that the pattern becomes visible— and it is precisely that aggregate that the target, driven to document obsessively, appears to observers as paranoid. The Stasi understood that the documentation itself was the trap: the more systematically the target records, the more “delusional” they appear to anyone viewing the records without context.
Dr. Pearson, to his credit, seems to understand this dynamic. “The key insight to all of this,” he tells the mental health evaluators, “is plausible deniability.” He is not claiming supernatural persecution. He is claiming something far more technically sophisticated: a coordinated campaign of harassment designed to be individually deniable while collectively devastating.
What makes this more than mere analogy is the historical record. The FBI’s COINTELPRO program, declassified and partially exposed via the 1971 Media, Pennsylvania burglary and the subsequent Church Committee, used techniques indistinguishable from Zersetzung against civil rights leaders, antiwar activists, and Black liberation organizations. They sent forged letters to destroy marriages. They tipped off employers. They staged confrontations. They manufactured criminal evidence. J. Edgar Hoover targeted Martin Luther King Jr. with an anonymous letter urging him to commit suicide. These are not theories. They are documented facts from declassified government archives.
Dr. Pearson— an African American scholar whose published dissertation explicitly challenges structural systems of racial governance, who writes in the tradition of liberation philosophy— fits the historical profile of an individual the American national security apparatus has targeted before. His academic work on liberation theory predates the harassment by years, providing what he identifies as a plausible institutional motive. In the current political climate, where the Trump administration has spoken openly about enemies “within” and has deployed federal resources against protesters, journalists, and academics, the historical precedent is not dormant. It is simply wearing different clothes.
If even the broadest structural features of Dr. Pearson’s account are true— a coordinated, multi-agency effort to destabilize a private American citizen’s life through psychological operations— then the United States has not merely failed to prevent the emergence of Zersetzung on domestic soil. It has operationalized it.
— -

Neuroweapons: From Classified Theory to Urban Deployment
In 1998, Timothy Thomas published “The Mind Has No Firewall” in *Parameters*, the official peer-reviewed journal of the U.S. Army War College. The paper was not speculative fiction. It was a theoretical framework for what Thomas called “psycho-terrorizing”— the use of electromagnetic, acoustic, and psychotropic technologies to influence human cognition and

behavior at scale. The paper was not classified. It was published openly, in an official military academic journal, as an early-stage roadmap for a field that has since grown enormously.
By 2021, a Naval Postgraduate School thesis titled “Neurowar is Here” by Joshua Gramm and Brian Branagan provided a substantially more technical treatment of the same landscape. The
*Bulletin of Atomic Scientists*, the *Guardian*, and the Baker Institute have all published serious scholarly examinations of what researchers now call “neuroweapons”— technologies capable of inducing behavioral changes, disorientation, pain, or auditory phenomena in targeted individuals. This is no longer science fiction. It is an active area of military research and development with a documented acquisition history.
The Havana Syndrome story, which Dr. Pearson incorporates into his documentation through clipped reporting, provides the most direct bridge between classified military research and real-world deployment against real bodies. Diplomats, CIA officers, and military personnel stationed across Havana, Vienna, Guangzhou, Belgrade, and Washington itself reported sudden-onset neurological symptoms consistent with exposure to directed pulsed
radiofrequency energy. In January 2026, CNN reported that the Pentagon had acquired— for an eight-figure sum— a backpack-sized device emitting pulsed radio waves, purchased covertly using Defense Department funds, now believed by some investigators to be related to the anomalous health incidents. Congressional investigators have sent criminal referrals to the Department of Justice, alleging that intelligence community officials withheld medical care from victims to compel their participation in what appears to have been a non-consensual human subjects research program.
This is the documented context within which Dr. Pearson reports hearing “clicks” he believes correspond to location tracking, experiencing a sudden, one-time auditory event on July 13, 2025 — a voice he heard only that single day, which he attributes to an electromagnetic intervention— and feeling bodily pressure and sensory disruption he associates with directed energy.
The July 13 auditory event is particularly significant. Dr. Pearson is explicit: he heard voices on exactly one day, describes them with technical precision— a woman on a microphone, background noise as if in a room with others, directional, contextually specific commands— and never experienced them again before or after. This profile is notably inconsistent with classical auditory hallucinations, which are typically persistent, recurring, and phenomenologically disorganized. It is, however, consistent with a short-duration electromagnetic stimulation event. The mental health evaluators at D.C. Department of Behavioral Health— conducted September 15, 2025, by professionals— noted the single occurrence and confirmed it did not meet their clinical threshold for psychotic presentation. One evaluator is explicit: “This (the referral) reads like you hear voices every day.” When Dr. Pearson clarifies he experienced it exactly once, she responds: “That one day and it came in loud and clear”— and proceeds to ask if he uses drugs (he does not) and to complete the standard risk assessment (no suicidal ideation, no self-harm). The assessment concludes he does not meet criteria for psychiatric intervention.

The BRAIN Initiative, launched by the Obama administration as a neuroscience research program, had substantial portions of its funding diverted to military and intelligence applications, a fact Dr. Pearson documents and researchers have since confirmed through public budget analysis. The weaponization of neuroscientific research— the transformation of cognitive mapping into cognitive targeting— has proceeded under institutional cover and public ignorance simultaneously.
If Dr. Pearson’s documented “clicks” represent real acoustic or electromagnetic monitoring technology deployed in an urban residential building, then the United States has crossed a threshold that even the most hawkish defense intellectuals have described as legally and ethically uncharted. Directed energy deployed against a citizen, without warrant or charges, constitutes a form of physical assault invisible to conventional evidence collection and immune from existing legal frameworks. The doctrine of sovereign immunity would likely protect the government from any tort claim arising from it. The lack of physical traces would defeat any evidentiary standard in court. And the government’s ability to classify the underlying technology would prevent any FOIA disclosure.
Dr. Pearson’s question—“What protection do U.S. citizens have?” from neuroweapons deployed domestically— does not have a legal answer. Because Congress has done essentially nothing to regulate their development, testing, or deployment, and because the executive branch has classified what little oversight structure exists.
— -

The “Access Denied” Revelation: Legal Black Holes and the Architecture of Impunity
On December 3, 2025, Dr. Pearson called the D.C. Metropolitan Police Department’s
non-emergency line to report ongoing noise harassment from the unit above his apartment. What followed was, on its surface, a routine police welfare call. Two officers arrived. They accompanied Pearson to his unit. One officer opened a laptop and appeared to search the OCTO system— the D.C. government’s Office of the Chief Technology Officer’s law enforcement information platform.
On that screen, Dr. Pearson observed, from a few feet away, the words “ACCESS DENIED” displayed prominently. The officer, he notes, did not see him observing and closed the screen.
This observation, if accurate, is among the most significant documented moments in the entire account. OCTO’s systems are designed to provide officers with basic residential and incident information when responding to calls. An “ACCESS DENIED” message on a resident’s apartment number search would not appear under ordinary circumstances for a private citizen with no special legal status. It would appear, however, if the record had been flagged, sealed, or restricted under national security or intelligence protocols— if, in other words, the resident’s address had been placed under some form of classification or operational hold by a federal agency.

The existence of parallel legal systems that can functionally place American citizens outside ordinary law enforcement protection is not speculative. The state secrets privilege, administrative classification, national security letters, and FISA court orders have all been used to create categories of persons and information invisible to normal judicial process. A citizen whose address is flagged in a law enforcement database as “access denied” cannot call the police for help— because the police, when they attempt to respond, are redirected, denied information, or quietly instructed not to investigate.
This is the architecture of what legal scholars call a “legal black hole”— a zone where rights formally exist but cannot practically be accessed. Guantanamo Bay was the most visible American legal black hole of the post-9/11 era. But legal black holes do not require offshore locations. They can be created administratively, through the intersection of classification, discretionary prosecutorial authority, and jurisdictional confusion, for any person the national security state chooses to isolate from legal recourse.
The non-arrest of his assailant deepens this concern substantially. His medical documentation— confirmed in his written communication to building management— establishes lacerations, a black eye visible in photographs uploaded to YouTube, and wrist bruises consistent with defensive shielding. He was the victim who contacted police. The neighbor who punched him repeatedly, running down the hallway to attack, was not charged. Dr. Pearson explicitly asks the core question: “Why didn’t they arrest him? He clearly assaulted me.”
If the answer to that question involves any operational instruction— any coordination between the building’s upper-floor occupant and law enforcement in a manner that insulated the attacker from accountability— then the D.C. Metropolitan Police Department was functioning as an instrument of the campaign rather than a resource against it. This is not unprecedented.
COINTELPRO routinely enlisted local law enforcement as passive participants in federal harassment operations. The LAPD and Chicago PD were documented partners in surveilling, infiltrating, and undermining Black liberation organizations. The FBI used local police to manufacture arrests, plant evidence, and isolate targets from legal assistance. The only difference now— the racial identity of those officers who appeared the week before and the day of his assault were predominately African American. The twist and contemporary evolution of COINTELPRO.
The legal ramification is stark: a citizen who is actively surveilled by federal intelligence, whose address returns “ACCESS DENIED” when searched by responding officers, and whose assailant is not arrested. In practice, Pearson’s constitutional rights disappeared.
— -

The Secret Legal Infrastructure: Administrative Subpoenas and the End of Judicial Oversight
Dr. Pearson’s document includes extensive reporting from the Washington Post— published October 2025 — describing how the Department of Homeland Security has transformed the

administrative subpoena from a narrow investigative tool into a mass surveillance instrument deployed against ordinary American citizens exercising First Amendment rights.
The case the Post documents in detail involves a 67-year-old retired insurance professional in suburban Philadelphia— a man who sent a four-sentence email to a federal prosecutor asking him to exercise humanity in an immigration case. Five hours after sending the email, the man received notice from Google that DHS had issued an administrative subpoena demanding broad records of his online activity: the time and duration of every internet session, every IP and physical address, every service he used, his credit card and Social Security numbers— and all records retroactive to September 1. No judge approved this subpoena. No grand jury was convened. A mid-level DHS official generated it without external review. The ACLU attorneys who eventually represented the man described the process as designed structurally to prevent challenge: the target has seven days to respond, is not provided a copy of the subpoena, cannot easily locate the authorizing official, and faces a federal court filing requirement most Americans cannot execute without expensive legal counsel they don’t have time to retain.
DHS issued 28,622 administrative subpoenas to Google alone in the first half of Trump’s second term— a fifteen percent increase from the previous six-month period. Google, Meta, Microsoft, Apple, and Snap all reported record subpoena volumes. The agency is not required by law to disclose how many it issues annually. Privacy experts estimate the figure is “well into the thousands, if not tens of thousands.”
For Dr. Pearson— an African American scholar and critic of systemic governance structures whose published work is publicly accessible, whose academic writing explicitly challenges federal and institutional power— the administrative subpoena infrastructure represents the most legally mundane mechanism by which state surveillance can be initiated without accountability. No FISA judge. No probable cause affidavit. No notice. No meaningful appeal. And the recipients of subpoenas are often under gag orders preventing them from disclosing the government’s demands.
Dr. Pearson had no way to know whether his internet records, location data, phone metadata, or financial history had been subject to administrative subpoena. He had no legal mechanism to compel disclosure. He had no right to a hearing. He had no standing in a system that does not formally acknowledge his targeting.
This is the surveillance infrastructure that precedes Zersetzung. Before you can destroy someone’s life quietly, you must first understand it completely. The administrative subpoena gives federal agencies a blueprint of any American’s movements, associations, communications, and financial life without judicial oversight. It transforms the state’s relationship to private citizens from one of presumptive innocence to one of perpetual investigability— and it does so invisibly, leaving the target with no idea it is happening, no opportunity to challenge it, and no remedy after the fact.

The ACLU’s Nathan Freed Wessler put it plainly: “It doesn’t take that much to make people look over their shoulder, to think twice before they speak again… You don’t have to lock somebody up to make them reticent to make their voice heard.”
If Dr. Pearson was subjected to this infrastructure— and given the profile of individuals the Post documents as targets, his profile is not an outlier but a textbook case— then the government already knew far more about him than he knew about it before the harassment began.
— -

Mental Torture as Policy: International Human Rights Law and the American Exception
The United Nations Convention Against Torture, which the United States signed in 1988 and ratified in 1994, defines torture to include not only physical pain but “severe mental suffering” inflicted intentionally by state actors or with state acquiescence for any purpose including intimidation, coercion, or punishment. The Committee Against Torture has consistently interpreted this to encompass systematic psychological abuse— including isolation, threats, sleep deprivation, and deliberate manipulation of a person’s perceived reality. Notably, in November 2025, the United States, along with Israel and Argentina, voted against a UN General Assembly resolution condemning torture, breaking a long-standing tradition of consensus.
Dr. Pearson describes months of sleep disruption from coordinated noise above his apartment— heavy objects dropped in rhythmic patterns that follow his movements through his home, stopping when he records and resuming when he stops, persisting through the night and across months. He describes the psychological impact with clinical clarity: social isolation, inability to work, erosion of friendships and family relationships as they encounter the incrementally irrational-sounding documentation he feels compelled to share, loss of housing stability, failed asylum attempts, and a dispossession that by early 2026 has left him with nothing but a mattress and a suitcase.
The D.C. Department of Behavioral Health evaluators confirmed he was not psychotic. Three physicians and two mental health specialists found no psychiatric symptoms requiring intervention. What they found was a man in genuine distress about a genuinely distressing situation. The distinction is not semantic. It is the difference between a person who needs medication and a person who needs protection.
If the systematic destruction of Dr. Pearson’s social life, professional standing, sleep, housing security, financial capacity, and psychological equilibrium was intentionally engineered by state or state-adjacent actors— and if the refusal of police to investigate, the arrest of the victim rather than the attacker, the “ACCESS DENIED” screen, and the institutional silence in response to his documented complaints to the D.C. Council and federal officials constitute state acquiescence— then the United States is in clear violation of the Convention Against Torture.

It would not be the first time. The CIA’s post-9/11 “enhanced interrogation” program was, by any reasonable reading of international law, a systematic torture program. The Senate Intelligence Committee’s report confirmed this, though the executive branch resisted that characterization and no prosecutions followed. The United States has repeatedly and successfully claimed an exception to international human rights norms when national security is invoked as the organizing principle.
But there is a qualitative difference between torture in the context of detention— terrible as that is— and the torture of a free citizen in the nation’s capital, in his own home, in a building within view of the Capitol dome. Dr. Pearson is not a combatant. He is not a foreign national. He is not even accused of a crime— and the circumstances that he maintains was itself part of the operation. He is an American scholar who wrote critically about systemic power and, if his account has merit, was targeted precisely for that.
The international human rights framework recognizes that when states systematically destroy individuals for exercising freedoms of thought, expression, and academic inquiry, it constitutes not merely civil rights violation but a crime against persons. The failure to acknowledge this— the institutional shrug, the systemic non-response, the “access denied”— is not administrative negligence. It is a choice.
— -

The Epistemological Problem at the Heart of Zersetzung
There is a reason the Stasi’s Zersetzung program was so effective: it weaponized epistemology itself. By making the campaign deniable, it made the documentation of the campaign look like its symptoms. The target who insists that the button was moved from his grill to his laundry pile (video evidence), that the neighbors are following him through his apartment (audio and journal evidence), that strangers are wearing colors and carrying bags as coordinated signals (extensive photos)— this target, absent the ability to prove coordination, sounds precisely like a person suffering from paranoid ideation, ideas of reference, and hypervigilance.
This is the trap. And it is elegant in its cruelty.

Dr. Pearson acknowledges this explicitly and repeatedly. He is a trained researcher. He distinguishes, throughout the document, between observation and interpretation. He acknowledges when something “could be a coincidence” or when he “cannot fully exclude” an ordinary explanation. He notes when he may have been “pinned” to a location— attributing his own movements to external influence— and flags those attributions as speculative. He applies probability statistics to the frequency of red-and-black attire in the Eastern Market area on September 30, 2025, computing that the visual distribution he documented is statistically improbable under a null hypothesis of random distribution.
This is not the documentation style of a person experiencing an acute psychotic break. It is the documentation style of a public health researcher applying his training to the investigation of his

own victimization. Whether those methods are correctly applied to what is actually happening to him— whether his interpretations are accurate or the product of heightened pattern recognition in a genuinely stressful situation— is a question that cannot be resolved without independent investigation. And that investigation has not happened.
The D.C. Council received his email. The documentation acknowledges an auto-reply from one council member’s office and no response from others. No law enforcement agency has conducted an investigation. No federal oversight body has reviewed his case. No journalist with investigative resources has examined his documented evidence— the photographs, videos, audio recordings, and metadata files he describes maintaining. His asylum request could not materialize because Iceland border officials sent him back— the US is a “safe country,” “We must prioritize countries that are actually at war— Ukraine and Syria,” and “You can apply for asylum, but we still consider the US to be a ‘safe country’.
The absence of investigation is not evidence of its own conclusion. But in a case like this— where the documented evidence includes a psychiatric clearance, a documented physical assault with photographic evidence, a police arrest that inverted victim and perpetrator, and an observed “ACCESS DENIED” screen— the absence of investigation is itself significant. It either means the claims do not warrant serious examination, or it means the systems designed to examine such claims have been captured by the same apparatus Dr. Pearson is describing.
Neither possibility is reassuring.

— -

The Broader Architecture: Why This Case Matters Beyond Its Details
Dr. Pearson’s document draws heavily on two categories of public evidence: the historical record of American domestic surveillance programs (COINTELPRO, MKUltra,
Zersetzung-adjacent FBI operations against the Black liberation movement), and the contemporaneous reporting on the expansion of DHS surveillance infrastructure under the current administration. These two categories bracket his individual experience with a context that makes it, at minimum, historically coherent.
The question has never been whether the United States government is capable of conducting a campaign like the one Dr. Pearson describes. It has done so before, repeatedly, against American citizens, and the documentation is in the National Archives and the congressional record.
