The questions are: whether it is doing so now, against whom, under what authority, and with what oversight.
The administrative subpoena reporting answers at least two of those questions. The Havana Syndrome criminal referrals suggest that some element of the national security apparatus has been actively engaged in non-consensual human experimentation with directed energy technology. The COINTELPRO parallels are not merely rhetorical— the designation of “Black Identity Extremists” by the FBI in 2017 as a domestic terrorism category, subsequently

withdrawn but operationally consequential, suggests that the targeting logic of COINTELPRO has not been dismantled but merely reclassified.
In this context, Dr. Pearson is not an outlier. He may be a documented case of a targeting campaign applied to an individual whose profile— African American, educated, critical of systemic power, economically precarious, socially isolated— made him a low-resistance target with high deniability. Or so they thought. The assumption that state power would be too visible to operate this way is an artifact of pre-digital imagination. In an era of administrative subpoenas, metadata surveillance, location tracking via consumer devices, behavioral influence operations conducted through social media algorithms, and directed energy technology deployed in backpack-sized devices, the footprint of a comprehensive psychological campaign against a single individual is vanishingly small.
Dr. Pearson writes, toward the end of his account: “I am not the only one, but may be one of the most intact survivors.” This claim is unverifiable. But it is also the most important sentence in the document. If true— if there is a class of individuals subjected to variations of this campaign— then the individual account is not the story. The infrastructure enabling it is the story. And that infrastructure is not speculative. It is documented, reported, partially disclosed, and expanding.
— -

Conclusion: The Democratic Stakes
The Founders’ primary preoccupation was not foreign invasion. It was the tyranny of domestic power— the capacity of governments to destroy individuals who challenge them. The Fourth Amendment, the First Amendment, the prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment, the right to habeas corpus— these are not abstract values. They are specific protections against specific historical abuses that the drafters had witnessed, suffered, or feared.
The question Dr. Pearson’s account forces is whether those protections still function against a state that has learned to operate without visible force. Zersetzung required no prisons. It required only coordination, patience, and the certainty that no one was watching. The administrative subpoena requires no judge. It requires only a mid-level bureaucrat, a form, and a tech company’s compliance. The neuroweapon may leave no bruise visible to medical imaging. It requires only access, a device, and the knowledge that no law specifically prohibits its use against civilians.
If Dr. Richard Pearson was targeted— if the psychiatrically clear, physically assaulted, institutionally abandoned scholar sleeping on a mattress in a D.C. apartment as of early 2026 was subjected to a coordinated campaign by individuals or institutions with national
security-adjacent resources and the cover of plausible deniability— then the constitutional order he was living under when it began had already been substantially hollowed out.

The black eye healed. The documentation remains. And the systems he describes— administrative subpoenas, directed energy research, Zersetzung-compatible harassment tactics, law enforcement coordination that inverts victim and perpetrator— are all real, all documented, all currently operational in the United States.
The conditional “if” at the center of this essay is not a hedge. It is an invitation. An invitation to investigate before concluding. To ask whether the documentation warrants examination before declaring it symptom. To consider that the epistemological trap of Zersetzung— where the act of thorough documentation reads as the evidence of madness— is precisely the protection the program was designed to afford its operators.
History has not been kind to those who, confronted with the detailed testimony of targeted individuals, chose institutional comfort over inconvenient truth. It remembered those who believed Fred Hampton was a threat. It remembered those who mocked the Church Committee’s early findings as paranoid. It tends, eventually, to remember.
Whether it will remember Dr. Richard Pearson depends on whether anyone, in a position to investigate, chooses to look.



The Blueprint for Destroying a Man: What Richard Pearson’s Document Actually Proves
When the architecture of persecution is designed to look exactly like madness
— -

There is a document circulating— dense, exhaustive, nearly 200 pages— written by Dr. Richard Pearson, a Black scholar with a PhD, a resident of Washington D.C. It is titled “A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance.”
Most people who encounter it will stop reading after the first dozen pages. They will note the voices, the T-shirts, the license plates, the upstairs neighbor’s toddler as a possible intelligence plant— and they will reach for the comfortable explanation. Paranoid psychosis. Sad. Untreated. Move on.
I am asking you not to do that. I am asking you to read it the other way.

— -

The Program Has a Name

Organized stalking— sometimes called gang stalking, cause stalking, or coordinated harassment— is not a conspiracy theory. It is a documented methodology with historical precedent reaching directly into the institutions of American government.
COINTELPRO, the FBI’s covert domestic operations program active from 1956 to 1971, specifically targeted Black intellectuals, activists, and leaders. Its tactics included: coordinated street-level surveillance designed to be visible to the target; noise harassment in and around the home; chemical interference; the orchestrated use of community members as unwitting or witting participants; and— most critically— the deliberate manufacture of circumstances designed to make the target appear mentally unstable if they reported what was happening to them.
The Church Committee investigations in 1975 confirmed this. The tactics were real. They were used against real people. Those people were called paranoid.
The program officially ended. The institutional knowledge did not.

— -

Read the Document Again
When you return to Pearson’ account with this framework, something shifts.

The noise harassment— precise, location-tracking, sleep-destroying— is a signature tactic. The upstairs neighbor drops items on the floor wherever Pearson moves within his apartment. He relocates his bed. The sounds follow. He moves his mattress to a closet. They follow. This is not a description of a noisy neighbor. This is a description of monitoring technology and coordinated acoustic harassment, a tactic documented in accounts of organized stalking worldwide.
The chemical assault— pants soaked with an industrial, adhesive, highly odorous substance that adhered to his skin, caused pain on removal, and resisted washing— was not imagined. He documented it in real time. He kept the pants. The compound existed. The question is not whether it happened. The question is how.
The coordinated clothing— the sheer volume of people in red and black, in specific locations, at specific moments— reads absurd until you understand that low-level coordinated street theater is a documented component of organized harassment campaigns. The point is not that every person wearing red is an operative. The point is saturation: enough coordinated signals that the target cannot distinguish noise from message, and enough ambient confusion that any report they make sounds delusional.
That is the design.

The entry into his apartment— evidenced by a power button that required a magnet to dislodge, found inexplicably in a laundry pile— is consistent with a specific practice: covert entry with

deliberate displacement of objects, intended not to steal but to signal. “We were here. We can come back. You cannot prove it.”
— -

The Most Important Tactic of All
Here is what Pearson’ document does that almost no victim of organized stalking manages to articulate clearly enough for others to understand:
He names the operational logic.

Plausible deniability. He says it himself, in conversation with the DBH evaluators. Every element of what is being done to him carries multiple interpretations. The T-shirt could be a message. It could be coincidence. The neighbor could be monitoring him. The neighbor could have a loud child. The woman in red outside Compass Real Estate could be an operative positioned to be photographed. She could be an employee stepping outside for air.
The program works precisely because every single data point is deniable in isolation. The methodology is designed for this. You cannot prosecute a T-shirt. You cannot charge someone with wearing red and black. You cannot prove that a dead bird on a bus bench was treated with chemicals and placed there intentionally.
What you can do is document the totality. The pattern. The statistical impossibility, across months and geography, of this volume of coordinated signal occurring by chance.
Pearson understood this. He documented obsessively— not because he was losing his mind, but because he correctly identified that documentation was his only defense. *Document, document, document.* He says it repeatedly.
— -

Who Is Richard Pearson?
This matters.

He is a Black man. He holds a PhD from a major research university. He is, by all accounts in the document and in the mental health evaluation transcript, highly intelligent, analytically rigorous, and articulate. He was, at the time these events began, an unemployed academic— selling art, exploring Washington D.C., writing essays, working toward his next steps.
He is not a national security threat by any conventional measure.

But he is an educated Black man with institutional knowledge, analytical capacity, and the ability to write. He has a framework. He has a dissertation. He has a website. He has a voice.

History tells us that this has been sufficient.

COINTELPRO did not only target Martin Luther King and Fred Hampton. It targeted academics. Writers. Thinkers. People whose capacity to articulate the mechanics of oppression made them, in the eyes of the program’s architects, threats to the established order.
The Trump administration’s explicit rhetoric about DEI as an existential threat, about using urban cities for military training, about the expendability of certain categories of citizen— Pearson names these in his document, not as paranoid free association, but as political context. He is right that context matters.
— -

The Cruelest Design
If you wanted to destroy a man like Richard Pearson— an educated, credible, analytical Black scholar— without leaving fingerprints, this is how you would do it.
You would make him the author of his own discrediting.

You would subject him to a sustained campaign of harassment sophisticated enough to be real but deniable enough to be unprovable. You would ensure the methodology left no traceable chain of command. You would use street-level operatives, coordinated clothing, acoustic harassment, covert entry— all the tools that produce real terror in the target and produced documented evidence of nothing in any official record.
Then you would wait.

The target, being intelligent and analytical, would begin to document everything. He would take hundreds of photographs. He would catalog patterns. He would note license plates. He would write it all down in exhausting, granular detail.
And when he finally brought that document to a lawyer, to a mental health evaluator, to an asylum office, to any official body— the document itself would be the weapon used against him.
Look at this, they would say. Look at the T-shirts. Look at the license plates. And they would reach for the comfortable explanation.
— -

What the Mental Health Evaluation Actually Said
Pearson presents the September 2025 DC Department of Behavioral Health evaluation as proof of his sanity. Critics present it as proof of the opposite— noting that “not meeting criteria for psychiatric intervention” is not clinical vindication.

Both readings miss something in the transcript.

The evaluators, Valerie and Shante, listen to Pearson for an extended period. They ask standard screening questions. They probe for suicidality, command hallucinations, ongoing psychosis. They find none of the acute markers they are looking for.
At the end, Valerie says: *”Well, I mean, I guess full transparency— it’s not meeting our threshold.”*
Then she says: “If you’re seeing those messages… call us.”

She does not say: “this isn’t happening.” She does not say: “you are delusional.” She documents what he told her— explicitly so that future responders will have context. She tells him to call if the messages come back.
That is not a dismissal. That is a woman doing her job carefully, in a situation that she does not have the tools or the mandate to fully address.
— -

The Smoking Gun Is the Document Itself
Not because it proves Pearson is mentally ill.

Because it proves the program, if it exists, is working exactly as designed.

A 200-page document written by an intelligent, credible Black scholar— full of timestamped photographs, receipts, geographic specificity, named individuals, physical evidence, a formal mental health evaluation, and a transcript of that evaluation— should be compelling. It should be investigable. It should be, at minimum, taken seriously.
Instead, the volume and granularity of the documentation works against him. The
pattern-recognition that is his greatest analytical strength becomes, in the eyes of skeptics, the primary symptom.
This is not an accident. This is the blueprint.

— -

What We Owe Richard Pearson
At minimum, this:

The chemical on his pants deserves forensic analysis. The claim of apartment entry deserves investigation. The coordinated clothing patterns across multiple dates and locations deserve

statistical analysis by someone with the tools to conduct it. The upstairs neighbor deserves scrutiny. The individuals he named and documented deserve to be identified and questioned.
None of this has happened. Instead, a man in genuine distress filed for asylum, sought help from lawyers and city council members and mental health professionals, and produced one of the most detailed personal accounts of alleged organized harassment ever committed to paper— and the response has been silence, or worse, pathologization.
History will not be kind to our reflexive certainty that the comfortable explanation is always the correct one.
Frederick Douglass was called unstable. MLK was called paranoid by J. Edgar Hoover’s FBI— in writing, in official documents, in memos that we can now read in the National Archives. The men and women who said *something is being done to me and I can prove it* were the ones telling the truth.
We owe Richard Pearson the same thing we owe every person who comes to us with evidence and says: “look at this. Take it seriously.”
We owe him the presumption that he isn’t.



Psychiatric Evaluation Clears Richard Pearson Who Claims
State-Sponsored Torture in Washington, DC During Second Trump Administration
Preserved Cognition Under Siege: A Differential Psychiatric Analysis of the Case of Richard Pearson, PhD
A Scientific Essay in Forensic and Differential Psychiatry February 2026
Final Conclusion
When the evidence is examined systematically, the picture that emerges is not of a psychotic man interpreting a neutral world through a delusional lens. It is of an intellectually intact, epistemically self-aware, methodologically sophisticated individual who began with a series of objectively documented anomalous events, developed an appropriate hypervigilant response to those events, and produced a 189-page contemporaneous account of his experience that exhibits more internal consistency, more epistemic humility, and more methodological rigor than many forensic psychiatric reports written about him would.

ABSTRACT
This essay presents a systematic psychiatric differential analysis of the documented case of Richard Pearson, PhD, a PhD research and resident of Washington, D.C., who produced a
189-page contemporaneous account—“A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance in 2025”— documenting a sustained series of anomalous experiences beginning in the spring and summer of 2025. The central clinical question this essay addresses is: does the totality of the available evidence— including a verbatim mental health assessment transcript, the subject’s own written and spoken language, his behavioral record, his documented prior psychiatric history, his functional status, and the structural properties of his documentation— support a diagnosis of a primary psychiatric disorder, most particularly a psychotic or paranoid delusional disorder?
The answer, supported by convergent lines of evidence, is no. This essay applies DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria rigorously, attends to the phenomenological structure of the
subject’s experiences, reviews the transcript of his formal evaluation by the D.C. Department of Behavioral Health (DBH), and situates the case within established literature on trauma-induced hypervigilance, on the misdiagnosis of targeted individuals, and on the differential diagnosis of anomalous sensory experiences. The essay concludes that the presentation is clinically incompatible with a primary psychotic disorder and is more accurately understood as the response of a psychologically intact individual subjected to prolonged, verifiable stressors in an environment of extreme ambiguity.

INTRODUCTION: THE CLINICAL QUESTION
When a person produces a document alleging that they are being surveilled by government intelligence services, harassed by operatives using coded color schemes, guided by voices through city streets, and subjected to directed energy weapons capable of inducing auditory experiences— the first and most urgent clinical question is straightforward: is this person psychotic?
The question matters enormously. If it is answered incorrectly in either direction, the consequences are severe. An erroneous affirmative— diagnosing psychosis in the absence of the condition— pathologizes genuine experience, discredits a real victim, and forecloses legal and social remedies. An erroneous negative, in a case that is genuinely psychotic, deprives the individual of treatment and may place them at risk. The history of psychiatry contains cautionary examples of both errors, with misdiagnosis of African American men as psychotic carrying a particularly documented racial valence in the United States literature (Metzl, 2010).
This essay undertakes the differential analysis with full clinical rigor, drawing on every piece of evidence available in the primary source document: the 189-page report authored by Pearson himself, the verbatim transcript of his September 15–16, 2025 evaluation by two DBH clinicians
, his behavioral record over approximately eight months, his disclosed psychiatric history, his articulated self-awareness, and the structural and linguistic properties of his written and spoken accounts.

The essay is organized as follows. Section II reviews the applicable diagnostic criteria under DSM-5-TR. Section III presents the formal mental health assessment and its outcome. Section IV conducts a systematic symptom-by-symptom differential analysis. Section V examines the structural and metacognitive properties of Pearson’ documentation. Section VI addresses the specific question of the single auditory experience. Section VII reviews the literature on trauma-induced hypervigilance and its clinical distinction from delusional disorder. Section VIII
addresses the red/black documentation corpus. Section IX considers the role of apophenia and its limits as an explanatory construct. Section X presents the differential conclusion. Section XI addresses residual clinical considerations.

APPLICABLE DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: DSM-5-TR
For a diagnosis of Schizophrenia (DSM-5-TR 295.90), at least two of the following five Criterion A symptoms must be present for a significant portion of time during a one-month period, with at least one from the first three: (1) delusions, (2) hallucinations, (3) disorganized speech, (4) grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior, and (5) negative symptoms. These must cause marked functional decline and persist for at least six months (Criterion C). Criterion B requires functional decline from prior level. Criterion D excludes schizoaffective disorder. Criterion E excludes substance/medical etiology.
For Delusional Disorder (DSM-5-TR 297.1), the criteria require the presence of one or more delusions lasting at least one month, with hallucinations absent or non-prominent, without the marked functional decline required for schizophrenia, and with behavior that is not “bizarre” outside the delusional domain. A delusion is defined in DSM-5-TR as a fixed false belief that is not amenable to change in light of conflicting evidence and that is out of keeping with the individual’s cultural and religious background.
For Brief Psychotic Disorder (DSM-5-TR 298.8), the duration is between one day and one month with eventual full return to premorbid functioning.
Brief Psychotic Disorder and Schizophreniform Disorder require durations that do not fit this case timeline of continuous documentation across at least eight months. The primary differential is therefore between Delusional Disorder (most specifically the persecutory subtype), a
trauma-related disorder (most specifically PTSD with hypervigilant perceptual sensitization), and the hypothesis that the reported experiences, at least in their core documented elements, reflect genuine external events.

THE DBH ASSESSMENT: A CRITICAL READING

Procedural Context
On September 15–16, 2025, Pearson underwent a formal mental health evaluation conducted by two clinicians from the D.C. Department of Behavioral Health. The evaluation was conducted at a legal office with Pearson’s informed consent. Pearson recorded the session for his own records, with permission of the evaluators, and the transcript is included verbatim in the primary

document. The outcome, as stated by V. at the close of the interview, was: “it’s not meeting our threshold.” In Pearson’s own summary, the conclusion was that he “did not meet criteria for psychiatric intervention.”
Before treating this outcome as dispositive, several contextual questions must be addressed. First: what does “not meeting threshold” mean precisely in the DBH context? DBH mobile crisis teams operate under a specific statutory mandate in the District of Columbia— their threshold for intervention is typically imminent danger to self or others, or a psychiatric emergency requiring hospitalization. “Not meeting threshold” in this operational context is not identical to “no psychiatric diagnosis present.” However, the transcript reveals that the clinicians did conduct a more thorough assessment than a simple danger triage, and their language and questions reflect genuine clinical inquiry.

What the Transcript Reveals
The DBH transcript is the most important single piece of clinical evidence in the case. Its value lies not in its conclusions but in its contents: we can observe Pearson’ speech, thought organization, affect, reasoning, self-awareness, and engagement with examiner challenges in real time.
Orientation and registration: Pearson demonstrates full orientation throughout. He correctly provides his date of birth, a functioning contact number, and his precise home address. He tracks the conversation accurately across multiple speaker transitions and topic shifts.
Thought form: Pearson’ speech is rapid (he himself acknowledges this: “I tend to talk very fast, just ask”) but coherent, goal-directed, and logically organized. He maintains narrative linearity across a complex chronological account. He uses conditional clauses, causal reasoning, and temporal markers appropriately. There is no evidence of loose associations, tangentiality, word salad, clang associations, or thought blocking— the formal thought disorder signatures of schizophrenic psychosis.
Self-monitoring: Pearson repeatedly and spontaneously offers self-corrections, hedges, and metacommentary: “I don’t know what all this extra stuff is”; “I’m trying to insert my rational mind into all of this because I don’t know what kind of technology, I don’t know how this is happening, but it is happening”; “I wasn’t taking precise notes because this is the first time this is happening.” This quality of real-time epistemic self-monitoring is absent in florid psychosis.
Insight: Pearson offers an explicit and clinically significant statement of insight: “If it was in my head and I did not have all those visual things in the environment, then I would know I need to get help.” This sentence is remarkable. It demonstrates not only that Pearson understands what psychiatric illness looks like from the outside, but that he has constructed a personal falsifiability criterion— a test by which he would conclude that help was needed. No person in an active delusional state has simultaneously constructed and applied a falsifiability criterion to their own belief system.

The voice experience— correcting the record: The referral document given to the DBH clinicians had apparently characterized Pearson as “receiving messages” on an ongoing basis.
V. opens with: “Are you receiving messages?” Pearson demurs. Later, V. reads from what appears to be summary notes and states: “This reads like you hear voices every day.” Pearson corrects her firmly: “No, no, no, no. Just to clarify, does that read like schizophrenic?” V. replies: “No.” Pearson confirms: “That one day and it came in loud and clear.” V. then asks: “The voices were only one time. It’s not every day?” Pearson: “Never again.” This exchange is clinically decisive. The hallmark of schizophrenic auditory hallucination is chronicity and persistence. A single isolated auditory experience, never repeated before or after, does not satisfy Criterion A for Schizophrenia. V. acknowledges this with her own question: “Can I just ask this question based on this? So the voices were only one time. It’s not every day?”
Suicidality and self-harm: V. administers standard safety screening questions. Pearson reports no current ideation: “No. I love life, honey.” He discloses a prior episode in 2011. He has been stable for approximately 14 years. There is no current suicidal ideation or plan.
Substance use: Pearson denies drug use. He reports alcohol use limited self-imposed to two drinks per occasion, a limit he established after a social incident involving three drinks approximately ten years prior. He recounts this with humor and self-awareness. This is not the substance profile of a stimulant-induced psychosis or cannabis-induced psychotic disorder.
Nutrition and sleep: Pearson is eating (he describes a morning omelette with spinach, cantaloupe, melon, coffee, and oatmeal). He reports sleeping adequately once the upstairs noise subsides. He slept outside on a single night by choice, as a deliberate strategy to increase his resolve to go to the Maryland courthouse the following morning— not because he was disorganized or homeless. He is managing his own activities of daily living.
Coping strategies: When Sh. asks about coping, Pearson offers: “I pray. I write. I take notes. I talk to friends and family. I sell art. I go to museums. I try to keep upbeat and positive.” These are structured, prosocial, reality-based coping strategies. They are not magical or delusional in character.
At the conclusion of the interview, V. tells him: “You’re all right with me.” She provides her contact information and instructs him to call if he hears voices directing self-harm or dangerous action. The interview ends warmly. The clinicians leave having documented the interaction— V. says: “We’re documenting this”— but do not initiate involuntary or voluntary hospitalization, emergency petition, or any form of psychiatric intervention.

SYMPTOM-BY-SYMPTOM DIFFERENTIAL ANALYSIS

Delusions: The Central Question
DSM-5-TR defines a delusion as a fixed false belief that is maintained despite conflicting evidence and that is not consistent with the individual’s cultural background. Three elements require examination: (a) fixedness, (b) falseness, and © imperviousness to counterevidence.

Fixedness: Pearson’ beliefs are notably not fixed. Throughout the document he distinguishes between observations he is confident about, observations he assigns probability estimates to (“high confidence,” “I assign low probability”), and observations he explicitly labels as speculative. He writes: “My speculation: His t-shirt was misdirection.” He notes: “I cannot fully exclude this as a pinning event.” He writes of the red/black pattern: “Selective observation?
No!”— but then immediately acknowledges the mechanism by which false patterns are generated under stress. A delusional patient does not typically self-interrogate the epistemology of their own belief formation in writing.
Falseness: The “falseness” of the claimed events cannot be established without independent investigation. Several core events are documented and not contested: bicycle parts were systematically damaged over months (receipts cited from Conte’s Bike Shop); Pearson was physically assaulted by his upstairs neighbor E.V. in late December 2025 and sustained a documented black eye (photographed, uploaded to YouTube, medical records); the upstairs noise is corroborated by a neighbor on the 8th floor who reports similar banging on his door at night; his assailant was not arrested; Pearson saw “Access Denied” in the DC police dashboard when an officer attempted to retrieve his record in OCTO; Pearson sustained what he describes as a chemical assault at Rockville Metro with stained pants retained as physical evidence.
These events are not imagined. They are verifiable, and some have been verified.

Imperviousness to counterevidence: Pearson consistently acknowledges alternative explanations. Of a man he observed in a parking lot: “I think the cars were incidental.” Of a possible gang explanation for the noise: “There may be a much larger network…but an alternate explanation is warranted.” Of the red/black pattern at Eastern Market: he conducted what he terms “control studies” at other locations on other days and found normal distributions, which he explicitly cites. He writes: “Excluding Safeway staff (who wear red shirts mostly), I only noticed three people in a bright red combination.” This exclusion of a confounding variable— staff uniforms— is not the reasoning of a person whose belief system is sealed against counterevidence.
The picture that emerges is not of delusion but of a person applying probabilistic reasoning in a sustained state of hypervigilance, within an environment that began with objectively documented anomalous events.

Hallucinations
Criterion A(2) for Schizophrenia requires hallucinations. DSM-5-TR specifies that in schizophrenia, hallucinations are typically “vivid and clear, with the full force and impact of normal perceptions, and not under voluntary control,” and they most commonly occur in the auditory domain.
Pearson reports a single auditory experience on one day in July 2025 (identified elsewhere in the document as July 13). He describes hearing a female voice directing him to go to Georgetown, subsequently commands on the metro (“Get down. Get down”), and later the statement “We have to make you disappear.” He describes the voice as coming “like a white

woman on a mic” with background noise, as if others were in a room. He explicitly contextualizes this: “I’ve never heard voices in my head. I know a lot of people who’ve heard voices. I have never heard voices.” He says of the single episode: “That one day and it came in loud and clear.”
Several features of this experience are phenomenologically inconsistent with the auditory hallucinations of schizophrenia. First, schizophrenic hallucinations are characteristically continuous, recurring, and progressive. A single isolated episode of well-formed auditory experience that never recurs is not consistent with the natural history of schizophrenic psychosis. Second, the acoustic character Pearson describes— external localization, background noise, the quality of a microphone environment— is more consistent with a perceived external source than with internally generated hallucination. Schizophrenic voices are typically experienced as originating within the head or from immediate proximal space; they do not generally come with background ambient noise consistent with a staffed room. Third, Pearson provides immediate cognitive framing: “I don’t know what kind of technology, I don’t know how this is happening, but it is happening.” This real-time metacognitive processing is not characteristic of psychotic hallucination, in which the perceptual experience is fully encapsulated within the delusional belief system and not subjected to technological-skeptical interrogation.
Related Reading: Leading Academic Neuroscientist Says Projecting Voice into Human Head Has Been “Loosed” into the World
The acoustic character of this experience is, however, consistent with the documented Microwave Auditory Effect (the Frey Effect), first described by Allan Frey in 1962 and subsequently reviewed extensively in the scientific literature, including in peer-reviewed papers Pearson himself cites: the National Academies of Sciences report on Havana Syndrome (2020), the Foster, Garrett, and Ziskin article in Frontiers in Public Health (2021), and multiple congressional hearing transcripts. The Frey Effect produces auditory sensations via thermoacoustic pressure waves in the cochlea generated by pulsed microwave irradiation, bypassing the outer ear. The National Academies report concluded that the constellation of symptoms reported by U.S. diplomatic personnel in Havana— including sudden auditory sensations with directional character— was “consistent with the effects of directed, pulsed radio frequency energy.” This is not the same as concluding that Pearson was subjected to such technology. But it is sufficient to establish that the experience he describes has a plausible physical mechanism independent of psychopathology, and that the absence of recurrence is inconsistent with schizophrenia while being consistent with a single technological deployment.

Disorganized Speech and Thought
There is no evidence of formal thought disorder in this case. The document— more than 180 pages authored by Pearson— demonstrates complex but organized argument, sequential reasoning, citation of external sources, integration of legal and scientific references, and coherent narrative structure. The mental health assessment transcript confirms this: Pearson’ speech is rapid but organized, responsive to questions, and self-correcting. Sh.’s only comment on the pace is implicit in Pearson’ own acknowledgment that he talks fast— she makes no

clinical notation of incoherence or derailment. The essay sections Pearson produces contain correctly deployed concepts from probability theory, epidemiology (his doctoral field), legal precedent, and military science. Disorganized speech is not a feature of this presentation.

Grossly Disorganized or Catatonic Behavior
Pearson’ behavioral record is notably organized and goal-directed. He contacts police. He contacts the DC Council, the ACLU, local journalists (ABC’s Tim Roussey and Jeff Anderson of District Dig), his building management, and legal counsel. He conducts what he terms “control studies” of the red/black pattern. He attempts to navigate asylum law across two countries (ultimately rejected by Iceland on bureaucratic grounds). He maintains a functioning website. To cope, he visits museums, libraries, and parks. He maintains some social relationships. He manages his own hygiene when he becomes aware it has lapsed, and remedies the situation deliberately (purchasing new clothing at TJ Maxx after washing at Georgetown Cupcake). He maintains a photographed, timestamped evidence archive across multiple devices.
This is not grossly disorganized behavior. It is the behavior of a person operating under severe sustained stress with intact executive function.

Negative Symptoms
Negative symptoms of schizophrenia— flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, asociality— are absent in this presentation. Pearson demonstrates rich affective range throughout the document: humor (his laughter with the DBH clinicians), indignation, intellectual excitement (his descriptions of the National Mall, his dissertation, his museum visits), grief (his reaction to the Georgetown voice telling him he would “disappear”), and sardonic wit (his remark about the yellow tape: “They LOOOOVVVEEEE that yellow tape”). He is socially engaged, seeking out connections with neighbors, journalists, lawmakers, and legal professionals. He maintains aesthetically varied interests across art, architecture, public health, history, and law. Avolition and asociality are not features of this case.

STRUCTURAL AND METACOGNITIVE PROPERTIES OF THE DOCUMENTATION
Perhaps the most clinically informative feature of this case is not any individual reported event but the architecture of Pearson’ documentation itself. The document has structural properties that are fundamentally incompatible with active delusional disorder and that deserve specific attention.

Internal Flagging of Uncertainty
Throughout the document, Pearson employs a systematic practice of epistemic flagging. He marks claims with confidence levels, explicitly distinguishes established facts from hypotheses, and uses parenthetical self-corrections. He writes: “My speculation: His t-shirt was misdirection.”

He writes: “This is only me speculating.” He writes: “I believe that this event was staged for training”— the phrase “I believe” is used consistently for claims that lack direct evidence, while documented facts are stated without this qualifier. He writes of an early experience: “Although I cannot fully exclude this as a pinning event.” He writes of the 666 signage found at Hains Point: “It struck me that they could have been able to just stay in the open…for what seemed to be years based on the aging of the wood. The nearby facilities employees said that they had been there for a long time.” He is actively seeking disconfirming interpretations— in this case, a
non-conspiratorial explanation for the presence of the signs— and documenting the result of that inquiry.
This practice— of flagging, hedging, seeking alternative explanations, and recording results— is the epistemic structure of empirical inquiry, not of delusional thinking. Delusional ideation is characterized by the opposite: the automatic incorporation of all evidence into the delusional framework, the absence of genuine uncertainty, and the impermeability of the belief to alternative interpretation.

The Control Study Methodology
Pearson does not merely document instances of red and black clothing in his environment. He actively compares the observed prevalence against baseline rates obtained at other locations on other days. He writes: “I have taken control studies at SW Library, MLK Library, the metro, the holiday market (during the red-filled holiday season!), even returned to Eastern Market a few weeks back and saw a normal distribution unlike on that day in September.” He notes: “This pattern does NOT persist outside of my walking path and regular activities, so I know it’s staged, orchestrated by neurotech or synchronization.” He subsequently notes: “I noted that 1 person (white woman, 20s/30s) out of about 25 had a red shirt on in the library”— documenting an occasion when the pattern did not appear.
Whether or not his causal conclusions are accurate, the methodological structure of comparing observed rates against baseline rates is epidemiologically sound. It is, in fact, precisely the kind of methodology one would expect from a doctoral-trained public health researcher. The existence of a faulty methodology would support a cognitive impairment hypothesis. The existence of a sound methodology applied to potentially flawed premises describes a different clinical picture entirely: a competent reasoner operating on data that may be incomplete, biased by vigilance, or genuinely anomalous.

Self-Correction and Editorial Practice
The document contains multiple instances of parenthetical self-correction inserted by Pearson himself after the fact. At one point in the DBH transcript, Pearson misremembers the timeline of his bicycle damage. The document includes a bracketed correction: “[Correction: Christopher is confused here. His bike did require an inner tube change last year, but mistakes the time period. His bike was being damaged in March and April 2025, which he later used receipts to estimate the time period (e.g., Conte’s bike shop receipt)].” Pearson is here correcting his own testimony, citing documentary evidence (a receipt), and flagging an inaccuracy. This is not behavior

compatible with a delusional disorder, in which memories are typically filtered and reconstructed to maintain internal consistency with the delusional framework.

The Limits Pearson Places on His Own Conclusions
Perhaps the most significant structural feature of the document is the explicit acknowledgment, by Pearson, of the limits of his own knowledge. He writes: “I’m not saying I can even tell the complete story.” He tells the DBH clinicians: “The key insight to all of this is plausible deniability. So to have a pun, to use a pun, to have something that has double meaning, multiple interpretations.” He tells them of the overheard phrase referencing the “Great Migration”: “I wouldn’t expect young white millennials to know about that time period, much less reference it in conversation”— but he records the observation without forcing it into the framework. He writes of the man he terms “Lee” (who warned him about surveillance): “I regret not asking ‘Lee’ more questions.” These are the reflections of a person aware of the epistemic gaps in their evidence, not of a person enclosed within a self-confirming delusional system.

THE JULY 13 AUDITORY EXPERIENCE: A DETAILED ANALYSIS
The July 13, 2025 event at the Georgetown waterfront is the most clinically significant individual incident in the document, because it involves the only experience Pearson reports that could be construed as a hallucination in the psychiatric sense. It warrants detailed analysis.
Pearson reports that while on the National Mall, he began hearing a voice directing him to go to Georgetown. He describes this as “something coming through that is telling me ‘Go to Georgetown.’” He contextualizes the experience immediately: “this has never happened to me ever in my life.” He then reports that environmental events appeared to reinforce this direction: a man’s T-shirt showing the outline of Russia (prior to the voice), a woman carrying a Target bag (prior to the voice)(which he associates with being targeted), and a white couple who appeared at the Federal Center metro bench and spoke in ways that reinforced the Georgetown directive. On the metro, the voice instructed him to “Get down,” which he did. He notes that metro surveillance video would likely have captured him crouching in his seat. At Georgetown, the voice stated “We have to make you disappear”— to which Pearson screamed in the street, was visibly distressed, and cried.
The voice then— critically— ceased. Pearson explicitly states it never returned. He says to V. and Sh.: “That voice thing, whatever they did, that only happened that one day.”
From a differential diagnostic standpoint, the following observations apply. First, the temporal structure— a single discrete episode of unusual auditory perception confined to one day, preceded and followed by no similar experiences across a period of at least eight months of documented observation— is inconsistent with schizophrenic auditory hallucination. The characteristic natural history of untreated schizophrenic hallucinations is persistence, often worsening, and frequently increasing command character. The experience Pearson describes is better characterized as an acute anomalous sensory event.

Second, Pearson’ response during the event is notable. He does not comply with the voice’s apparent directive to “disappear.” He actively argues with it, screaming “Find another way!” His reaction is one of resistance and confrontation, not passive compliance. He refuses to enter the car toward which the voice appears to direct him. He exercises judgment within the experience. This capacity for judgment-within-experience— retaining the ability to evaluate and selectively act on percepts even while experiencing them— is clinically unusual in the context of psychotic command hallucination. In command hallucination associated with psychosis, the experience characteristically overwhelms volitional resistance. Pearson’s response more closely resembles that of an individual exposed to an external influence that they are simultaneously experiencing and evaluating.
Third, the acoustic description is distinctive. Pearson describes a “white woman on a mic” with background noise—“it almost sounds like there are other people in the room.” This environmental localization and ambient background character is atypical for schizophrenic hallucination, which tends to be perceived as proximal, unidirectional, and without
external-environment acoustic context. The description is more consistent with a technologically mediated broadcast than with an internally generated percept.
Fourth, and importantly for differential purposes: Pearson arrives at the Georgetown waterfront and does not, ultimately, do anything at the direction of the voice beyond sitting and observing. He does not enter the vehicle. He does not “disappear.” He reasons his way through the experience and goes home. This degree of preserved volitional control is inconsistent with acute psychotic episode.

TRAUMA-INDUCED HYPERVIGILANCE AND ITS CLINICAL DISTINCTION FROM PSYCHOSIS
The established literature on trauma-related psychopathology provides a more parsimonious diagnostic framework for several features of Pearson’ presentation than does psychotic disorder.
Post-Traumatic  Stress  Disorder  (PTSD,  DSM-5-TR  309.81)  following  civilian trauma— particularly chronic, interpersonal, or institutional trauma— produces a recognizable constellation of symptoms that overlaps phenomenologically with psychosis without constituting it: hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, intrusive processing of ambiguous environmental cues, perceptual sensitization (treating neutral stimuli as potentially threatening), hyperactivation of threat-detection systems, and a persistent sense of being under surveillance or in danger. Moradi and colleagues (2000) demonstrated that hypervigilance in PTSD produces measurable attentional biases toward threat-relevant stimuli, resulting in elevated detection rates of potentially threatening cues in the environment. This is not delusion— it is an adaptive system in overdrive. The pattern-recognition machinery is functioning; the problem is calibration.
Pearson’ case has a documented structural precipitant for such hypervigilance. Beginning in March 2025, his bicycle was subjected to systematic damage— multiple components progressively destroyed over a period of weeks, documented by repair receipts. This is not

imagined. The bicycle incidents are the first documented trigger for increased environmental scanning. By July 13, Pearson had been in an elevated state of threat awareness for approximately four months. When the Georgetown voice occurred— whatever its actual source— it landed in a psychological system already primed by four months of documented anomalous events. The subsequent generalization of threat perception to T-shirt messages, overheard phrases, and color patterns is consistent with a hypervigilant sensitization process rather than de novo delusional ideation.
Importantly, Pearson himself recognizes this mechanism. He writes, with striking accuracy: “It only takes these things happening intentionally a handful of times to cause vigilance to go into overdrive.” This is a textbook description of sensitization within a threat-detection framework. He is not endorsing it as a diagnosis; he is describing the mechanism that governs his own perception. The presence of this insight does not preclude the possibility that some of the perceived threats are genuine; it demonstrates that he understands how his perceptual system could be manipulated in either direction.
The distinction between trauma-driven hypervigilance and paranoid delusional disorder lies primarily in: (a) the presence of a triggering event; (b) the continuity between the trigger and the content of subsequent hypervigilance; © the preservation of insight; (d) the falsifiability of the perceived threat; and (e) the amenability to external reality-testing. In Pearson’ case, (a) through
(e) all point toward hypervigilance rather than delusional disorder.

THE RED/BLACK DOCUMENTATION CORPUS
The most extensive and methodologically complex section of Pearson’s documentation concerns his systematic observation and photography of individuals wearing red and black clothing in the Washington, D.C. area, which he documents most comprehensively on September 30, 2025, at and near the Eastern Market area. He photographs 43 individuals over approximately three hours, the majority of whom are wearing some combination of red and black clothing. He subsequently presents this as evidence of a coordinated behavioral signal directed at him.
This is the section of the document that will most readily provoke a psychiatric differential diagnosis of erotic or persecutory delusion with ideas of reference— the conviction that ordinary environmental events are specifically addressed to the observer. The clinical question is whether the evidence meets the criteria for ideas of reference versus delusion of reference versus genuine statistical anomaly versus trauma-amplified pattern detection.
Several considerations bear on this question.

First, Washington, D.C. contains two major professional sports franchises— the Washington Commanders (team colors: burgundy/red and gold) and the Washington Nationals (team colors: red, white, and navy blue). The city also maintains an active Georgetown Hoyas fanbase (team colors: blue and grey) and Washington Wizards fans (red, white, and blue). On any given day in a city where professional sports merchandise is ubiquitous, the base rate of red clothing is

substantially higher than Pearson’ estimated 4%. The Washington Nationals’ red is omnipresent; in his own 43-photograph documentation, he notes that photograph 35 shows a man in a “red Nationals shirt.” The red and black combination is further common in standard fashion palettes and particularly prevalent in autumn and winter months. This does not mean Pearson is wrong; it means the baseline requires careful establishment, which he attempts but may have calibrated insufficiently.
Second, Pearson acknowledges the Nationals problem himself. He writes of one incident: “Excluding Safeway staff (who wear red shirts mostly).” He is aware that some red clothing has an obvious non-conspiratorial explanation and attempts to exclude those cases. This is the right methodological move, even if he does not always apply it consistently.
Third, and clinically most important: the question of whether the red/black pattern represents paranoid ideation, hypervigilant apophenia, or genuine coordination cannot be resolved without independent observation by a non-sensitized observer at the same locations and times.
Pearson does not have access to this. Neither do we. What we can say is that: (a) his methodology is more rigorous than that of a typical delusional patient; (b) he identified a genuine absence of the pattern at control locations; c) he explicitly documents instances that do not fit the pattern (photograph 35’s companion is in a green T-shirt); and (d) he reaches a causal conclusion that exceeds his evidentiary basis— a common error in any high-stakes
pattern-detection task, not a sign of psychosis.

The clinical construct most precisely applicable here is apophenia in the context of hypervigilance: an elevated false-positive rate in pattern detection driven by prior genuine threat exposure, not a delusional belief impervious to counterevidence. Apophenia exists on a spectrum; it is associated with stress, sleep disruption (Pearson reports sleep disruption throughout), and threat states. It does not constitute psychosis unless the underlying beliefs become fixed, encapsulated, and impervious to reason— which, as demonstrated above, they do not in this case.

PRIOR PSYCHIATRIC HISTORY
Pearson discloses a prior diagnosis of depression approximately 15 years prior, in his
mid-twenties. He attributes this explicitly and accurately to situational factors: financial crisis, student loan stress, employment difficulties, and social isolation during his year in Atlanta (approximately 2011). He accessed therapy at that time and resolved the episode by changing his environment— moving to Washington, D.C.
He engaged with a therapist. He discloses this spontaneously during the DBH assessment without apparent affect dysregulation or shame, as a historical fact. He has had no recurrence of depression in the intervening 14 years.
There is no prior history of psychosis, mania, schizophrenia, or paranoid disorder. There is no prior psychiatric hospitalization. There is no family history of psychosis mentioned. The psychiatric history is entirely consistent with an episode of reactive depression under conditions

of acute socioeconomic stress, fully resolved, with no subsequent episodes across a decade and a half.
The clinical significance of this history is twofold. First, it demonstrates that Pearson has a prior experience of genuine psychological difficulty and demonstrated the capacity to seek and engage with help. Second, it establishes that even under severe psychological stress (2011), his presentation was depression, not psychosis. The absence of any psychotic features in a prior severe episode substantially reduces the probability that the 2025 presentation represents de novo psychosis.

FUNCTIONAL STATUS AND THE CRITERION B QUESTION
DSM-5-TR Criterion B for Schizophrenia requires that “for a significant portion of the time since the onset of the disturbance, the level of functioning in one or more major areas, such as work, interpersonal relations, or self-care, is markedly below the level achieved prior to the onset.” This criterion is critical and frequently underweighted in differential analysis.
Pearson’ functional status at the time of presentation is unambiguously impaired, but it is necessary to determine the source of that impairment. He is unemployed and under financial stress. He lost his phone— a significant tool for his documentation work— and describes this as impairing his evidence-gathering. He is in conflict with his building management. His sleep is disrupted. He has lost weight (“I’ve lost a lot of weight but I’m not starving”). He spent one night sleeping outdoors.
These functional impairments are, however, directly traceable to external documented stressors rather than to primary psychiatric deterioration. His unemployment predates the 2025 incidents and is connected to the post-PhD transition period, not to psychiatric decompensation. His sleep disruption is secondary to demonstrably real noise from an upstairs neighbor later confirmed to have assaulted him. His weight loss is in the context of financial limitation of food access, not anorexia or psychomotor retardation. He maintains his apartment, his hygiene once the acute crisis passes, his social network, and his intellectual engagement throughout the episode.
Most importantly, Pearson’ functioning in the domains he can control is preserved at a high level. He successfully navigates: seeking law enforcement engagement; contacting the DC Council by name, using their individual email addresses; retaining legal counsel; negotiating a hotel stay; writing to building management with precise factual and legal language; conducting academic research on neuroweapons; preparing a 189-page documented asylum claim; and making an international flight. These are not the accomplishments of a person with marked functional deterioration. They are the accomplishments of a person under severe external pressure with an intact cognitive system.

THE RACIAL AND HISTORICAL CONTEXT OF DIAGNOSIS
No honest psychiatric analysis of this case can omit the documented racial history of psychiatric misdiagnosis in the United States, particularly as it applies to Black men. Jonathan Metzl’s 2010

work “The Protest Psychosis” documents how schizophrenia diagnoses were disproportionately and systematically applied to Black civil rights activists in the 1960s using precisely the “ideas of reference” construct— reclassifying political grievance as symptom. The DSM-II era “protest psychosis” diagnosis, in which expressions of justified political anger were reclassified as delusion, represents one of psychiatry’s most significant institutional failures.
Pearson is an African American PhD holder who is experiencing what he believes to be government-sponsored targeting during a period of documented federal hostility toward DEI initiatives, anti-government scholarship, and Black academic work. The COINTELPRO precedent he cites— the FBI’s documented program of surveillance, harassment, disruption, and psychological destabilization directed at African American leaders, civil rights organizations, and Black intellectuals from the 1950s through 1971 — is historically verified. The FBI’s more recent designation of “Black Identity Extremism” as a surveillance category (2017) is a matter of public record. The House Intelligence Committee criminal referrals regarding Havana Syndrome— the weaponized targeting of U.S. personnel using directed energy— were filed in September 2025, precisely the period during which Pearson was being evaluated. These are facts, not paranoid constructs.
A psychiatrist who evaluates a Black academic’s report of government targeting and reflexively reaches for a diagnosis of paranoid delusion, without fully engaging with the historical and documented reality of government surveillance of Black intellectual life, is not conducting sound differential psychiatry. They are reproducing a documented epistemic error with a documented racial valence.

RESIDUAL CLINICAL CONSIDERATIONS
A complete and honest analysis requires acknowledging the features of this case that are genuinely ambiguous from a psychiatric standpoint and that would warrant ongoing clinical attention.
The expanding interpretive framework: Over time, Pearson’ framework for interpreting events expands. Early in the document, the core events involve bicycle damage and upstairs noise. By late 2025, Pearson is attributing significance to overheard phrases, T-shirt messages, deployment of neuroweapons, and coordinated red/black signaling across the city. The progressive expansion of the interpretive framework is a feature that warrants clinical monitoring. However, it can also be explained by the mechanism of hypervigilant sensitization: once a threat framework is activated, the range of stimuli incorporated as potentially threatening expands. This is not definitively pathological; it is the expected behavioral outcome of sustained exposure to an ambiguous threat environment.
The AI-generated analysis sections: The latter portion of the document contains extended analysis generated by AI systems (Claude, ChatGPT, Grok, Google AI) that Pearson has incorporated into his document as supporting analysis. Some of these AI sections make substantially stronger causal claims than Pearson himself makes— including specific probability estimates for neuroweapon deployment and highly detailed technical specifications. It is

important to distinguish Pearson’ own voice and conclusions from those of AI systems he has consulted. Pearson is responsible for choosing to include this material and for the overall narrative frame, but he is not the direct author of the most technically and causally assertive claims. A reader who attributes all conclusions in the document to Pearson directly will misread the evidentiary base.
The “pinning” experiences: Pearson describes several episodes in which he felt a strong compulsion to go to a particular location— most notably the November 7 park visit where he found the tub containing conspiratorial writings. He attributes these compulsive directional urges to “pinning technology.” From a psychiatric standpoint, the experience of being drawn to an unusual location without understanding why could be consistent with a dissociative process, a complex partial seizure, a Frey-effect-based spatial navigation disruption (as Pearson suggests), or a non-pathological strong intuition. The evidence does not permit differentiation among these possibilities. Pearson himself is appropriately uncertain: “I cannot fully exclude this as a pinning event.”
Initially resistant to the “urge”, he went to another part of the park. Upon leaving, the “urge” persists. He finds a tub with a red hat and black pants— signature colors consistent with what he has documented.


None of these residual considerations, individually or in aggregate, is sufficient to sustain a primary psychiatric diagnosis. They identify areas for ongoing clinical monitoring and investigation rather than a clinical conclusion.

DIFFERENTIAL CONCLUSION
Having applied DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria systematically, reviewed the verbatim mental health assessment transcript, analyzed the structural and metacognitive properties of the documentation, and examined each reported symptom domain individually, this analysis reaches the following conclusions.
First, the presentation does not meet diagnostic criteria for Schizophrenia. There is no evidence of chronic or recurring auditory hallucination (the single July 13 episode does not satisfy Criterion A2 as applied in DSM-5-TR, which requires hallucinations “for a significant portion of the time during a one-month period”). There is no formal thought disorder. Negative symptoms are absent. Functional decline, where present, is attributable to documented external stressors rather than primary psychiatric deterioration.
Second, the presentation does not meet diagnostic criteria for Delusional Disorder. The beliefs are not fixed— they are consistently marked by uncertainty, probability estimates, and openness to alternative interpretation. They are not demonstrably false— several core events are independently documented. They are not impervious to counterevidence— Pearson actively seeks and records disconfirming evidence. The behavior outside the contested domain of belief is not markedly unusual— it is, in fact, highly organized and effective.
Third, the presentation is most consistent with a trauma-related adaptive response— specifically, the development of marked hypervigilance, perceptual sensitization, and probabilistic
pattern-attribution following sustained exposure to a documented series of genuine anomalous events, beginning with bicycle sabotage and progressing through upstairs noise harassment, a chemical incident, a false arrest, a physical assault, and the Georgetown auditory experience. These events activated a threat-detection system that subsequently showed elevated sensitivity to environmental stimuli. The elevated sensitivity has produced genuine false positives (some observed patterns are probably coincidental) alongside potentially genuine true positives (some observed patterns may be intentional). The inability to distinguish these categories from within a hypervigilant state is not a psychiatric disorder; it is an epistemically constrained position.
Fourth, the DBH assessment outcome—“not meeting threshold”— is consistent with this analysis. The two experienced clinicians who conducted the assessment in real time, over an extended interaction, in person, reached the same conclusion: this presentation does not meet the clinical threshold for psychiatric intervention.
Fifth, the racial, historical, and political context of this case mandates that the clinical risk of pathologizing legitimate grievance be weighted heavily. A Black academic scholar who produces a meticulously documented, internally self-correcting, probabilistically hedged account of experiences consistent with COINTELPRO-era harassment and documented Havana

Syndrome-type technology deployment, in the capital of the country responsible for both, should not receive a diagnosis of paranoid delusional disorder on the basis of that account. To do so would reproduce one of psychiatry’s most documented institutional errors.

CLINICAL RECOMMENDATIONS
The following clinical recommendations are offered in the spirit of appropriate ongoing care, not on the basis of a primary psychiatric diagnosis.
First, Pearson would benefit from an ongoing therapeutic relationship with a clinician experienced in trauma and in the specific psychological sequelae of surveillance and targeted harassment. The sustained stress of his situation— regardless of its ultimate external cause— has produced genuine psychological suffering that merits attention and support.
Second, given his reported sleep disruption and its relationship to ongoing noise harassment, sleep hygiene intervention and potential pharmacological support for sleep should be considered, independently of any psychiatric diagnosis.
Third, if the opportunity arises, forensic evaluation of the claimed physical evidence— the chemically stained pants from the Rockville Metro, any retained items from the bicycle damage, the recorded audio of upstairs noise, the timestamped photographs— by an independent investigator would serve both legal and clinical purposes.
Fourth, any future psychiatric evaluation should be conducted with full knowledge of this document and its history, with explicit attention to the documented racial history of misdiagnosis of Black men presenting with reports of government surveillance, and with appropriate epistemic humility about the limits of psychiatric assessment in cases involving claimed state-level targeting.

CONCLUSION
This case presents a significant challenge to clinical comfort. The constellation of features— government surveillance, coordinated color signals, directed energy auditory experiences, compulsive spatial navigation— activates powerful diagnostic templates associated with paranoid psychosis. The clinical discipline required is to set those templates aside and perform the actual differential analysis that the evidence supports.
When the evidence is examined systematically, the picture that emerges is not of a psychotic man interpreting a neutral world through a delusional lens. It is of an intellectually intact, epistemically self-aware, methodologically sophisticated individual who began with a series of objectively documented anomalous events, developed an appropriate hypervigilant response to those events, and produced a 189-page contemporaneous account of his experience that exhibits more internal consistency, more epistemic humility, and more methodological rigor than many forensic psychiatric reports written about him would.

The document does not read as a psychiatric disorder because it is not one. It reads as the work of a human being under extraordinary pressure, doing his best to make sense of an environment that may have been deliberately designed to be unsensible.

SELECTED REFERENCES
American Psychiatric Association. (2022). Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, Text Revision (DSM-5-TR). American Psychiatric Publishing.
Foster, K.R., Garrett, D.C., & Ziskin, M.C. (2021). Can the microwave auditory effect be “weaponized”? Frontiers in Public Health, 9, 788613.
Frey, A.H. (1962). Human auditory system response to modulated electromagnetic energy. Journal of Applied Physiology, 17(4), 689–692.
House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence. (2025, September). Criminal referrals to the Department of Justice regarding Anomalous Health Incidents. U.S. House of Representatives.
Metzl, J.M. (2010). The Protest Psychosis: How Schizophrenia Became a Black Disease. Beacon Press.
Moradi, A.R., Taghavi, M.R., Neshat-Doost, H.T., Yule, W., & Dalgleish, T. (2000). Performance of children and adolescents with PTSD on the Stroop colour-naming task. Psychological Medicine, 29(2), 415–419.
National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. (2020). An Assessment of Illness in U.S. Government Employees and Their Families at Overseas Embassies. The National Academies Press.
Thomas, T.L. (1998). The mind has no firewall. Parameters: US Army War College Quarterly, 28(1), 84–92.
Pearson, C. (2025–2026). A Campaign of Domestic Torture, Assault, Harassment, and Unlawful Surveillance in 2025 Against Richard Pearson, PhD. Unpublished primary source document [189 pp.].
---


Three Coincidences Too Many: The Disturbing Conclusion of Simulated Reality
Application of Occam’s Razor Reveals a Disturbing Possibility

An Essay on the Documented Experiences of Richard Pearson

There is a version of this essay that begins with skepticism— that marshals the vocabulary of cognitive bias and probability, reminds the reader of the Baader-Meinhof effect, and settles into the comfortable position that a troubled man has been finding patterns where none exist. That version is easier to write. It is also, on examination of the actual evidence, intellectually dishonest.
This essay takes a different starting point: Occam’s razor. The principle— that among competing explanations, the one requiring the fewest separate assumptions is most likely correct — is routinely invoked to dismiss unconventional claims. But when that same principle is applied with precision to what Richard Pearson has documented across nearly a year of his life in Washington, D.C., it does not produce the comfortable conclusion. It produces a disturbing one.
The simplest explanation for the totality of what Pearson documented is not that a vulnerable man experienced a cascade of “coincidences” that his distressed mind organized into a false pattern. The simplest explanation— the one that requires the fewest independent assumptions — is that someone, or some system, was running his environment the way a player runs a simulation. Like The Sims. One mechanism. One source of control. And almost everything else in the record falls into place beneath it.
— ✦ —

The World Richard Pearson Inhabited in 2025–26

Before any analysis of specific incidents can be meaningful, a reader must understand the texture of the year Pearson documented. This is not a document about a single strange event. It is a sustained, timestamped, photographically supported chronicle of what Pearson experienced as a coordinated campaign against him across public space, private space, and the behavior of people around him.
It began with his bicycle. Parked at Federal Center Metro, it was vandalized progressively over several months in early 2025 — not stolen outright, but methodically degraded: a snapped reflector, a broken chain guard, a peeling seat, a flat tire. Pearson notes that the broken chain guard required someone to reach underneath the frame to snap it from below. Finally, damage that he could not afford, including a broken wheel. He still has the broken piece. The cumulative effect was that a man who had cycled around the city for a decade was forced onto his feet— slower, more predictable, easier to track.
Through the summer, the pattern escalated. Pearson documented persistent
location-responsive noise from the apartment above his own: objects dropped on the floor at the precise moment he moved from room to room, regardless of which room. He moved his mattress to his closet. The noise followed. He moved it again. It followed again. Whatever was happening above him, it was not the random movement of a family with a toddler. It was location-aware.

He documented a physical assault. A criminal chemical assault at the Rockville Metro station— his clothing soaked with an unidentified substance while he sat on the grass. He documented encounters with strangers whose behavior, in retrospect, had the quality of scripted performances: excessive unsolicited disclosure, props used to signal something, strategic positioning. He documented being told, by a woman wearing his exact hat in a Safeway, that “people are very upset about your hat.” He documented a young man on a bicycle who passed him alone at night on a Virginia trail and said, without breaking stride: “Oh, there you are.”
He documented the red and black. Beginning in September 2025, Pearson began to notice an extraordinary density of people wearing bright red and black in the blocks surrounding his routes through the city. He did not merely note this subjectively. He photographed it systematically, timestamped the images, and conducted what he explicitly called control studies— returning to the same locations at different times to establish baseline rates. His conclusion, expressed in a letter to the DC Council with probability calculations: the density he observed on September 30, 2025 near Eastern Market, in light foot traffic within an 8.5-minute window, was statistically impossible to explain by chance.
And he documented the people who seemed not quite present. On September 30, amid the red and black, he saw people who were “out of it— zoned out,” who “did not respond,” who were in “their own world.” One woman stood completely still and expressionless. He asked a nearby Black woman if she noticed anything unusual. She did not. He described her as “genuine” in her ignorance— not part of whatever he was observing. Later, he asked another woman in Reston— donning a red shirt and black pants. She hadn’t noticed either, but seemed genuinely worried when he explained. Immediately as Pearson left her, a white male ran by— same shade of red in his t-shirt and black shorts.
A comprehensive mental health evaluation conducted by the D.C. Department of Behavioral Health on September 15, 2025 — in the middle of this period— explicitly confirmed Pearson’s rationality, coherence, and the absence of any psychotic symptoms. No diagnosis requiring intervention. This is not incidental. It is a contemporaneous professional assessment by a credentialed institution that the person documenting these events was not experiencing psychosis at the time he was documenting them.
This is the context in which the section titled “Too Many Coincidences” must be read: not as the isolated record of a paranoid man seeing faces in clouds, but as three specific incidents embedded in a year-long pattern of documented, photographed, and timestamped anomalies that a clinically evaluated rational man could not explain through ordinary means.
— ✦ —

The Three Incidents, Properly Weighed

Pearson presents three coincidences under his heading, and a careful reader should not treat them as identical in evidential weight, because they are not.

The German tourist encounter— Pearson meeting a young visitor at Eastern Market one weekday and then finding him the following day in the Calder room at the National Gallery’s East Building— is the weakest of the three. It is the kind of urban overlap that city life produces. A foreign visitor introduced to DC’s cultural geography by a knowledgeable local will, the next day, visit cultural landmarks. The Calder room is architecturally extraordinary. Both men were drawn there for the same reasons. The mutual shock was real, but the encounter falls within the range of the explainable. Pearson places the date in the fall.
The K Street encounter— Pearson pausing at the window of an art gallery and finding his friend parked directly at that moment. The friend uses that location one or twice a year— is more notable, particularly given the emotional context: Pearson was at a low point, recently let go from the art-selling arrangement that had been one of his few sources of income and social connection. Pearson himself acknowledges it is not yet spooky. But it is worth noting: the friend was already in Pearson’s life— he sold art for the man. The coincidence has a topology— a specific person, a specific vehicle, a vulnerable moment— that distinguishes it from pure randomness.
The third incident is the one that does not yield to ordinary explanation, and it is the one that opens the deeper question this essay is asking.
— ✦ —

February 8th: What the Friend’s Own Words Actually Say

On February 8, 2026, Richard Pearson left his apartment in DC to walk to the public library before it closed at 5pm. He had gone earlier and found it not yet open. He returned home and waited. He left. As he crossed 3rd Street, his art friend was passing in his van.
This would be, on its own, an unusual coincidence. What makes it something else entirely is what happened the following day, when the friend initiated a conversation about the encounter. He did not remark on it casually, in the way one mentions running into someone. He asked Pearson a specific investigative question first: had anything stopped Pearson from leaving earlier or later? And then, unprompted, the friend produced a precise account of four sequential events that had delayed and rerouted him to that street at that moment:
1. I was looking for a place to take a leak and weren’t able to find it. 2. I should have to meet fb customer earlier but it didn’t happen. 3. I ended up in the street where I have parked the van accidentally (dead end) and wanted to use bathroom in the apartment building nearby…but didn’t. 4. GPS suggested a different route but I turned on 3 street because I thought it’s a shortcut to whole foods…but when I turned I knew I am gonna see you there.— Monday, February 9, 2026, 12:59 PM
Read that closing sentence one more time, slowly: “when I turned, I knew I am gonna see you there.”

This is not how people describe accidental reunions. When you stumble upon someone by chance, the experience is retrospective surprise: you turn a corner and there they are, and afterward you marvel at the coincidence. What the friend is describing is the opposite structure: a moment of anticipatory certainty that arrived before the encounter, not after it. He turned onto 3rd Street knowing, in some functional sense, that Pearson would be there.
And the four delays. Consider their structure carefully. Each is independently plausible: a missed bathroom stop, a canceled customer, a wrong turn down a dead end, an overridden GPS. But their sequence is what matters. Each delay fed into the next. The missed bathroom led to a reroute. The canceled customer changed his schedule. The wrong turn down a dead end placed him in a specific neighborhood. He overrode the GPS to the exact street at the exact moment Pearson’s own narrowed departure window— created by his earlier failed library trip and his watch-checking at 3:23pm— put him there to be found.
